Full: out/llm_audit.json
Vienna apartment report
Listings (left) + Leaflet map (right). Click a listing to zoom/highlight the marker; click a marker to jump to the listing.
What you’re seeing: apartments for purchase (Willhaben), max price €700k. District order is proxy-based. Late-evening busyness is a composite proxy within ~300m: food/drink density + transit density + culture venues + plaza/pedestrian-zone signal + pedestrian-friendly street length.
(filters)
Overlays (100m grid): each square shows OSM POI counts binned into a 100m×100m grid over Vienna; color intensity is log-scaled density within that grid cell.
Map legend: Exact + confirmed match Exact, match unconfirmed Approx/mismatch flagged
Glossary: District = Vienna municipal district; Pre-war/classic building category; Newer-build category; Metro; Suburban rail; Tram; Sidewalk seating.
Report

Ranked listings

#1
Unbefristet vermietete 3-rooms-apartment mit Balkon in bester Lage - Donaukanal und metro um´s Eck (Unbefristet vermietete 3-Zimmer-Wohnung mit Balkon in bester Lage - Donaukanal und U-Bahn um´s Eck)
€315,000 · 74.21 m2 · 3 rooms · Gonzagagasse, Vienna, 01. District, Innere Stadt
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €315,000
  • Size: 74.21 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • District: Vienna 1

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — it’s currently unbefristet rented, so “move-in” hinges on tenancy terms, rent level, and handover scenario.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (uncertain possession; balcony helps but tenancy risk dominates) Investor: ✔ (central micro-location + steady tenancy can be attractive if rent/legal terms make sense) Lifestyle buyer: ❌ (you’re buying the location but not necessarily the lifestyle access)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the price can be compelling for 1010, but only if the existing lease economics and building situation are clean and verifiable.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €260k–€290k (best-effort guess; I don’t have comps or the current rent/contract details). Use one hard lever: unbefristet tenancy uncertainty — request the lease contract, current net rent, indexation history, and any agreed side-letters; if rent is low vs market or protections are strong, price must re-rate downward. Secondary levers: missing heating/energy data + unknown floor/orientation; make your offer conditional on receiving complete operating cost breakdown (repairs reserve, management, elevator, upcoming works) and energy/heating documentation.

Bottom line

You’re paying for the micro-area: Donaukanal/metro proximity and very high amenity density. But the deal lives or dies on the tenancy file and the building’s medium-term capex (reserves + planned works). Two ways this goes wrong fast: the rent is far below market with limited increase potential, or the building hits you with upcoming major works that make the “cheap 1010” headline price a mirage.

Agent assessment — property (ad-anchored)

This note sticks to what the seller actually states in the ad text and what is present as structured fields. If the ad is thin, treat specifics as unknown until you verify them.

What the seller emphasizes:

  • No strong seller emphasis could be reliably extracted from the text.

What is explicitly stated:

  • Key building/usage details are not clearly specified in the ad text.

What is not stated (so assume unknown):

  • Exact floor level / storey
  • Elevator / lift
  • Outdoor space (balcony/loggia/terrace/garden)
  • Parking / garage availability and terms
  • Energy certificate figures (HWB / fGEE)
  • Tenancy status (vacant vs rented, end date)
  • Furnishing included/excluded

Caveats / how to read this ad:

  • If the pin is not confirmed, treat nearby-amenity context as approximate and verify the exact micro-location.

Fit (who this suits)

  • Best fit: buyers who are comfortable doing verification at the viewing (building condition, fees, exact floor/lift, what is included).
  • Less ideal if: you need full upfront disclosure in the listing text before investing time.

Micro-area (local guide)

As stated in the ad, no specific nearby landmarks or transit lines are named. For this apartment, that matters because you should rely on on-the-ground checks (evening noise, walk times) and the report’s generic OSM proxy signals rather than assumed venue proximity.

Macro-area (local guide)

General background (not source-verified): Vienna 1 can vary block-by-block. The best version of central Vienna living is quiet-at-home but walkable-to-everything; the trade-offs are tourism gradient, event-driven crowding, and late-night noise. Treat courtyard vs street exposure and window quality as the real differentiators.

Orientation

Centrality proxy is not available for this listing. Key orientation/access details are not specified (floor, lift).

#2
Smarte City apartment bei der Oper I Absolute (Smarte Stadtwohnung bei der Oper I Absolute Ruhelage)
€375,000 · 28.6 m2 · 1 rooms · Vienna, 01. District, Innere Stadt
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €375,000
  • Size: 28.6 m2
  • Rooms: 1
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 1

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends (likely fine for immediate use, but a 1957 building + gas heating means you want a hard look at condition and future capex).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (too small; 1-room micro-flat) Investor: ✔ (prime micro-unit, strong rental/exit liquidity if building governance is sane) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (city base next to the Opera; high walkability without street-noise if “absolute quiet” holds)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location is elite and the “quiet at 7th floor” angle is real value, but the €/sqm is inherently punchy for 28.6 sqm and gas/heating + building reserve need diligence.

Negotiation strategy

Anchor on: (1) small-unit pricing risk, (2) energy performance (HWB ~94) + gas exposure, (3) any upcoming building works (lift/roof/façade), (4) whether monthly costs exclude heating. A reasonable guess target range: €340k–€365k depending on interior condition, reserve level, and any planned major works.

Bottom line

You’re buying “1010 + Opera adjacency + quiet” in a tiny, efficient package. If the room layout feels genuinely livable and the building finances are clean, it’s a strong city-base / rental asset. If the unit feels dark/cramped in person or the building is gearing up for big works, walk away fast—micro-units punish mistakes.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (recent refresh + 2024 new boiler), but the “quiet” claim hinges on courtyard orientation and building services.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ✔ Lifestyle buyer: ✔

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — A clean, tiny 1010 base with unbeatable transit, but the 38 sqm / inner-courtyard outlook and 1962 building energy profile cap upside at this ask.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €340k–€370k (best-effort; true comps unknown). Anchor on (1) courtyard-facing “light well” view, (2) 1962 building + reported HWB 97, and (3) gas heating/boiler dependency. Concrete lever: request proof of building reserve level and planned works (lift/roof/facade/risers) and price any upcoming special contributions into your offer.

Bottom line

You’re buying location and convenience, not space or views. Perfect as a crash pad / city pied-à-terre; less compelling if you expect airy living or long-term resale momentum. Two ways this goes wrong: courtyard noise/smells from shafts or neighbors, and surprise building capex (lift/risers) that makes the “low monthly costs” look misleading.

#4
FIXPREIS** INVESTMENT IN DER WIENER city centre- WOHNEN - BUeRO - PRAXIS - EINMALIGE GELEGENHEIT IM HERZEN WIENS ** (FIXPREIS** INVESTMENT IN DER WIENER INNENSTADT- WOHNEN - BÜRO - PRAXIS - EINMALIGE GELEGENHEIT IM HERZEN WIENS **)
€425,000 · 45 m2 · 2 rooms · Vienna, 01. District, Innere Stadt
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €425,000
  • Size: 45 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Floor: ground floor
  • Heating: central heating
  • District: Vienna 1

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (renovated; functional 2-room, barrier-free).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (too small; ground-floor trade-offs) Investor: ✔ (District 1 liquidity + flexible use case, but regulation risk) Lifestyle buyer: ➖ (iconic location, but likely noise/privacy compromises)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great “address value” asset, but ground-floor + energy/heating profile + use-case uncertainty mean you should not overpay.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €385k–€410k (best-effort; true comp set unknown from provided data). Levers: (1) Price inconsistency in the marketing (one figure in the text vs another extracted) — force a written confirmation of the true fixed price and use the confusion to justify a cut. (2) Ground-floor discount: push on privacy/noise/security and resale pool limitations. (3) Energy/heating angle: gas central heating + HWB ~100.7 suggests higher running-cost sensitivity; ask for recent heating bills and use any uncertainty as a discount. (4) Investor pitch stress-test: ask for written clarity on what uses are actually permitted (office/practice/short-term) and price in the risk if any are restricted.

Bottom line

You’re buying “micro-location” more than the apartment. If short-term rental isn’t realistically allowed/viable, the investment story weakens fast. Treat it like a premium ground-floor unit: pay for location, not for hype.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ✔

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — prime central location and turnkey condition, but you’re paying a premium for a small, ground-floor apartment with potential noise/privacy trade-offs.

Negotiation strategy

Aim to negotiate toward roughly €400k–€425k (guess): lean on ground-floor drawbacks (street noise/privacy), small size (45 sqm), and gas heating as future-proofing risk; ask for clear confirmation on building fees, any pending works, and the exact window orientation before you firm up.

Bottom line

This is a “buy the postcode” apartment: unbeatable walk-everywhere city life. Great as a personal pied-à-terre or a stylish base in the center. Be honest with yourself about ground-floor living—if that bothers you, no discount will make you happy.

# SQ - SANIERTE 2 rooms IM ERDGESCHOSS IN 1010 Vienna - TIEFER GRABEN (# SQ - SANIERTE 2 ZIMMER IM ERDGESCHOSS IN 1010 WIEN - TIEFER GRABEN)
€440,000 · 43 m2 · 2 rooms · Vienna, 01. District, Innere Stadt
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €440,000
  • Size: 43 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • District: Vienna 1

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (renovated in 2020), but ground-floor realities + missing energy certificate make “final comfort” dependent on a visit.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (43 sqm, 2 rooms, ground floor in the city core) Investor: ✔ (1st district liquidity + strong demand for compact units; watch yields at this entry price) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walk-everywhere, restaurant/cafe density, transit-rich micro-location)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location is elite, but the €/sqm is punchy for a small ground-floor unit and the unknown building/energy details are leverage.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €400k–€425k (best-effort guess; true comps not provided). Anchor on (1) ground-floor discount, (2) missing energy certificate, (3) noise/privacy risk in a tourism-heavy micro-area, and (4) likely higher maintenance burden in historic-core buildings. Ask for: exact floor height vs street, window orientation, sound insulation, heating type, building condition/reserves, and any planned works.

Bottom line

If you want “Vienna on foot” with zero commute, this hits hard. But you’re paying for postcode more than square meters—make the seller price in the ground-floor trade-offs. Do not waive due diligence: energy, damp/mold checks, noise at night, and building reserve status.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — physically sounds in decent shape, but it’s unbefristet vermietet (since 2009), so you’re not moving in anytime soon.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (too small + tenant blocks self-use) Investor: ✔ (stable rent, prime micro-location, but yield looks thin at this ask) Lifestyle buyer: ❌ (District 1 vibe is great; owning a unit you can’t use is not)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location is excellent, but the unbefristet tenancy + 4th floor with no lift should force a meaningful discount versus vacant units.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €360k–€410k (best-effort; exact comps unknown). Anchor hard on (1) unbefristet vermietet, (2) no elevator at 4th floor, (3) limited upside on rent. Ask for the Sachverstaendigengutachten and check: current legal rent regime, indexation history, tenant protections, and any backlog of building works.

Bottom line

This is basically a “prime-address bond” rather than a home. If you want District 1 lifestyle, pass and buy something vacant. If you want safety + long-duration hold, only works if the price reflects the tenancy handcuffs.

#8
Am Salzgries: Bezugsfertig + Attraktive Zentrumslage + Wohnen in historischer Umgebung + Ruhige Westausrichtung + Voll moebliert (Am Salzgries: Bezugsfertig + Attraktive Zentrumslage + Wohnen in historischer Umgebung + Ruhige Westausrichtung + Voll möbliert)
€495,000 · 50 m2 · 1 rooms · Passauer Platz, Vienna
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €495,000
  • Size: 50 m2
  • Rooms: 1
  • Heating: Gasheizung
  • District: Vienna 1

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (being sold fully furnished), with the usual “verify what’s actually included + condition” caveat.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (1 room, 50 sqm) Investor: ➖ (easy to rent in 1010, but yield pressure at this price + furniture value is often overstated) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (central, walk-everywhere, historically pretty micro-location)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — premium address and micro-area convenience are real, but 495k for 50 sqm + gas heating + unknown energy data means you should push hard on price and documentation.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort): €450k–€475k. I don’t have verified comps here, so treat this as a positioning range, not a valuation. Use the “fully furnished” angle against them: demand an itemized inventory + ages/receipts, and price it like a normal apartment (furniture rarely justifies a big premium). Ask for energy/usage proof (gas), building reserves status, and any planned works; if documentation is thin or building CapEx is looming, that’s your leverage.

Bottom line

If you want a compact, city-core base with instant walkability: this is the right kind of spot. If you care about quiet, comfort, and predictable long-term running costs: the unknown building details + gas make this a diligence-first deal. Don’t overpay for “historic vibe” unless the building quality and paperwork back it up.

Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking

Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View

  • Rooms: 1
  • Floor: 4
  • Lift: Yes (as stated)

  • District: Vienna 1

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — “lightly in need of renovation” plus a non-functioning heating system likely means immediate capex before it’s comfortable year-round.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ✔

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — unbeatable centrality and quiet courtyard orientation, but the pricing needs to reflect the real cost/risk of fixing heat + refreshing a small unit in a premium micro-location.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €430k–€470k (comps unknown; this range assumes you’re funding heating replacement + cosmetic/functional upgrades and taking “small-unit premium” risk). Push hardest on: (1) non-functioning heating (scope + timeline + permits), (2) any upcoming building works / reserve adequacy, (3) whether the “courtyard parking” is actually usable/reliable. Concrete lever: require written confirmation of the heating fix path (what exactly is broken, replacement options, and whether flue/chimney is usable) and price the uncertainty.

Bottom line

You’re buying a hyper-central, quiet-ish crash pad, not “value.” If the building and heating situation is clean, it’s a great lifestyle asset — if not, the downside is you overpay for a 30m² headache. Make the seller subsidize the uncertainty via price, not promises.

Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Address/map mismatch flagged (likely approximate pin).
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €521,496
  • Size: 72.43 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 1

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — “fully renovated” + underfloor heating suggests turnkey, but missing basics (energy class, floor/lift, windows/courtyard vs street) are the make-or-break details.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (72 sqm / 2 rooms in the 1st district is tight; great if it’s just 1 kid and you value centrality over space) Investor: ✔ (strong tenant/expat demand in 1010, but price-per-sqm and running costs need rent reality-check) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (this is classic “walk everywhere Vienna” living; restaurants/parks density reads very day-to-day convenient)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — looks like a high-livability 1010 renovated 2-room, but you should not pay “prime” money until you confirm quietness, building quality, and the real monthly all-in costs.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €485k–€510k (best-effort guess; true comps for Lackierer Lane/1010 can swing hard, and I don’t have direct comp set here). Push on unknowns: floor/lift, orientation (street vs courtyard), noise, windows spec, roof/pipe/electrics scope of renovation, and any upcoming building works. Use the monthly costs (€231.41) to ask for the full Betriebskosten/Ruecklage split and the last house statements.

Bottom line

If it’s quiet + good building + decent natural light, this is a “live small, live central” winner. If it’s street-noisy or a walk-up with weak light, it becomes an expensive compromise fast. Pay for the micro-location, not for marketing words.

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (modern bath, fully furnished), but verify building condition, noise, and heating costs.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (41 m² / 2 rooms is tight long-term) Investor: ✔ (liquidity + location + furnished appeal; watch regulation/fees) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (balcony + instant “city core” living)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — you’re paying peak-1010 pricing for a small unit; it can work if the balcony/noise/building checks out.

Negotiation strategy

Keep the furniture as a value-add, not a reason to overpay.

Bottom line

2-room, balcony, ultra-central: it’s a “Vienna weekend / pied-à-terre” archetype. If the balcony is truly usable and the windows are quiet, this becomes a very livable micro-home. If it’s loud or the building is tired, the premium evaporates fast — negotiate hard or walk.

€564,000 · 87.94 m2 · 3 rooms · Gonzagagasse, Vienna, 01. District, Innere Stadt
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €564,000
  • Size: 87.94 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Lift: Yes (as stated)
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 1

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — the unit itself may be fine, but you’re effectively buying a long-term tenancy situation, not a home you can simply occupy.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (indefinite tenant blocks self-use in any normal timeframe) Investor: ➖ (stable but extremely low rent; return profile hinges on legal/market realities) Lifestyle buyer: ❌ (you won’t get the lifestyle benefits of 1010 if you can’t live there)

Final verdict

🔴 PASS — unless you explicitly want a “tenant-in-place” asset with low income today and uncertain path to higher yield later.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort): €420k–€500k. Comps are unknown here, but the indefinite tenancy (since 1989) + no lift + sale from insolvency estate are material price suppressors. Anchor on (1) net rent level, (2) inability to self-use, (3) 1st-floor period building without elevator, and (4) any building reserve / upcoming works. Concrete lever tied to a verifiable uncertainty: ask for (or condition your bid on) the building’s reserve fund level and planned capital works (roof/façade/risers/gas lines). If reserves are thin or works are pending, that’s immediate downside you can price.

Bottom line

This is “prime location, non-prime control.” The rent is tiny for 1010 — you’re paying for a long-duration option, not a cash-flowing investment. Two ways this goes wrong: tenant situation becomes effectively permanent (no practical re-pricing), and building capex hits while your income stays capped.

Move-in readiness

Depends — listing explicitly signals renovation needed; assume you’re buying “bones + location,” not a turnkey home.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (3 rooms helps, but ~72 sqm in 1010 is tight; renovation + city-center noise/traffic trade-offs) Investor: ➖ (prime district supports demand, but renovation risk + unclear floor/lift/layout caps certainty) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (if you want central Vienna with everything at your doorstep and can stomach works)

Final verdict

But centrality also amplifies noise/tourism and makes renovation logistics more painful. Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (new build/first occupancy feel; underfloor heating is a strong comfort signal).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (37.46 sqm / 1-room is fundamentally tight) Investor: ➖ (trophy address helps liquidity, but entry price-per-sqm and likely HOA/operating costs can compress yield) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (iconic view + ultra-walkable core-city living)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — you’re paying for “Inner City + landmark view” more than for usable space; make sure the noise/tourism trade-off fits your daily life.

Negotiation strategy

Anchor on value-per-sqm and livability risks (tourist footfall, delivery noise, short-stay vibe, heat in top-floor units, resale pool being niche for 1-room). Bottom line

This is a “Vienna postcard” apartment: tiny, premium, and emotionally priced. If you want a pied-à-terre and will actually enjoy the city-core intensity, it can make sense. If you want daily calm or future flexibility, the 1-room format at this price is the constraint, not the finish quality.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — physically sounds in decent shape, but functionally not move-in because it’s indefinitely rented.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (you can’t realistically plan to live here) Investor: ➖ (prime micro-location, but the rent is extremely low and tenancy is the whole story) Lifestyle buyer: ❌ (you’re buying a view from the sidewalk, not keys)

Final verdict

Action: 🔴 PASS — unless you are specifically hunting a long-duration, low-yield, tenant-in-place 1st-district asset and you price it like one.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort guess; comps and tenant terms unknown): €430k–€520k. Your lever is the verifiable uncertainty: demand the full tenancy file (contract, rent indexation history, any side agreements, deposit, maintenance responsibility clauses) plus building reserve status and recent major works. Anchor hard on (1) indefinite tenancy since 1988, (2) net rent ~€187/month, (3) gas floor heating exposure, and (4) insolvency sale dynamics (highest-bid framing) — all of which compress investor demand.

Bottom line

You’re paying “District 1 + balcony + lift” pricing for an apartment you likely won’t control for a long time. The deal can be fine only if the price reflects the tenancy reality, not the postcard location. If you want a home, this is the wrong product; if you want optionality, make the discount the entire point.

Negotiation strategy

Two ways this goes wrong: (1) canal/ Quay noise + nightlife vibe makes it less livable than the photos imply, (2) building operating costs (concierge, systems, garage) come in high and cap your resale/letting yield.

Move-in readiness

Depends — physically sounds fine, but practically it’s “not move-in” because it’s indefinitely tenanted.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (you can’t plan to live there on any sane timeline) Investor: ➖ (prime micro-location, but the rent is extremely low and the tenant situation dominates the return) Lifestyle buyer: ❌ (you’re buying a story, not an apartment you’ll enjoy)

Final verdict

Action: 🔴 PASS — unless you specifically want a long-duration, low-yield, legally sticky 1010 holding with upside tied to a very uncertain vacancy event.

Negotiation strategy

This should trade at a steep “tenanted forever” discount vs. a vacant 1010 3-room period building. With comps unknown, my best-effort target is €380k–€480k (anchored to the fact the net rent is ~€184/month and the tenancy is unbefristet since 1987). If the seller won’t move meaningfully, walk. Negotiation lever: demand hard proof on one verifyable uncertainty — exact tenancy terms (contract, indexation history, who pays what, any side agreements) and the building’s reserve + planned works; if either is weak, that’s immediate price leverage.

Bottom line

You’re paying 1010 pricing for 1010 location, but you’re not getting 1010 usability. The two ways this goes wrong: the tenant stays for many more years (or succession rights apply), and/or you inherit costly building works (old period building + lift) that your tiny rent can’t absorb. If you want an investment, buy a cleaner rent situation; if you want to live in 1010, buy vacant — this is neither.

  • Size: 38.5 m2
  • Rooms: 1
  • Outdoor space: balkon
  • Heating: district heating
  • District: Vienna 1

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (2022 build, high-spec finishes, modern HVAC).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (38.5 sqm / 1-room + sleep niche is tight long-term) Investor: ✔ (prime 1010 micro-location; but price-per-sqm + VAT mechanics are the whole game) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (concierge building + balcony/loggia + canal/ring vibe)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — trophy micro-location and new-build comfort, but you’re paying luxury pricing for very small size and the VAT/investor-price structure can change the “real” number massively.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €610k–€645k (if buying as an end-user and VAT is relevant, push for a structure/clarity that keeps your effective total in this band; comps unknown, so treat this as a risk-adjusted anchor, not a comp-based fact). Key levers: (1) VAT clarity: confirm in writing your total gross purchase price as private buyer vs investor (“Anlegerpreis” wording is a negotiable friction point). (2) Noise/orientation: verify terrace orientation + glazing and spend 15 minutes on-site at 08:00 and 22:30—Donaukanal + Ring traffic can be a deal-breaker in a small unit. (3) Running costs: ask for full breakdown (operating costs vs reserve fund vs concierge) and the last owners’ meeting minutes—premium services can creep.

Bottom line

You’re buying a serviced, new-build “city bolt-hole” in the strongest macro-area Vienna has. The risk is liquidity/value: tiny units at luxury €/sqm plus concierge premiums can be harder to resell outside boom cycles. If the noise is acceptable and the all-in price is controlled, it’s a clean lifestyle/investment hybrid.

Negotiation strategy

Start with a reality check: €670k on 61.89 sqm is ~€10.8k/sqm, which demands (a) a truly exceptional view, (b) strong sound insulation, and (c) a clean building file. Without comps here, target price range is best-effort: €610k–€650k (push toward the low end if it’s a lower floor, street-facing, or needs window upgrades). Ask specifically for: operating cost breakdown (repairs reserve), last 3 years of building works, planned major works, window specs, and proof of permitted use (main residence vs any restrictions).

Bottom line

If you want peak urban convenience and a “lights-on-the-canal” evening view, this is the right kind of listing. If you’re noise-sensitive or want a calm family base, treat this as a weekend apartment, not a forever home. The deal lives or dies on floor level, window quality, and building maintenance—not the marketing copy.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — zero interior/condition info + missing floor/energy data means you’re buying the location first and the apartment second.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (52.5 sqm for 3 rooms is tight; day-to-day practicality likely compromised) Investor: ➖ (ultra-prime area supports demand, but resale/rentability hinges on layout, light, and building/legal details) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (if you want “walk out the door into the city” and can tolerate noise/tourism)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the Stephans Square halo is real, but the data gaps (floor, condition, energy, exact building situation) are where buyers get overpay-trapped.

Negotiation strategy

Ask for: floor level, orientation (street/courtyard), window quality, lift, year/renovations, any building works planned, and a full breakdown of monthly costs (incl. repair reserve). Target price range (best-effort): €610k–€650k. Comps are unknown from the provided data; if the flat is bright/quiet/renovated you may end up closer to ask, if dark/noisy/low-floor you should press harder.

Bottom line

This is a micro-apartment priced like a trophy address (~€12.9k/sqm at ask). If it’s not quiet + bright + well-renovated, you’re paying for a postcard and living with the downside. Treat due diligence as the deal: layout and light decide whether this is a flex or a regret.

Buyer fit

Final verdict

Negotiation strategy

Make the offer contingent on a clean docs pack (WEG/house rules, reserve fund, last owners’ meeting minutes, energy/technical reports if available).

Bottom line

If you want Vienna “walk out into the city” living, this is the right kind of address. The balcony is a vibe, not a tranquility feature — assume evening buzz. Buy it for lifestyle + micro-location; don’t overpay for marble and marketing adjectives.

Move-in readiness

Depends — the unit itself reads “styled + turnkey”, but you can’t occupy until the current fixed-term tenancy ends (30.06.2027).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (2 rooms / ~54 sqm, 1st district lifestyle more than family practicality) Investor: ✔ (prime micro-location + existing tenant; yield is modest) Lifestyle buyer: ➖ (amazing spot, but delayed move-in unless tenant leaves earlier)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — killer 1010 micro-location and a polished product, but price-per-sqm is punchy and the “investment” story is capped by the low stated yield + locked-in tenancy.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €630k–€670k (best-effort; no comps provided, so this is a risk-adjusted range for a small 1950s building unit with tenancy lock-in). Use the hard levers: (1) fixed-term tenancy until 2027 (your capital is tied up), (2) request proof of net rent and all tenant terms + deposit + indexation, (3) ask for building reserve level and upcoming works (1952 stock can hide expensive vertical systems), (4) clarify what the ground-floor “atelier/lager/werkstatt” legally is (ownership allocation, permitted use, access rights, operating costs).

Bottom line

You’re paying for “Passauer Square / Maria am Gestade vibes” and walk-everywhere life. If the ancillary ground-floor unit is cleanly documented, it’s a nice differentiator; if it’s fuzzy, it’s a future headache. Great hold for a patient buyer; not a slam-dunk return play at this price.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready on paper (first occupancy + underfloor heating), but the real test is build quality + noise/overheating performance typical for top-floor units.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (tiny 2-room, terrace doesn’t fix the space constraint) Investor: ➖ (ultra-prime address helps, but 38.6 sqm at €699k is yield-hostile unless you’re betting on scarcity) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (if you want a compact “city base” with terrace in the absolute center)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — phenomenal micro-location and the terrace/first-occupancy angle are strong, but the €/sqm is extreme and the typical attic risks (summer heat, wind exposure, roof/terrace waterproofing) need a price cushion.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €620k–€660k (best-effort guess; without solid comps for similar new-build/top-floor micro-units in 1010, treat this as a negotiation anchor, not a valuation). Push on: small size (exit liquidity), attic climate risk, terrace maintenance/waterproofing concerns, and any missing clarity on floor/lift/building works. If the seller won’t move on price, ask for tangible value: kitchen package, shading/AC readiness, or documented construction/warranty details.

Bottom line

This is a “buy the location” product: you’re paying for 1010 walk-everywhere density, not for interior volume. If you love living in the city and can handle compact space, it can be a great personal base. As an investment, it’s only compelling if you accept low yield in exchange for prime scarcity and long-hold stability.

  • Heating: underfloor heating

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — very likely a renovation/spec deal: "shell" (belagsfertig) vs turnkey is a real quality fork.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ✔

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location is elite, but the pricing hinges on finish level, building health, and micro-noise/orientation.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort): €500k–€525k if belagsfertig; €560k–€585k if truly turnkey to a verifiable spec. I don’t have closed-sale comps here, so treat this as an anchor, not a valuation. Levers: (1) demand the full written fit-out specification and warranties (flooring, windows, underfloor heating system) and price any gaps; (2) ask for the building reserve status + upcoming works (roof/façade/lift/common piping) and discount for any planned capex; (3) tie price to noise/orientation uncertainty by making your offer conditional on a quiet-hours visit and window performance confirmation.

Bottom line

This is a “buy the postcode” apartment: you’re paying for the 1st district lifestyle and walk-everywhere convenience. Two ways this goes wrong: renovation/finish bait-and-switch (materials differ from marketing) and surprise building capex that lands right after you close. If the turnkey package is genuinely high-quality and documented, it can be a clean, low-hassle city base — but don’t pay luxury pricing for brochure promises.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — marketed as “first occupancy after renovation,” but it’s still in-progress and shown with symbolic images.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (53.8 sqm / 2 rooms is tight) Investor: ➖ (prime address + liquidity, but yield likely thin at this €/sqm) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (quiet 1010 pocket + walk-everywhere life)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — top micro-location and “quiet Inner City” is rare, but you’re paying for a promise (ongoing works, unclear floor/lift) and should price in execution risk.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €585,000–€615,000 (comps not provided, so this is a risk-adjusted guess). Ask for (1) detailed renovation scope/spec sheet + warranties, (2) firm completion date + handover protocol, (3) confirmation of floor, lift, courtyard/street orientation, window spec, and (4) building reserves + last owners’ meeting minutes. Bottom line

You’re buying the address and the vibe: central, intensely walkable, surprisingly quiet if the orientation checks out. At this size/price, the deal only makes sense if finish quality is genuinely premium and the building logistics (lift, access, storage) fit your daily life. If the quiet claim is real and the renovation is executed well, this is a high-liquidity ‘keep forever’ pied-à-terre — otherwise it’s an expensive headache in a tight envelope.

Move-in readiness

Depends — cosmetically easy (walls/tapet, entry flooring), but the no-lift + older-building realities are the real “liveability” questions.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms + no lift limits long-term practicality) Investor: ✔ (District 1, quiet courtyard orientation, strong rental appeal if condition checks out) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (classic Old Vienna feel + genuinely central but shielded from street noise)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location is elite and the courtyard-facing setup is a rare win, but price-per-sqm and the 2nd-floor/no-lift combo should translate into a meaningful discount.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €580k–€620k (best-effort; true comps unknown from the provided data). Push hard on: no lift, staircase/building wear, cosmetic renovation budget, and verify whether “quiet” still means restaurant/inner-courtyard noise. Also confirm the real asking price consistency (the text mentions a different figure) before you anchor.

Bottom line

If you want Inner City living without street chaos, this checks the right boxes. You’re paying for postcode + vibe; don’t pay “perfect-condition” money for a unit that admits renovation needs and has no lift. Courtyard-facing in 1010 is a lifestyle cheat code — but only if the courtyard is truly calm at night.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — the fit is more about the tenancy through 2028 than the condition; spec sounds high-end and “ready,” but you can’t treat it like an immediate self-use home.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (fixed tenancy until 2028 makes timing awkward) Investor: ✔ (clear rent line; strong central demand; furnished takeover optional) Lifestyle buyer: ➖ (amazing location + quiet courtyard bedroom, but you’re buying future lifestyle)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — prime-edge-of-1st location + 5th floor with lift is a rare combo, but a fixed lease until 2028 should buy you a meaningful discount versus vacant-possession pricing.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €630k–€675k (best-effort; I don’t have verified comps here). Anchor the offer on (1) “befristet vermietet bis 2028” liquidity/financing friction, (2) energy/heating risk (gas unit + mid-century building), and (3) any uncertainty on micro-noise (Schottentor) despite courtyard bedroom.

Bottom line

You’re paying for a top-tier urban node: transit density, park proximity, and daily-life convenience are off the charts. The hidden story is optionality: this is either a clean hold-to-2028 investment, or a delayed personal-use play — don’t overpay as if you could move in tomorrow. If the tenant terms are solid and the building/common areas check out, it’s one of the more “defensible” buys at this size in this zone — at the right price.

Move-in readiness

Needs work — “sanierungsbeduerftig” at ~28 sqm usually means you’re buying a project, not a turnkey studio.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ✔

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — the micro-location is strong, but the renovation unknowns can easily eat the “cheap” headline price.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €105k–€120k (comps unknown, so this is a risk-adjusted range, not a market-anchored comp set). Levers: (1) renovation scope uncertainty (electric/plumbing, windows, damp), (2) missing floor/orientation info (noise/daylight), (3) building financials after “modernized” works (reserve fund + any upcoming facade/roof/courtyard projects). Ask for the last 2–3 owners’ meeting minutes + reserve status; if they can’t provide quickly, price that uncertainty.

Bottom line

Neustift Lane in 1070 is the kind of address people actually want to live on — dense amenities, walk-everywhere, lots of transit. But at 27.98 sqm, the deal lives or dies on light, layout, and whether the building’s “modernization” is truly finished. If it’s ground floor / courtyard-dark / noisy street-facing, this becomes a value trap unless you buy it meaningfully below asking.

€162,000 · 42.4 m2 · 2 rooms · Halbgasse 20, Vienna, 07. District, Neubau
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €162,000
  • Size: 42.4 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Floor: ground floor
  • District: Vienna 7

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — it’s unbefristet rented (you’re buying the tenancy, not a home you can occupy).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ❌

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — prime 1070 micro-location, but the “investment” story collapses if the rent is truly ~€47.49 net/month and/or the tenancy is hard to reposition.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €120k–€145k (best-effort guess; comps and true rent/tenancy terms unknown). Anchor hard on the unbefristet tenancy + extremely low stated net rent + ground-floor liquidity discount; make your offer conditional on (1) full rent roll + contract review, (2) building reserve status and upcoming works, (3) confirmation of whether the unit has any balcony/right to use the new hofseitig balconies (or if that’s just a building feature). If they won’t move meaningfully on price, ask for seller to cover transaction-related costs (e.g., a portion of fees) or to document capex already done and what’s still pending.

Bottom line

This is “Betongold” only if you’re pricing it like a long, slow bond with tenant-protection risk. Great area, but you’re buying cashflow that looks close to symbolic — upside depends on legal/tenancy realities, not vibes. Two ways it goes wrong: you’re stuck with a legacy tenant at a tiny rent for years/decades; or the 1900 building hits you with reserve shortfalls + follow-on works (façade/roof/risers) that eat the yield.

Family: ➖ (7th is great day-to-day, but without size/rooms/floor you can’t judge family practicality) Investor: ✔ (central, amenity-dense micro-area; but yield math is unknowable without sqm/fees) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, restaurant-heavy, strong inner-city vibe)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location is doing the heavy lifting, but the listing extract is missing the deal-critical inputs (unit size, floor, running costs, energy/heating), so you negotiate hard or wait for a specific unit sheet.

This is a premium inner-7th micro-location (dense food/drink, groceries, parks, transit all within ~300m) — great for living and generally liquid. The risk is not “the area,” it’s the unit: orientation/noise, fee structure, and what €162k actually buys. If you can’t get a full unit fact sheet and a clean cost breakdown quickly, assume you’re being sold a headline price and be ready to walk.

Move-in readiness

Depends — it’s unbefristet rented, so “ready” is about the tenancy file and building finances, not your own move-in.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (you likely can’t occupy it) Investor: ✔ (only if you’re explicitly fine with ultra-low rent + long holding) Lifestyle buyer: ❌ (wrong product: tenant in place, 2 rooms, investor framing)

Final verdict

Action: 🔴 PASS — at this rent level, you’re buying optionality on future vacancy/value uplift, not an income property; the timeline is not yours.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €165k–€185k (best-effort guess; comps unknown). Anchor on the economic reality: net rent ~€78/month is effectively negligible versus capital tied up, and monthly costs (~€155) suggest the cashflow story is not the headline. Make the offer contingent on full tenancy document review + building reserve (Ruecklage) statement. Key lever: verify the exact rent regime/contract terms (what can/can’t be indexed/raised, and under which conditions) — price the deal off what’s legally collectible, not marketing.

Bottom line

You’re paying 1070 address premium for a unit you can’t control. Two ways this goes wrong: (1) tenant remains for decades → you’re stuck with near-zero yield; (2) 1900 building surprises (major works/reserves shortfall) turn this into a capital-call machine. If you want Vienna 7 as an investment, aim for either a realistically rentable unit (vacant/limited lease) or a price that reflects “bond-like” cashflow and illiquidity.

Move-in readiness

Depends — physically likely fine (refreshed common areas), but functionally it’s not “ready for you” because it’s sold with an indefinite tenancy.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (indefinite tenant + ground-floor dynamics) Investor: ➖ (works only if you accept ultra-low yield and long hold) Lifestyle buyer: ❌ (you can’t occupy it on your timeline)

Final verdict

Action: 🔴 PASS — unless you explicitly want a long-duration, low-cashflow bet on 7th district appreciation with constrained control.

Negotiation strategy

This is priced like a “story” listing ("safe capitalanlage") but the disclosed net main rent (~€78/month) is the entire reality: cashflow is near-zero and control risk is high. Use that to push hard. Target price range (best-effort guess; comps unknown): €140,000–€175,000 (lower end if the lease is truly un-terminable/very protected; upper end only if there’s a clear, lawful path to meaningful rent uplift). Bottom line

You’re not buying an apartment — you’re buying a legal situation. 7th district micro-location is strong, but the income line is shockingly weak. If you proceed, your return thesis must be “decades + appreciation,” not yield.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (2018 renovation; “second occupancy” after a rental).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (32.78 sqm / 1 room) Investor: ✔ (liquid micro-location near major transit; easy-to-rent format) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, restaurant-heavy, central)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — excellent 7th-district micro-area and a tidy small unit, but the price needs to reflect 1-room liquidity limits, gas heating risk, and “sold as-is.”

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort guess; comps not provided): €195,000–€205,000. Anchor with: (1) gas “individual heating” future capex/transition uncertainty, (2) 1-room ceiling on resale buyer pool, (3) no energy class shown, (4) “sold as it lies and stands” language, (5) ask for proof of 2018 works + invoices. If they won’t move on price, push for seller-paid fixes, full documentation, and a clean handover protocol.

Bottom line

2nd floor with lift in a classic building + strong transit access = high day-to-day convenience. You’re buying the location more than the layout: great for a solo city base, less flexible long-term. At ~€6,560/sqm, it needs to feel truly turn-key and low-surprise—verify everything.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (1-room, 34 sqm) Investor: ✔ (liquidity + rental demand likely strong in this micro-location) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walk-everywhere, café/restaurant density, city-first living)

Final verdict

Bottom line

If the unit is quiet and genuinely bright, it’s a very sellable little asset — but don’t pay peak price without proof.

Depends — livable, but the “WC on the corridor” setup is a lifestyle/marketability drag until you remodel.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (works for a couple with a baby; gets tight fast, and the WC situation is awkward) Investor: ➖ (location is A+, but layout/WC config can limit tenant pool and resale liquidity) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (7th district, side street, cafés/transit density — strong day-to-day Vienna living)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location and classic period building feel, but price should reflect the WC-in-hall + renovation/approval uncertainty.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €265,000–€285,000 (best-effort; exact comps unknown). Bottom line

You’re buying the 1070 lifestyle first: walkability, food/drink density, transit access. But you’re also buying a “story” apartment — the corridor WC will be the first thing every future buyer/tenant reacts to. If the building/reserves check out and WC integration is straightforward, this can become a very sellable 2-room city base.

  • Size: 51.91 m2
  • Rooms: 2

  • District: Vienna 7

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — likely fine structurally, but “courtyard quiet + terrace” often hides trade-offs (light, ground-floor feel, moisture risk) that you must verify.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms / ~52 sqm is tight long-term) Investor: ➖ (strong 7th district liquidity, but terrace/courtyard specifics can narrow tenant/buyer pool) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (classic 1070 value prop: walkable, restaurants, transit, with a private outdoor space)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location and “quiet + terrace” is rare in 1070 at this ticket, but you need to price in light/noise realities of an inner courtyard unit.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €275k–€290k (best-effort guess; without comps/condition/monthly costs/energy data this is a risk-adjusted range). Push on missing operating costs, heating system age (individual heating), and any courtyard/terrace maintenance issues.

Bottom line

You’re buying the 1070 lifestyle in miniature: dense amenities + transit, then retreat to the courtyard. Terrace is the trump card — but only if it’s sunny enough and not a “patio in a light well.” If the light is decent and the costs are sane, it’s a clean little city base; if not, it’s an expensive compromise.

Move-in ready (recent renovation/“first occupancy” positioning), but verify workmanship + what exactly was replaced (electrics/plumbing/windows).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (42.8 sqm, 1 bedroom) Investor: ➖ (great tenant demand, but gas + U6 noise risk + 7th district entry yield may be tight) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (central, walkable, tons of cafés/restaurants, fast transit)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong micro-location and renovated period building appeal, but you’re paying for “7th district + turnkey”; make sure the building/energy/heating story doesn’t bite later.

Negotiation strategy

Ask for the full renovation scope (invoices, guarantees), building reserve + planned works, and confirm courtyard-side bedroom quietness and U6/road noise at peak times. Target price range: €270k–€290k (best-effort guess; without comps this is a positioning range, not a valuation).

Bottom line

You’re buying a compact, highly liquid “city base” in a very convenient pocket near U6. Biggest swing factors: noise + gas heating + building-level capex (old building). If the courtyard bedroom is genuinely quiet and the building is financially healthy, it’s a solid small-home play.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (first occupancy fit-out implied; verify handover standard + warranties).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms + ground floor can work for a couple with baby, but long-term space tight) Investor: ➖ (rentability depends on ground-floor discount + terrace premium; check lift/house condition + running costs) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (huge courtyard terrace in a prime, walkable pocket is the main story)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong micro-location and rare terrace-to-size ratio, but ground-floor risk (light/privacy) means you should not pay “upper 7th district” pricing without proof it lives well.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort, comps unknown): €270k–€290k. Anchor with (1) ground-floor discount, (2) unknown monthly costs/reserve fund, (3) terrace usability (sun hours, noise, privacy) being unproven until you stand there. Bottom line

If the terrace is genuinely quiet and gets decent light, this is a lifestyle steal for 7th. If it’s shaded/cold or overlooked, you’re buying a ground-floor compromise with a photo-optics terrace. Treat the viewing like a terrace audit first, apartment second.

Betongold -unbefristet vermietet - unbefristet vermietet - sichere Kapitalanlage im Herzen der Stadt mit Zukunftspotenzial (Betongold -unbefristet vermietet - unbefristet vermietet - sichere Kapitalanlage im Herzen der Stadt mit Zukunftspotenzial)
€305,000 · 79.01 m2 · 3 rooms · Halbgasse 20, Vienna, 07. District, Neubau
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €305,000
  • Size: 79.01 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • District: Vienna 7

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — it’s explicitly advertised as indefinitely rented, so “move-in” is not the product here.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (indefinite tenancy blocks self-use) Investor: ✔ (cheap entry price for 7th district, but rent level looks ultra-low) Lifestyle buyer: ❌ (you can’t actually live there on your timeline)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — interesting 7th-district base asset, but the indefinite lease + extremely low net rent makes the economics and exit strategy the whole story.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €240k–€280k (best-effort guess; I don’t have reliable comps here, and the indefinite tenancy should materially discount the price). Use the verifiable uncertainties as levers: (1) confirm the legal rent regime and tenant protections (category rent / regulated / free market) and whether any indexed uplift is permitted; (2) get the building reserve + planned works and show how any capex will crush an already thin yield; (3) request documentation on the balcony addition (approvals, who maintains it, any special assessments).

Bottom line

This is “Betongold” only if you’re comfortable owning prime-location real estate with bond-like cashflow — except the coupon here looks closer to a rounding error. You’re mainly buying location optionality (7th district, dense amenities/transit) while accepting tenant/lease constraints. If the paperwork doesn’t clearly support future rent adjustment paths, you’re buying a very long-duration, low-yield asset — price needs to reflect that.

  • Size: 54.84 m2
  • Rooms: 2

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — it’s indefinitely rented, so it’s “hands-off ready” for income, not for owner-occupancy.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (unbefristet vermietet = no timeline control) Investor: ✔ (stable tenant profile + prime 7th-district liquidity) Lifestyle buyer: ❌ (you’re buying paper + patience, not a home you can use)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location and classic old-building appeal, but the 2.27% yield + indefinite lease caps upside unless the entry price is meaningfully sharp.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €285,000–€305,000 (best-effort; comps not provided). Anchor on the low yield, indefinite tenancy (limited reversion value/timing), and unknown unit-level specs (floor/heating/energy class). Push for full lease pack (rent indexation terms, tenant rights, deposits, maintenance history) and price the risk, not the brochure.

Bottom line

Prime new-build (1070) streets + strong amenity density means this will stay sellable. But you’re effectively buying a bond with equity risk: low current yield, uncertain reversion timing. If they won’t discount for the lease constraint, walk—there are easier investor buys in Vienna.

Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €325,000
  • Size: 44.07 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 7

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — missing core info (floor, condition, monthly costs, energy cert), but the basics (size/room count) suggest a straightforward city flat if the building + heating check out.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (44 sqm / 2 rooms in a busy central zone) Investor: ✔ (high-demand micro-location; liquidity likely strong if costs + building condition are sane) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (if you want walk-everywhere Vienna and can live with inner-city noise)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — fantastic 7th-district address and daily-life convenience, but individual heating + unknown running costs/building state create price-risk.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €305k–€320k (best-effort guess; comps not provided, so treat as a negotiation anchor not a valuation). Ask for: (1) Betriebskosten + Ruecklage + last 2–3 years increases, (2) energy certificate, (3) house condition/roof/façade/stairwell, (4) heating system type/age + real winter bills, (5) any planned building works and special assessments.

Bottom line

This is the kind of micro-area you buy for lifestyle and resale liquidity: restaurants/groceries/transit density is elite. The “gotchas” will be noise, building maintenance surprises, and heating economics. If the building is well-run and costs are normal, it’s a strong small-city-home; if not, you want a meaningful discount or you walk.

Move-in readiness

Depends — the unit reads “ready,” but the office/usage setup + ground-floor reality should be sanity-checked before you commit.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms + ground floor can work for a couple w/ baby, but it’s not a classic family layout) Investor: ✔ (7th district demand + explicit short-term rental permission is rare; check operating costs + ground-floor tenant profile) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (city-core living with a quiet inner-courtyard terrace and flexible work-from-home vibe)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong location/value story, but the “office in ownership” + EG (ground floor) + basement-heavy value means you should price it like a slightly harder-to-resell asset.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €300k–€320k (best-effort guess; I don’t have direct comps here). Anchor on: ground-floor discount, office dedication/administrative friction risk, and that a big chunk of “value” is in the ~36 sqm basement (useful, but not priced like living area). If they push back, trade: keep price but ask for garage spot pricing concessions or seller covering part of closing/contract costs.

Bottom line

If you want a 7th-district base that doubles as a serious home office/studio, this is compelling. If you want “classic Vienna resale liquidity,” ground floor + office framing are the two things that will always narrow your buyer pool. The courtyard terrace + modern-ish 2002 building/energy numbers are the stabilizers here.

#55
Hell, viele Fenster und idealer Grundriss fuer 2 Personen (Hell, viele Fenster und idealer Grundriss für 2 Personen)
€335,000 · 46.83 m2 · 2 rooms · Vienna, 07. District, Neubau
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Address/map mismatch flagged (likely approximate pin).
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €335,000
  • Size: 46.83 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Floor: 4
  • Lift: No (as stated)
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 7

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (assuming typical cosmetic touch-ups); the big “work” item is the 4th-floor walk-up lifestyle.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (too small; walk-up friction) Investor: ➖ (strong location liquidity, but micro-size + walk-up narrows tenant/buyer pool) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (classic 7th district city-base for 1–2 people)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — fantastic central micro-area and brightness potential, but the no-lift top-floor walk-up should be priced in.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €305k–€325k (best-effort guess; without comps this is a walk-up discount thesis, not a comp-backed valuation). Anchor on: 4th floor without lift, gas floor heating operating cost/modernization risk, and any “Energiepass is fine but not great” narrative. Ask for: recent building works history, reserve fund status, last staircase/roof façade works, any planned gas system changes, and exact layout measurements.

Bottom line

2-room + separate kitchen in a prime 7th district pocket is a very livable setup. You’re paying for location and windows—make sure you’re not also overpaying for the stairs. If you love the neighborhood and you’re fine carrying groceries up four flights, this can be a high-satisfaction home base.
  • Micro-area is elite for daily life: very high food/drink density plus groceries and transit nearby; you’ll live mostly on foot.
  • Quiet-bright combo is plausible: courtyard-facing + top floor often means less street noise and more daylight.
  • Liquidity is strong, but the buyer pool is narrower: 46.8 m² sells well in 1070, yet “no lift, 4th floor” is a permanent objection for many.
  • Layout signal is good: 2 rooms + separate kitchen reads more like “real apartment” than a studio, better for work-from-home or couple living.
  • 1900 building = charm + unknowns: check common-area condition, moisture history, windows age, and whether the building has upcoming big-ticket works.
  • Gas floor heating: comfortable when working well, but budget for maintenance and ask about any building/municipal direction on future gas constraints.
  • Monthly costs look relatively light: but confirm what’s included/excluded and the reserve fund trajectory (low fees can hide deferred maintenance).

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great location + functional layout, but old-building unknowns + individual heating + missing basics (floor/energy/monthlies) should translate into a price concession.

Negotiation strategy

Furnished can be a plus (instant move-in) or a red flag (covering shortcuts) — inspect finishes and plumbing/electrics carefully. If the documents confirm low ongoing costs and no looming building works, it’s a solid small-city-base unit; otherwise, price is your only protection.

Final verdict

Bottom line

If it’s truly renovated and there’s a lift: you’re paying for a very strong 1070 lifestyle at a tolerable size. If it’s a walk-up refresh with older systems: buy only at a discount — convenience and resale will hinge on stairs + building works. Paperwork matters here (project-like multi-unit sale + double-broker note): treat it as a due-diligence-heavy deal, not an impulse buy.

Family: ➖ (could work, but missing rooms/sqm + likely central/noisy micro-location) Investor: ✔ (7th district + Mariahilfer proximity = resilient demand, but only if layout/condition and monthly costs pencil out) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walk-everywhere, restaurants, shops, transit density; strong “Vienna city life”)

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the micro-location is a banger, but without sqm/rooms/floor you’re buying a headline; treat this as “high potential, high verification.”

  • Size: 46.83 m2

  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 7

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (sold furnished), but expect “old-building reality”: check windows/heating, wiring, and any deferred maintenance.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (effectively a large studio / not a true 2-bed setup) Investor: ✔ (high-demand micro-location; furnished + transit-heavy area) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Spittelberg/Mariahilfer vibe: walk-everywhere, eat-everywhere)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — fantastic 7th-district micro-area, but you’re paying a premium for location and the “2-room” framing doesn’t match the functional layout.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €310,000–€325,000 (comps unknown from provided data; range reflects studio-ish layout + likely period building capex risk). Push on: (1) it’s described as Garçonnière / one large living-sleep room, (2) gas floor heating running costs + future regulatory/upgrade risk, (3) missing key facts in the extract (energy cert, operating costs, lift, floor plan). If you want it renovated “as first occupancy,” demand a fixed, itemized scope, timeline, and warranty—otherwise treat “on request” pricing as open-ended.

Bottom line

You’re buying one thing here: 1070 walkability (100+ food/drink spots within 300m is the tell). Great as a city base or rental, less convincing if you need true separation of bedroom/living. Do due diligence like an investor even if you’re buying with your heart.

++NSG122++ Charmante 4-rooms Altbauwohnung mit viel Potenzial in Toplage - sanierungsbeduerftig" (++NSG122++ Charmante 4-Zimmer Altbauwohnung mit viel Potenzial in Toplage - sanierungsbedürftig")
€348,890 · 83.23 m2 · 4 rooms · Neustiftgasse, Vienna, 07. District, Neubau
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Address/map mismatch flagged (likely approximate pin).
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €348,890
  • Size: 83.23 m2
  • Rooms: 4
  • Lift: Yes (as stated)
  • District: Vienna 7

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Needs work — explicitly sold in renovation-needed condition, so budget/time for a full refresh is part of the deal.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (good size/room count, but renovation disruption + unknown noise/orientation) Investor: ➖ (strong location liquidity, but capex and monthly costs can eat returns) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (prime 1070 vibe + walk-everywhere amenities if you want to design your own space)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — location is doing the heavy lifting, but you should only buy if the renovation math and building plans don’t surprise you.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €315k–€340k (comps unknown; range is a condition/uncertainty discount). Anchor on “sanierungsbeduerftig/as-is”, missing heating/energy info, and the fact that real renovation costs + downtime are on you. Concrete lever: request clarity (in writing) on planned common-area works and the reserve usage plan; if major works are upcoming or the reserve is earmarked, push price down or ask seller contribution.

Bottom line

This is a classic “buy the postcode, fix the apartment” play in 1070. Two ways it goes wrong: renovation budget blowout (old-building surprises) and a noisy/low-privacy orientation (street/traffic) that you can’t remodel away. If your plan is to renovate anyway and you can verify quiet orientation + manageable building pipeline, it can be a very smart entry ticket to the district.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (likely): modern kitchen + district heating suggests a “showable now” apartment, but condition still hinges on windows/bathroom/paint.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ✔

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great 1070 micro-location and a clean, sellable size, but too many unknowns (floor, courtyard vs street, noise, building condition) to “buy fast” at this price.

Negotiation strategy

Ask for the missing deal-breakers in writing: exact floor, lift, windows orientation (street vs inner courtyard), year/building type, recent renovations (roof/façade/riser pipes), and any planned special assessments. Target price range (best-effort): €320k–€340k. I don’t have true local comps from the listing, so this range is based on the premium 1070 micro-area + small-unit liquidity, offset by missing key specs and typical Burg Lane noise risk. Use the monthly costs (€279.47) + reserve line to probe building health: request the last owners’ meeting minutes and the reserve usage history.

Bottom line

This is the classic “small, central Vienna apartment” play: easy to rent/sell, great daily-life convenience. The single biggest swing factor is noise/light (street-facing Burg Lane vs quiet courtyard). Don’t bid blindly without that. If it’s quiet + good light, it’s a solid long-term hold; if not, it’s an overpay at list price.

Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €349,000
  • Size: 75.77 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • District: Vienna 7

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — the unit is unbefristet rented, so it’s “ready” only as a held investment, not for owner-occupancy.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (unbefristet tenancy blocks your timeline) Investor: ✔ (income from day 1, but yield is thin) Lifestyle buyer: ❌ (you’re buying a story, not a home you can use)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great 1070/new-build micro-location, but the stated ~2.04% yield + unbefristet tenant is a leverage point on price.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort guess; comps not verified): €320 000–€340 000. Anchor hard on “unbefristet = liquidity + exit-risk discount” and treat it like a bond: the price must reflect the capped upside. Push for a target price range of EUR 315,000–335,000 (best-effort guess; I don’t have local rent-law/comps specifics here, so call that out during negotiation). Also demand full tenancy pack (contract, rent breakdown, indexation history, operating cost split, deposit, arrears history) and building capex history (roof/facade/stairwell/balconies) to quantify risk.

Bottom line

If you want to live in 1070 soon: wrong product. If you want Vienna “core” exposure with stable cashflow: location is the win, but you must buy it cheap enough to justify the low yield. The courtyard/balconies/common-area refresh reads like “kept alive,” not a luxury reposition — price should reflect that reality.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ✔

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — prime 7th district micro-location and a 45 m² 1-room layout can work great, but the €/m² is punchy and DG units can hide comfort/maintenance trade-offs.

Negotiation strategy

If you want a high-energy, walk-everywhere Vienna life, this nails it. If you need quiet, cool summers, and “set-and-forget” ownership, the DG + old-building risk profile needs hard checks. At the right price it’s a strong city pied-à-terre; at asking it’s flirting with overpaying for the postcode.

Family: ➖ (2 rooms at ~51 sqm works for a couple + baby, not long-term family-proof) Investor: ✔ (liquid micro-location, strong rental demand profile for 7th) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walk-everywhere, cafés/culture, courtyard calm potential)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — great 7th-district positioning and classic building appeal, but the price looks punchy for the size unless the interior finish is genuinely high-end and quiet.

Negotiation strategy

Ask for the hard facts: renovation year of the apartment, window spec, courtyard vs street orientation, noise, elevator, reserve fund, planned works, and the exact balcony/general-garden rules. Target price range: €340k–€360k (best-effort; without verified comps this is a directional range, not a valuation). Use the missing/unclear items (energy class, heating, elevator, detailed condition) to justify a structured discount or seller-paid fixes.

Bottom line

2-room in a prime, urban 7th-district pocket: the location does a lot of work for you. The courtyard upgrades + big green common garden are meaningful quality-of-life upsides if the unit is oriented quiet. Pay up only if the interior condition and acoustics are excellent; otherwise negotiate hard because the sqm is small and the building-era risks are real.

Kurz vor Fertigstellung - PROVISIONSFREI - City-Apartment - 2 rooms possible - approx. 45 m2 WFL - hochwertige Ausstattung - Neubauprojekt (Kurz vor Fertigstellung - PROVISIONSFREI - City-Apartment - 2 Zimmer möglich - ca. 45m² WFL - hochwertige Ausstattung - Neubauprojekt)
€379,000 · 45.34 m2 · 2 rooms · Seidengasse 19, Vienna, 07. District, Neubau
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €379,000
  • Size: 45.34 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: district heating
  • District: Vienna 7

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — “near completion” can mean anything from turnkey to weeks/months of snagging + missing documents.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ✔

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — prime 7th-district micro-location and new-build comfort, but the €/sqm is punchy for ~45 m² and delivery risk is real.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €345k–€365k (best-effort guess; comps not provided, so treat this as a negotiation anchor, not a valuation). Levers to press: - Completion/snagging: require a written handover date, a detailed defect list process, and clear retention/holdback terms if anything is unfinished. - Legal/technical certainty: ask for a complete document pack (final floor plan as-built, usage approval status, energy certificate, building spec sheet). Any missing item = price haircut. - Monthly carrying costs: if operating costs/reserve contributions aren’t disclosed yet, assume upside risk and negotiate accordingly.

Bottom line

You’re buying “city life density” more than space: restaurants/amenities are right there, which is great—until noise and late-night foot traffic land on your facade. If it’s street-facing or above active ground-floor uses, this can turn from chic to exhausting fast. Get the delivery + documents nailed down, then pay a fair number—not a marketing number.

#69
Betongold -unbefristet vermietet - sichere Kapitalanlage mit 2,48 % Rendite im Herzen der Stadt mit Zukunftspotenzial (Betongold -unbefristet vermietet - sichere Kapitalanlage mit 2,48 % Rendite im Herzen der Stadt mit Zukunftspotenzial)
€385,000 · 54.95 m2 · 2 rooms · Halbgasse 20, Vienna, 07. District, Neubau
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €385,000
  • Size: 54.95 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • District: Vienna 7

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — it’s positioned as a refreshed building, but this unit is sold unbefristet rented (so “ready” is irrelevant unless you can legally/realistically regain use).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (unbefristet tenancy makes self-use unlikely/slow) Investor: ➖ (stable cashflow, but low yield + tenancy risk) Lifestyle buyer: ❌ (you’re buying a tenant + legal constraints, not a home)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location in 1070, but the unbefristet rent locks your upside; the deal only makes sense at a price that compensates for tenancy rigidity and low initial yield.

Negotiation strategy

Start from the cashflow reality: net rent ~€796/month (~€9.6k/year) against €385k is a thin yield for Vienna 7 unless the tenant situation is unusually favorable. Target price range (best-effort guess): €315k–€345k (comps unknown; this range is driven by yield/risk rather than comparable sales). Concrete lever: demand hard documents before offer — full tenancy agreement (incl. start date, indexation clause, category, any side agreements), proof of current payments, and the building’s reserve/major works plan; if any of these are weak/unclear, discount aggressively.

Bottom line

You’re paying 1070 pricing for a bond-like asset — safe-ish income, limited control. Two ways this goes wrong: (1) the rent is materially below market and you can’t rebase it for years/decades; (2) building capex (roof/DG, façade, plumbing, balconies) hits and eats the already-thin yield. If you want optionality (self-use, resale to owner-occupiers), this is the wrong product unless the price is meaningfully lower.

Move-in readiness

Needs work — the “in the years” condition + likely older wet rooms/kitchen means plan on a renovation before it feels modern.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (66 sqm / 3 rooms can work, but the split-level + stairs and renovation load make it situational) Investor: ➖ (District 7 demand is real, WG-friendly helps, but duplex oddity + capex + unclear floor/lift/energy data adds risk) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (quiet courtyard windows in new-build + walkable culture/food density is exactly the inner-city sweet spot)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location and courtyard quiet are premium, but the unit is a renovation project and the unusual two-level layout should buy you a discount.

Negotiation strategy

Bottom line

You’re buying “quiet courtyard in the middle of everything,” not turnkey square meters. If you can renovate smartly, this can become a killer city base; if you want plug-and-play, you’ll resent it. The duplex vibe is charming but niche — make sure the layout actually improves daily life before you pay for the novelty.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Needs work — clearly renovation-required, plus building-wide works running through Q1 2026.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms + likely street noise; workable for a couple/new baby, not long-term family) Investor: ➖ (renovation risk + no lift + building works timing; could work if bought right) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (1070 near Lane/Mariahilfer energy, high ceilings, classic period building feel)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location and ceiling height, but the “sanierungsbeduerftig” condition + no lift + 2026 common-area works mean you need a meaningful discount to make the math and hassle worth it.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €340k–€370k (best-effort guess; I don’t have verified comparable sales/renovation quotes here, so treat this as a negotiation anchor, not a valuation). Ask for: (1) detailed scope + funding plan for the scheduled building works (and whether special levies are expected), (2) recent reserve status, (3) any known façade/roof/staircase issues beyond the listed items. Use the no-lift factor and renovation uncertainty to push price down. Also flag the listing inconsistency on floor (“1st floor” vs “2nd storey”) and make them clarify exactly how many flights.

Bottom line

If you want 1070 walk-everywhere life, this is the right neighborhood and the unit has the period building bones (height, layout flexibility). But you’re buying a project: renovation cost, timeline stress, and potential building levies are the real price. Win condition is simple: buy it cheap enough that you can renovate without regretting it in 18 months.

Needs work — it’s explicitly unsaniert and described as requiring a full renovation.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ Investor: ✔ Lifestyle buyer: ✔

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location is genuinely prime, but the renovation scope + building works through ~Q1 2026 + missing energy cert should translate into a meaningful discount.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €350k–€375k (best-effort guess; I don’t have comps for this exact street/micro-block and condition). Bottom line

This is a classic 1070 ‘buy the block, rebuild the flat’ play. The upside is walkability and future resale desirability; the downside is cost/time uncertainty and living through a building that’s mid-upgrade. Two ways this goes wrong: renovation costs blow out (old wiring/plumbing, floors, windows/orientation noise mitigation), and the building works trigger extra levies or delays past Q1 2026 while you’re also renovating your unit.

If they won’t move on price, ask for seller-side concessions (e.g., credit for renovation, faster handover, clear documentation).

Bottom line

Two things are doing the heavy lifting here: the 7th district micro-area and the lift+loggia combo. Two things can bite: renovation uncertainty and “close to U6” living reality. Buy it if you want to shape a long-term home and can stomach a proper refurb budget + timeline.
