Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €419,900
  • Size: 84.06 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Outdoor space: loggia
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 7

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Needs work — reads like a full gut-renovation (layout is fine; condition is not).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (3 rooms works, but Urban-Loritz- Square traffic/noise + renovation downtime) Investor: ➖ (rentability depends on total capex + gas heating + energy performance) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (prime 7th vibe, west loggia, classic period building canvas)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — great location + 3rd floor with lift + loggia, but the “generalsanierungsbeduerftig” flag and high HWB should buy you a meaningful discount.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort): €365k–€395k (comps unknown; range assumes substantial renovation scope + risk buffer). Push hard on: full electrical/plumbing/boiler risk, windows, floor leveling, bathroom stack constraints, and noise mitigation for the west-facing living room/loggia. Ask for recent building works, reserve status, and any planned common-area capex; use uncertainty to justify price protection.

Bottom line

If you want “finished and calm,” this isn’t it. If you want a 7th-district base with real upside and you can manage a renovation: this is exactly the kind of property that can turn into a long-term keeper. The winning move is budgeting brutally and negotiating like it’s a construction project, not a cute period building.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — sounds generally usable, but the listing reads like a “generic refurb” pitch; you need a viewing to confirm noise, windows, and building condition.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (good 3-room layout, but the Urban-Loritz- Square micro-location can be loud/chaotic for kid life) Investor: ✔ (high-demand rental pocket, strong transit + amenity density, but watch building/legal/fee details) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walk-everywhere, cafes, culture, U6 on your doorstep; loggia helps sanity)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great 7th-district edge location and a real loggia, but U6/major junction noise + gas heating + high monthly costs demand a price haircut.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €385k–€405k (comps unknown from the provided data, so treat this as a risk-adjusted bid, not a market-derived one). Use (1) U6/Urban-Loritz- Square noise risk, (2) gas system + future running-cost/regulatory uncertainty, (3) monthly costs €358.38, and (4) “KI-generated visualizations” (marketing over substance) as leverage. Ask for: Betriebskosten/Ruecklage split, last 3 years of house minutes, planned works, and any lift/roof/facade projects.

Bottom line

This is a classic “city-life” buy: you’re paying for being in the flow. The make-or-break is acoustic comfort (street + U6) and the building’s forward maintenance plan. If it’s quiet inside and the building is clean financially, it’s a strong lifestyle/investment hybrid.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — “shortly before completion” usually means timelines + final snag list can still bite.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ✔

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — prime 1070 micro-location + courtyard/terrace is strong, but new-build delivery risk and unclear running costs/energy spec mean you buy a promise unless paperwork is clean.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €405k–€420k (best-effort guess; comps not provided, so treat this as a negotiation anchor, not a valuation). Push on (1) delivery date + penalties, (2) what “weighted” area (gew. NFL) includes, (3) terrace/courtyard orientation and noise assumptions, and (4) any add-ons that inflate true cost (garage, fit-out choices).

Bottom line

You’re paying 1070 pricing for a compact, new-build courtyard unit with terrace — great daily-life convenience if it’s genuinely quiet. Two ways this goes wrong: handover delays/defects, or “courtyard” still being loud/dim (and the terrace ends up unused). If the documents confirm low ongoing costs + solid building reserves plan and the courtyard is truly calm, it’s a defensible buy at the right number.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (3 rooms helps, but 2nd floor no lift + corridor WC is a daily-friction combo) Investor: ➖ (location is strong, but capex + functional obsolescence can crush yield unless bought well) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (if you want 7th-district energy and can stomach renovation + street noise risk)

Final verdict

Bottom line

You’re buying location first, apartment second. If the renovation reveals floor/installation surprises, this can swing from “smart buy” to “money pit” fast. Make it a deal only at a price that leaves real buffer for capex + building contributions.

Depends — reads like a refreshed building with a generally “ready” unit, but key unit-level details (condition, windows, roof/DG build-out quality, heating/energy) aren’t evidenced here.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms / 59 sqm in a very lively micro-area; workable for a couple + baby, tight for more) Investor: ✔ (7th district liquidity + strong rental demand; watch yield at this price and any DG-specific maintenance risk) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (prime walkable new-build/Mariahilfer proximity; cafés/restaurants/transit density is the main draw)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great district + likely attractive DG “city lifestyle” appeal, but €450k for 59 sqm needs justification via finish level, outdoor space, and building/energy facts.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €405k–€435k (best-effort guess; without true comps and without unit finish/energy/lift/outdoor confirmation, pricing power is uncertain). Ask for: (1) DG/roof refurbishment timeline + warranties, (2) heating type and energy certificate, (3) lift status and whether it serves the DG, (4) balcony size/orientation if any for Top 23, (5) reserve fund + planned works, (6) exact monthly all-in costs incl. repair reserve, (7) noise/heat in summer (DG) and window specs.

Bottom line

Two-room DG in the 7th is a proven “easy to love, easy to resell” category. Your risk is paying the “scene district premium” without getting premium unit specs (outdoor space, lift, energy, quiet). Treat this as a lifestyle purchase first; make the building paperwork do the heavy lifting before you pay up.

  • Size: 33.5 m2

  • Lift: Yes (as stated)

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — the unit itself sounds freshly refreshed (floors), but the “extra” cellar/utility vibe and any garden/drainage realities are what decide day-1 comfort.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (1 room + small interior footprint) Investor: ➖ (great location/uniqueness, but liquidity/tenant pool is narrower and “garden + special cellar” adds diligence) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (quiet courtyard + big private garden in 1070 is the whole point)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location and rarity are real, but the €/m² is being driven by the garden/bonus space, so you should pay for usability you can prove, not marketing.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €410k–€440k (comps unknown from the provided data, so treat this as a leverage range, not a valuation). Push on (1) the small interior size (33.5 m²), (2) the fact that garden value varies massively by privacy/sun/noise/maintenance, and (3) the “cellar as hobby/atelier” angle needs clarity on legal use, dampness, and ongoing costs. Make the offer contingent on reviewing the building docs (condominium agreement, minutes, reserve fund, any planned works) and a quick moisture/ventilation check of the cellar.

Bottom line

2 killer features: courtyard quiet + a genuinely large private garden in 1070. But it’s still a 33.5 m² one-room — you’re buying a lifestyle, not flexibility. If the cellar is dry and the garden is truly private/usable, it’s a rare niche win; otherwise you’re overpaying for a story.

Family: ❌ (1 room + ground-floor / mixed-use feel) Investor: ➖ (quirky layout + garden is a plus, but resale/letting audience is narrower) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (if you want a creative studio vibe and actually value garden + workshop/storage)

🟡 NEGOTIATE — District 7 micro-location looks strong, but this is effectively a hybrid ground-floor studio with substantial basement area; price only makes sense if the basement is truly usable (dry, insulated, heated/ventilated) and the “unit type” fits your intended use.

This is not a conventional 1-bed apartment — it’s a creative-use unit with a rare garden angle. If the basement is genuinely high-quality usable space, it’s a compelling live/work setup. Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — sounds generally refreshed (common areas + façade + new balconies), but roof-floor condition, insulation/overheating, and any ongoing building works need confirmation.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (56 sqm can work for a couple + baby, but it’s tight and roof-floor quirks matter) Investor: ➖ (7th is liquid, but price-per-sqm is punchy and terrace/attic risk factors can cap returns) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, restaurant density, close to Mariahilfer/MQ vibe; likely great “city base”)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location is premium and the product is attractive, but at this ticket you want hard proof on terrace legality/ownership, heat management, and building financials before paying “new-build tax.”

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €420,000–€450,000 (comps unknown, so treat this as a negotiation anchor, not a valuation). Use: (1) missing key data (energy class, heating, floor specifics), (2) roof-floor thermal/maintenance risk, (3) any ambiguity on “huge roof terrace” rights (exclusive use vs. true ownership) and waterproofing warranty, (4) monthly costs vs. reserve fund transparency. If parking is pushed, treat it as a separate deal and discount the flat accordingly.

Bottom line

You’re buying walkability and 7th-district livability first; the apartment is the wrapper. Roof-floor + big terrace can be magic — or a maintenance/overheating headache. Pay up only if documents make the terrace and building economics bulletproof.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (3 rooms helps, but 56 sqm is tight long-term) Investor: ✔ (7th district liquidity + small-ish unit size tends to rent/sell easily, if layout is sensible) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, restaurant density, near major retail/culture; courtyard garden/balcony is the big quality-of-life lever)

Final verdict

You’re buying the 7th district lifestyle and resale safety — not space. The courtyard-side balcony + unusually large shared garden can be a real “daily happiness” upgrade if the unit faces quiet. If the unit is street-facing or the layout wastes sqm, the price becomes the problem fast.

Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking

  • Price: €480,000
  • Size: 63.35 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: individual heating

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — marketed as first move-in after renovation, but completion is stated for Feb 2026, so your timeline and the build/finish quality risk matter.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (great 7th-district lifestyle, but 63 sqm / 2-room mezzanine living is tight long-term) Investor: ➖ (top location, but mezzanine + delayed delivery can narrow tenant/buyer pool and raise risk/hold time) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, culture/food density, close to U3 and central districts)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — location is excellent and the concept sells, but mezzanine + delivery/finish uncertainty means you should only buy at a price that compensates for those risks.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €435k–€465k (best-effort; I don’t have verified comps for this exact micro-location/condition). Ask for: (1) a written renovation scope/spec sheet, (2) firm completion date + penalty/escrow mechanism if delayed, (3) clarity on what exactly is included (kitchen, fixtures, flooring, windows), (4) confirmation of noise situation (street vs courtyard) and any planned building works. Use mezzanine/resale liquidity + “photos are virtually staged” + long lead time to justify a discount.

Bottom line

You’re paying for the 7th-district lifestyle and walkability — and it delivers on that. The risk is not the neighborhood; it’s the mezzanine exposure + renovation execution and timing. If you can negotiate a real “risk-adjusted” price and lock the spec, it can be a smart buy; otherwise you’ll feel overpaying later.

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (assuming the “modern equipped / fitted kitchen” reflects current condition; verify windows, electrics, and any loggia waterproofing).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (works for a small family, but 62.1m² net can feel tight; 5th floor + lift helps) Investor: ➖ (strong area + transit, but 1960s stock + running costs mean yields may be merely okay) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (quiet courtyard orientation + south loggia in 1070 is a real quality-of-life combo)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — Great micro-location signals (walkable, transit-rich, quiet) but the pricing needs to be justified vs. true net m², building condition, and any upcoming works.

Negotiation strategy

Bottom line

You’re buying the 1070 lifestyle: dense restaurants (53 food/drink within 300m), real daily convenience (16 groceries), and excellent connectivity — while still getting courtyard calm. The south loggia is the differentiator; if it’s truly quiet and usable, it’s worth paying for. Make sure the “value story” isn’t just marketing around weighted area and a nice balcony.

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great area and classic building story, but the unbefristet tenancy + 2.15% headline yield means your upside is capped unless the tenancy economics are unusually favorable.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €460k–€490k (best-effort guess; true comps for *unbefristet rented* period building units vary a lot, so consider this a leverage anchor, not a valuation). Negotiation levers: (1) tenancy terms and rent regime (what exactly is the legal basis of the current rent, indexation, and tenant protections), (2) near-term capex risk in a 1900 building (roof/courtyard façade/stairwell works, reserves), (3) any ambiguity on the new courtyard balcony’s approvals/maintenance allocation.

Bottom line

You’re paying for “1070 + stability,” not for cashflow. This can be fine if you want a long-duration store of value — but you must be comfortable with tenant power and slow value realization. If the paperwork doesn’t clearly show controllable rent dynamics + healthy building reserves, the “safe investment” narrative breaks fast.

  • Price: €549,000
  • Size: 70 m2
  • Rooms: 2

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — too many blanks (floor, heating, energy class, running costs) to call it “plug-and-play.”

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms / 70 sqm can work for a couple + baby, but it’s not a long-term family layout) Investor: ➖ (7th is liquid, but 2-room at this ticket needs strong rentability + clear building costs) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (quiet + green outlook in 1070 is exactly the “Vienna city living without the chaos” pitch)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great macro/micro location signal, but price only works if the building, costs, and “green-view quietness” are genuinely exceptional.

Negotiation strategy

Start by forcing clarity: ask for floor level, orientation, courtyard vs street exposure, windows type, heating system, energy certificate, monthly running costs (BK + reserve), and any planned building works. Target price range (best-effort guess): €510,000–€535,000 (comps not provided, so treat as a negotiation anchor; adjust after seeing running costs + condition).

Bottom line

1070 + “quiet oasis” is a premium combo — if it’s real, it’s worth paying for. But missing cost/energy/building details is where buyers get burned. Make the seller prove the quiet + the greenscape + the building health, then price it accordingly.

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (fresh 2024 renovation; assume only minor snag-list items).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (77 sqm / 2 rooms can work for a couple + baby, but it’s not “family-forward” long-term) Investor: ➖ (great rental appeal, but price per sqm + energy metrics may cap yield) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (classic Vienna old-building feel + high ceiling + walkable cafe density)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong “period building + turnkey + lift” combo in a prime inner area, but you should price in energy performance risk and verify micro-location (listing text reads more like 8th).

Negotiation strategy

Start with an inspection-led offer and anchor on (1) energy performance (HWB D / overall efficiency E stated), (2) any building/common-area capex despite a repair fund, and (3) the district/address ambiguity (text says 8th; address field says 7th). Bottom line

Turnkey, high-ceiling Gruenderzeit apartment in a very “liveable Vienna” pocket—this is the kind of home you enjoy daily. The main watch-outs aren’t inside the unit; they’re building economics (energy/capex) and paying full retail for a renovation story. If the building checks out and the layout is truly central-begehbar (not awkward), it’s a solid buy at the right number.

Family: ➖ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ✔

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (assuming the kitchen install is completed as advertised).

Buyer fit

Family: ✔ (3 rooms + outdoor space is rare this central) Investor: ➖ (great liquidity in 7th, but garden units can be more niche and yields may be tight at this price) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (new-build vibe + food density + private outdoor space)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong location + rare garden, but price per m² and “freshly renovated” premium need to be justified with building context and quietness.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €525k–€555k (comps unknown, so treat this as a leverage range, not a valuation). Push on (1) ground-floor trade-offs (light, privacy, security), (2) any upcoming building works/reserves, (3) exact garden/terrace legal status and maintenance obligations, (4) proof of renovation scope (invoices, warranties, window spec, electrical/plumbing sign-off). If they won’t move on price, ask for seller-paid lawyer/notary contribution or guaranteed garage spot allocation (if possible).

Bottom line

If you want “city living but I can step outside with coffee” in the 7th, this is a compelling setup. Your main risks are not inside the apartment — they’re building quality, noise/light on Neustift Lane, and whether the outdoor area is truly as usable as it sounds. Negotiate like a ground-floor buyer: you’re paying a top-district price, so the trade-offs must be compensated.

  • Size: 78.78 m2
  • Rooms: 4
  • Outdoor space: terrasse
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 7

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — interior sounds “turnkey” (renovated, underfloor heating), but building works (planned attic conversion + promised lift) can mean months of noise/dust and schedule slippage.

Buyer fit

Family: ✔ (4 rooms on ~79 sqm is a strong, efficient layout if the rooms are real bedrooms) Investor: ➖ (great district + “first occupancy” vibe, but renovation/promise-based elements + symbolic photos add diligence risk) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (7th district energy + lots of food/drink + hofseitige outdoor space is the sweet spot)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — compelling micro-location and spec, but you’re paying for “new” inside an old shell and the listing itself flags uncertainties (symbol photos, lift tied to future build-out).

Negotiation strategy

Anchor hard on uncertainty: (1) “symbol photos” (you’re not buying the photos), (2) lift is only “promised” in the context of a future attic project, (3) clarify what exactly is renovated in the unit vs. common parts, (4) confirm legal status/permits/timeline for DG works and who bears what costs. Target price range (best-effort guess): €545,000–€570,000. I don’t have direct comps here; if recent new-build/1070 renovated-period building comps support the ask, expect the counter to stick closer to €575k–€590k.

Bottom line

You’re buying the 7th district lifestyle with a modern interior package. The main risk isn’t the apartment — it’s the building phase around it (DG expansion, lift delivery, disruption). If the outdoor space is truly quiet/hofseitig and the rooms have good proportions/light, it’s a strong “live here” play at this size.

stylish period building! Ruhige, hofseitige 2-rooms Altbauwohnung in der Westbahn Street! (STILALTBAU! Ruhige, hofseitige 2-Zimmer Altbauwohnung in der Westbahnstraße!)
€595,000 · 74.49 m2 · 2 rooms · Vienna, 07. District, Neubau
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View

  • Size: 74.49 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 7

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (used sale, described as good/ready state; still treat as “depends” until you see windows/heating + electrics).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms + 74.5 sqm can work for a small household, but it’s fundamentally a couple/single layout) Investor: ➖ (great micro-location + quiet courtyard, but “ownership in formation” adds process risk; yield vs ~€8k/sqm likely tight) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (7th district vibe + hofseitig quiet is a rare combo)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — excellent 1070 micro-area and quiet courtyard orientation, but mezzanine/low floor plus gas floor heating and “WE in formation” should translate into a meaningful discount.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €535k–€565k (best-effort guess; I don’t have verified building comps for Westbahn Street/1070, so treat this as a negotiating anchor, not a valuation). Push hardest on: mezzanine (light/privacy), gas boiler age/service, window performance (old-style box windows can be charming but drafty/noisy depending on condition), and the extra friction/uncertainty of ownership being established.

Bottom line

Quiet courtyard period building in prime 1070 is the headline. If the unit is genuinely calm, bright enough, and the boiler/windows check out, it’s a strong live-in buy. Don’t overpay for “style” — make the building/admin/legal setup earn its keep in price.

Final verdict

Bottom line

If you want a liveable, central 7th-base with light from both sides and outdoor space: this checks key boxes. The deal hinges on renovation reality + building health (reserve/repairs) — get that clarity, then push price. At €599k it needs to feel either freshly updated or exceptionally quiet/green-facing to be a clean “buy now.”

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — marketed as first occupancy after renovation, but it’s still mid-reno, so final finish quality + handover scope matter.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (4 rooms on 71 sqm can work, but expect tight bedroom sizes + storage compromises) Investor: ➖ (1070 is liquid, but price-per-sqm is punchy; return depends on exact micro-location + finished spec) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (prime 7th-district energy, walk-everywhere, courtyard balcony/garden upside)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great district + “done-for-you” renovation can be worth paying for, but 71 sqm/4 rooms and first-floor realities mean you want price discipline.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort guess; comps not verified): €550 000–€580 000. Anchor on uncertainty: renovation completion risk, exact room sizes/layout efficiency, sound (street vs courtyard), and whether there’s a lift. Ask for a detailed finish spec sheet + warranty + energy/heat info + floorplan with dimensions. Target price range: EUR 545,000–575,000 (best-effort guess; I don’t have direct comps for Halb Lane in this finish level, so treat as a negotiation anchor, not a valuation).

Bottom line

This is “buy the location” in 1070 with a modernized interior — a strong lifestyle play. The trap is overpaying for a renovation while ending up with small rooms and limited storage. If the balcony is truly quiet courtyard-facing and the finish is premium, it becomes a very defensible long-term hold.

NFL - Klima - Terrasse und Eigengarten - hochwertige Ausstattung - Neubauprojekt)
€649,000 · 62.77 m2 · 2 rooms · Seidengasse 19, Vienna, 07. District, Neubau
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €649,000
  • Size: 62.77 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: district heating
  • District: Vienna 7

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — “near completion” usually means you’re close, but expect snagging, handover timelines, and some finishing dust.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (big outdoor space helps, but 2 rooms is tight long-term) Investor: ➖ (great 1070 liquidity, but garden/terrace + premium spec often caps yield) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (courtyard apartment + garden in 1070 is a rare quality-of-life combo)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the product is attractive (garden, AC, new build, district 7), but at €649k for ~63 sqm you’re paying peak-premium; make the developer “earn it” with terms and extras.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €610k–€630k (best-effort guess; I don’t have direct comps for this micro-location/new-build stack, so treat this as a starting anchor, not a valuation). Push points: confirm weighted vs actual area math; written completion/hand-over date with penalties; snagging protocol; what exactly is included (AC units, shading, kitchen, flooring upgrades); garden/terrace waterproofing + maintenance responsibilities; storage/bike room; and get a clean breakdown of monthly operating costs + reserve fund.

Bottom line

This is “quiet courtyard living” in one of Vienna’s most walkable, restaurant-dense neighborhoods — a lifestyle asset. The outdoor space is the headline, but the trade-off is you’re buying a premium unit type where negotiation is about terms/spec as much as price. If you’re sensitive to light, humidity, or privacy: courtyard + ground-floor garden units can be amazing or annoying — verify on-site.

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (vacant + furnished), but expect “EG/1955” to hide a few building-level gotchas.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms/51 sqm works for a couple + baby, but ground-floor + station-zone lifestyle isn’t very kid-first) Investor: ✔ (layout + separate kitchen + near Hauptbahnhof is rentable, if noise/EG risk is priced in) Lifestyle buyer: ➖ (great connectivity; trade-off is street energy/noise and less “Vienna charm” feel)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location is extremely liquid, but ground-floor + 1955 building means you only buy this if the price correctly reflects noise/thermal/condensation and building-reserve realities.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €205k–€225k (best-effort guess; comps not provided, so treat this as a risk-adjusted starting band, not a valuation). Ask for concessions tied to verifiable uncertainties: (1) building reserve status + planned works (roof/façade/lift/lines), (2) documented noise situation (orientation to tram/arterial, window spec), (3) any humidity/mold history typical for EG units.

Bottom line

This is a “buy the network node” apartment: Hauptbahnhof access is the product. If it’s quiet enough and the building finances are clean, it’s a solid small-unit hold. If it faces the wrong side and the house is gearing up for big capex, your yield and quality-of-life get crushed fast.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (sounds like small city units; rooms/sqm not provided) Investor: ✔ (prime 4th district + “free rent” positioning + garage option can support liquidity) Lifestyle buyer: ➖ (great central living, but likely compact + urban noise/traffic trade-offs)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the micro-location is excellent (dense amenities/transit), but the listing is light on the hard deal terms (exact size, floor, running costs, energy, layout), so price discipline matters.

Negotiation strategy

Your main risk isn’t the neighborhood — it’s overpaying without knowing the unit’s exact size, floor, and monthly carrying costs. Treat it like a numbers-first buy: verify the fundamentals, then move fast.

#105
Schaeffer Lane 18-20 - Wohnen mitten im Vierten (Schäffergasse 18-20 - Wohnen mitten im Vierten)
€270,000 · Schaeffergasse 18-20, Vienna, 04. District, Wieden
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €270,000
  • Heating: central heating
  • District: Vienna 4

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — this reads like a new-build project page, but without a specific unit (rooms/sqm/floor) you can’t judge fit, light, or noise.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (great location, but unit specifics + outdoor space unknown) Investor: ✔ (4th district demand + strong amenity/transit density, but watch micro-noise and unit liquidity) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, restaurant-heavy, central, very “live-in-the-city”)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — prime central micro-area and likely solid resale/rental demand, but you’re currently buying a brochure: missing unit data is where bad deals hide.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €255k–€270k (best-effort guess; comps unknown from the provided data, so treat this as a starting bracket). Key lever: make the offer conditional on clarifying the exact unit + documented running costs (monthly operating costs/reserves) + building completion/hand-over terms. Two ways this goes wrong: (1) street-facing orientation + central living = late-night noise you can’t “renovate away”; (2) new-build contract details (extras, handover quality, timelines) can inflate your all-in price and delay move-in.

Bottom line

You’re buying the 4th district lifestyle: dense food options, groceries, parks, and transit within a short walk. Make it a unit-first decision: floor/orientation and true monthly costs will decide whether this is a gem or an overpriced city-facing box. If the unit is quiet and bright, it’s hard to beat this pocket for everyday living without tourist overload.

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (freshened building + “sofort beziehbar”; kitchen likely included, but verify exact inventory/spec).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (1-room / ~35 sqm) Investor: ➖ (strong micro-location demand, but small-unit pricing + free rent setting can cut both ways) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (quiet courtyard-side studio in a prime inner-city pocket)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great Wieden / TU / U1 positioning and “hofseitig” is a real perk, but studio pricing only works if layout, light, and building costs check out.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €245k–€260k (best-effort guess; I don’t have verified comps for this exact micro-street/building). Anchor on (1) studio liquidity risk, (2) cost uncertainty post-modernisation, and (3) any daylight/noise compromises even if courtyard-facing. If reserves are thin or costs are about to step up, that’s a clean, factual price lever.

Bottom line

You’re buying the micro-area: Naschmarkt/Karls Square orbit + U1 + TU is a demand machine. Courtyard-facing is the stealth value here—if it’s genuinely quiet and gets decent light, this becomes an easy “city base” apartment. The risk is paying “new-build premium” for a tiny unit where resale depends heavily on feel (light, ceiling height, corridor loss, window orientation).

  • Price: €306,730
  • Size: 48.76 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Lift: Yes (as stated)
  • District: Vienna 4

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (looks turnkey on paper), but “depends” until you verify roof/insulation + building condition and get an energy certificate.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Too small and the attic layout tends to be less kid-practical. Investor: ➖ Rentability could work on location, but attic/heat/energy unknowns add risk; check demand for 2-room units and HOA reserves. Lifestyle buyer: ✔ Quiet attic feel in a very central, well-connected pocket; great for a single/couple who wants city convenience without nightlife noise.

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — excellent micro-location and “quiet + bright” upside, but missing energy documentation and typical attic unknowns mean you should price the risk.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €285,000–€300,000 (best-effort guess; true comps not provided). Anchor on: (1) no energy certificate, (2) attic-specific heat/cooling + roof exposure, (3) 5th-floor attic premium only pays if summer comfort is proven, (4) verify building reserves and any planned works (roof, lift, facade). If they won’t move on price, push for documented upgrades/guarantees (roof waterproofing history, insulation notes, last major works) and a clean set of running-costs + reserve-fund statements.

Bottom line

You’re buying the location first: walk-everywhere centrality with strong transit and daily-life amenities. Attic apartments can feel special—until summer heat or roof maintenance shows up. Get the documents, test the comfort (top-floor temperature), then negotiate like a skeptic.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ✔ Lifestyle buyer: ➖

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location and smart layout, but the tenant-in-place until 2029 + tiny size caps both flexibility and resale audience.

Your downside is locked-in cashflow + limited exit routes; your upside is location scarcity and a clean, modern finish. If the rent isn’t compelling on paper, treat the price as the thing you’re buying — and push hard on it.

Zentraler wird's nicht. Spannender auch nicht (Zentraler wird's nicht. Spannender auch nicht)
€319,000 · 62.71 m2 · 2 rooms · Vienna, 04. District, Wieden
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €319,000
  • Size: 62.71 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Lift: Yes (as stated)
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 4

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — overall modernization needed; bathroom explicitly needs renovation, kitchen is already fitted.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ Investor: ✔ Lifestyle buyer: ➖

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — excellent centrality and courtyard orientation, but you’re buying a 1955 building unit with clear renovation scope (bathroom + general modernisation).

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €285k–€305k (best-effort; true comps unknown). Anchor hard on: (1) bathroom renovation, (2) “overall modernization needed” wording, (3) individual heating (check system age/efficiency), (4) missing energy class + any unknown monthly running costs. If the seller references a “Richtpreis €295k”, treat that as the real pricing signal and push to land at/under it unless the condition is better than described.

Bottom line

Central Wieden near Hauptbahnhof is a liquidity play: easy to live, easy to rent, easy to resell. Your risk is not the area — it’s renovation cost creep and building/technical unknowns. Win condition: buy at a price that already “pays for” the bathroom + a sensible refresh.

Move-in readiness

Depends — looks like a new-build project page, but the extracted data is missing unit specifics (size/rooms/floor/heating/energy).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ➖

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — treat this as “interesting if the exact unit + running costs check out,” not a blind buy off the project branding.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €310k–€335k for a €340k ask (best-effort guess; comps not provided, so this is an uncertainty-discount). Primary levers: (1) unknown monthly costs (Betriebskosten + reserve + any developer service fees), (2) unit orientation/noise risk on/near Laxenburger Street, (3) specs gap (sqm/rooms/floor) → you’re not pricing a unit yet, just a marketing page.

Bottom line

If the unit faces the street, noise/air quality can turn “new-build shine” into daily friction. If it’s courtyard-facing with solid windows + low fees, it can be a pragmatic buy — but the current data is too thin to greenlight. Don’t negotiate like it’s a finished apartment; negotiate like it’s uncertainty you’re absorbing.

#114
MODERNE City apartment IN TOPLAGE AM KARLS Square (MODERNE STADTWOHNUNG IN TOPLAGE AM KARLSPLATZ)
€369,000 · 44 m2 · Vienna, 04. District, Wieden
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €369,000
  • Size: 44 m2
  • District: Vienna 4

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — “modern” suggests turnkey finishes, but with missing basics (floor, room count, heating, energy class) you should assume there’s at least some unknown risk until you see the full exposé.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (44 sqm is typically a squeeze unless it’s an unusually efficient 2-room) Investor: ✔ (prime inner-city demand + transit; but watch for micro-layout and running costs) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Karl Square-level centrality; you’ll live on foot)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — location is doing the heavy lifting, but the price-per-sqm is punchy for 44 sqm unless the building + floor + light + noise situation are excellent.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort guess, comps unknown): €330k–€355k. Push on “unknowns” as leverage: room count/layout proof, floor + lift, courtyard vs street orientation (noise), heating type, energy certificate, and any upcoming building works. If it’s street-facing near Karls Square, make noise/air quality the central bargaining chip.

Bottom line

This is a “Vienna on easy mode” address: restaurants, groceries, parks, and transit density are off the charts. At 44 sqm you’re buying lifestyle + liquidity, not space — the layout and light decide whether it’s a gem or a fancy shoebox. If it’s quiet (courtyard) and bright, it’s compelling; if it’s loud/dark, don’t overpay just for the pin on the map.

Optimale apartment in guter Lage und saniertem Gebaeude! (Optimale Wohnung in guter Lage und saniertem Gebäude!)
€375,000 · 56.29 m2 · 2 rooms · Leibenfrostgasse 8, Vienna, 04. District, Wieden
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Address/map mismatch flagged (likely approximate pin).
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €375,000
  • Size: 56.29 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 4

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (modernized unit + renovated building common areas), but verify workmanship/spec list and what “partly renovated” means across the project.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms / ~56 sqm works for a couple + baby, tight for long-term) Investor: ✔ (liquid micro-location near Naschmarkt/Karls Square; strong tenant demand if the building is truly sorted) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, restaurant density, quick access to core; “city living” location)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — excellent 1040 micro-area and renovated building, but the ask (~€6.7k/sqm) needs to be justified by finish quality, house costs, heating setup, and any upcoming building works.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €350,000–€365,000 (comps not provided, so this is a best-effort bracket). Push on: (1) individual heating operating cost + any modernization needs, (2) unclear lift/floor/quietness (street corner), (3) “images from stock” / marketing vs actual fit-out, (4) any pending special assessments despite the renovated common areas. If they won’t move much, ask for tangible value: documented warranties, detailed completion list, or seller-paid closing friction (where possible).

Bottom line

You’re buying the location first: this pocket of 1040 is hard to beat for daily life and resale liquidity. The building sounds “on the right track” (renovated façade/common areas), which is the big period building risk reducer. Make sure the apartment’s real condition matches the glossy story, and price it like a 56 sqm city flat—not a boutique showpiece unless it truly is one.

#116
Exklusives Wohnen im revitalisierten Eckzinshaus - Leibenfrost Lane 8 (Exklusives Wohnen im revitalisierten Eckzinshaus - Leibenfrostgasse 8)
€375,000 · Leibenfrostgasse, Vienna, 04. District, Wieden
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €375,000
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 4

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — “revitalized” can mean turnkey finishes, or “nice shell + buyer choices”; confirm what’s actually delivered for this specific unit.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (great centrality; but 4th district street noise + limited green inside the block can be a grind with kids) Investor: ➖ (liquidity is strong; but individual boiler + unknown monthly costs can cap yield and raise vacancy risk) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (high-walkability pocket with dense dining/transit and a classic corner-building vibe)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the micro-location looks excellent, but the listing is missing the key deal-definers (size/room count/floor/costs/energy), so price may be “from” rather than fair.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €340k–€365k (best-effort guess; comps are unknown from the data provided). Use one hard lever: individual heating typically means an in-unit boiler—ask for boiler age/spec, maintenance history, chimney/flue compliance, and projected replacement cost; if unclear, price in a near-term replacement and push the range downward. Two ways this goes wrong: (1) “Revitalized” hides thin sound insulation and street-facing bedrooms in a corner building (sleep quality + resale hit). (2) Building economics surprise you: reserves low, upcoming façade/roof/lift works, or short-term rental restrictions/HOA rules that limit flexibility.

Bottom line

You’re buying the location first here: dense amenities + transit in 1040 is a real quality-of-life asset. But don’t pay “showroom pricing” until you know the unit’s floor/orientation, the total monthly carrying costs, and what exactly was renovated versus merely refreshed.

Move-in readiness

Depends — “first occupancy” suggests turnkey, but confirm renovation scope, permits, and final finish quality.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (38.44 sqm, 2 rooms) Investor: ➖ (great location but small-unit pricing risk; check rentability + fees) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (central, green courtyard view vibe, near Belvedere)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — location is strong, but the price per sqm is punchy and the listing is light on technical/operating details.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €350k–€365k (best-effort guess; comps not provided). Bottom line

You’re buying the micro-location and the classic building feel — not space. Great as a city base or high-quality pied-à-terre; mediocre as a value play if the building/fees surprise you. Do the boring due diligence hard: building condition, heating system age, and upcoming common-area works decide whether this is a gem or a trap.

€395,000 · 46.48 m2 · 2 rooms · Schoenburgstrasse 5, Vienna, 04. District, Wieden
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €395,000
  • Size: 46.48 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 4

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (modern spec package; depends mainly on final handover quality and snagging).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (46.5 m², 2-room compact) Investor: ➖ (strong location + new-build features, but pricing likely tight vs achievable rent) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (central, polished, efficient, “set-and-forget” building tech)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — excellent central 4th-district micro-area and high-end comfort features, but you’re paying a premium per m² for compact size and “developer-new” pricing.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €360,000–€385,000 (best-effort guess; I don’t have confirmed close-by comps from this dataset, so treat as a negotiation anchor, not a valuation). Push on: (1) unit size/podest-as-space trade-off, (2) floor/position uncertainty (Top 2A often means low floor—noise/privacy matters), (3) any construction timeline risk, (4) smart-home/cooling as nice-to-have rather than value-per-m². Ask for: exact floor + courtyard/street orientation, window exposure, and a written list of what’s included (shading, smart controls, kitchen scope, fancoils vs ceiling cooling details).

Bottom line

You’re buying comfort engineering (cooling + underfloor heat + triple glazing + shading) in a genuinely convenient 4th-district spot. The question is not “is it good?”—it’s “is it worth ~€8.5k/m² for 46 m² and likely a lower-floor position?” If orientation is quiet and light is good, it’s a high-quality city base; if street-facing/low-floor, negotiate hard or pass.

€397,027 · 66.27 m2 · 2 rooms · Weyringergasse, Vienna, 04. District, Wieden
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €397,027
  • Size: 66.27 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Lift: Yes (as stated)
  • District: Vienna 4

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (kitchen + full bath in place), but attic units always warrant extra diligence on roof/building reserves.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms / 66 sqm works for a couple + baby, but tight long-term) Investor: ➖ (good transit + amenities, but attic-specific capex risk and price/sqm matters) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (quiet street feel + skyline view + walkable, transit-rich core)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — location and daily livability are strong; make the deal contingent on roof/building documentation and a price that reflects attic risk.

Negotiation strategy

Ask for: (1) building reserve fund balance + last 3 years of building accounts, (2) any roof/elevator works planned, (3) attic conversion permits/approvals and warranties (if applicable), (4) heating system details and running costs, (5) a clear statement on the broker’s conflict/dual representation. Target price range: ~€365k–€385k (comps not provided; this is a risk-adjusted anchor vs ~€6.0k/sqm ask). If documentation is pristine and the finish is genuinely high-end, be ready to move toward €385k–€395k.

Bottom line

You’re paying for “quiet, central, views, and U1 access.” The only thing that can turn this into a regret is hidden building/roof spend. If the paperwork is clean, it’s a very livable 4th-district home base.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (implied “first occupancy after renovation”), but budget for final snag fixes + verify building works if balcony addition is planned.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (40 sqm / 2 rooms is tight long-term) Investor: ➖ (great location + liquidity, but small-unit price-per-sqm risk) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (central, courtyard-facing calm, strong walkability)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — prime 1040 micro-area and courtyard view are real, but the price per sqm is punchy for a 40 sqm unit and “balcony possible” can be a long/uncertain process.

Negotiation strategy

Anchor on small-unit premium limits + individual heating operating costs + unknown floor/energy data. Target price range (best-effort guess; comps not provided): €360k–€385k. If they won’t move on price, push for seller-paid remediation/finishing items, documented renovation scope, and clear written confirmation on balcony feasibility (house consent + permits + estimated costs/timeline).

Bottom line

This is a classic “buy the location, not the size” Wieden play. Courtyard view helps livability a lot in the inner districts. The swing factor is whether the renovation quality is truly top-tier and whether the balcony idea is realistic, not just marketing fluff.

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the address is strong, but the extracted data is too thin to justify paying full new-build premium blindly.

District 4 near the center is rarely a “bad” place to own — the question is whether this specific unit is quiet, bright, and efficiently planned. The OSM snapshot hints at a very livable, transit-rich pocket with green nearby; downside risk is traffic/noise and overpaying for a marketing shell. Don’t proceed without the unit’s orientation + floor + exact sqm; those three decide 80% of Vienna day-to-day happiness.

Depends — likely fine for a quick move-in, but “floor/orientation + heating details” are big unknowns that can flip comfort and running costs.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (good size for a couple + baby, but 2 rooms can feel tight long-term) Investor: ✔ (strong central demand profile; watch tenant/heating/capex risks) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (dense amenities + transit nearby; walkable city living)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — the micro-location looks excellent, but the missing basics (floor, lift/no lift, noise/orientation, energy performance) are exactly what should buy you a discount.

Negotiation strategy

You’re paying for centrality: lots of food/drink, parks, groceries, and transit in a short walk. Two ways this deal goes wrong: you buy into a noisy street-facing unit, or you get surprised by heating/energy costs + building capex that weren’t priced in. If the unit is quiet (courtyard-facing) and the building finances are clean, this is the kind of place you’ll resell easily.

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (assuming the 2005 renovation still presents well; verify gas heating, windows, and bathroom wear).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms and the layout reads more “couple/solo” than family-friendly) Investor: ✔ (strong rental-demand pocket near Hbf/Belvedere; but tenant appeal depends on quiet-courtyard proof + floor/light) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (central, walkable, parks nearby; good “city base”)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — location is a real plus and the building seems maintained, but price per sqm is punchy for an older-newbuild (1961) and the listing leaves key value drivers (exact floor/light/courtyard exposure) vague.

Negotiation strategy

Bottom line

2-room, 78 sqm in Wieden near Hbf/Belvedere is a fundamentally liquid product. Your upside is the quiet courtyard angle + big-ish size for 2 rooms. Your downside is paying “premium-newbuild money” without clarity on light, exact micro-position, and gas-heating trajectory.

Family: ➖ Investor: ✔ Lifestyle buyer: ✔

Price-per-sqm is not cheap for the trade-offs, so your edge must come from negotiation or unusually strong rental economics.

Move-in readiness

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (4 rooms helps; but likely noise/logistics near Hauptbahnhof + renovation effort) Investor: ➖ (location rents well, but capex + gas individual heating risk/discount pressure) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (period building character, big windows, central 4th; perfect if you want to craft your own look)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong district and layout potential, but “vision project” + gas individual heating + unknown condition should buy you a meaningful discount.

Negotiation strategy

Ask for full disclosure: recent building works, Ruecklage, planned façade/roof/lift works, and any gas/heating-related upgrades. Push a price haircut for renovation uncertainty and heating risk. If it’s truly turnkey inside, expect the seller to defend closer to ask.

Bottom line

You’re buying location + volume + period building vibe, not finishes. If you’re renovation-tolerant, this can become a standout home in the 4th. If you need plug-and-play or low operating-risk heating, keep looking or price it accordingly.

  • Size: 103.92 m2
  • Rooms: 4
  • Outdoor space: garten
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 4

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — it reads like a solid shell (light, high ceilings, classic details) but likely needs modernization and careful budget control.

Buyer fit

Family: ✔ (4 rooms + parks/schools nearby, but check noise/traffic and layout privacy) Investor: ➖ (good location helps, but renovation risk + gas heating optics can bite; run numbers) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Wieden/Belvedere/Hbf proximity, lots of cafés/transit; great “city base”)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong micro-location and size-per-euro for 1040, but you’re probably buying a renovation project and the unknowns should be priced in.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €440,000–€470,000 (best-effort guess; no comps provided). Anchor on: likely renovation scope for a 1900 building, gas floor heating (future-proofing), missing monthly running costs/energy info, and any building-level CapEx (roof/façade/risers). Make the offer conditional on: (1) viewing + moisture check, (2) electrical/plumbing status, (3) clear confirmation of lift status + basement storage, (4) full operating costs + reserve fund.

Bottom line

You’re paying for location + big period building volume, not for finishes. If the layout is efficient and the building is financially healthy, this can become a high-quality long-term home. If the “handwork” is more than cosmetic, the deal only works at a discount.

Move-in readiness

Depends — the title claims modern fit-out, but the core facts (floor, heating, energy class, building condition) are missing, so treat “move-in ready” as unverified.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (only 2 rooms / ~61 sqm; great for a couple + baby phase, tight for longer-term) Investor: ➖ (strong rental microlocation, but price/sqm likely premium; depends on building/energy + rent cap realities) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (4th district, very walkable, lots of food/drink + transit density)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — location looks legitimately strong, but the ask reads “urban premium”; without floor/light/noise + energy/heating details, you should price in unknowns.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €450k–€475k (best-effort guess; comps unknown). Bottom line

This is the classic “Wieden lifestyle premium” play: you’re paying for the neighborhood and walkability. If it’s high-floor or quiet courtyard-facing, it’s a very liveable 2-room. If it’s low-floor/street-facing, you should negotiate hard or walk.

Depends — likely fine on basics (central heating) but too many blanks (condition, floor, light, windows, energy) to call it confidently.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (2 rooms / ~61 sqm is tight unless it’s a couple-with-baby phase) Investor: ➖ (strong rental micro-location, but entry price €/sqm is punchy and operating/maintenance details are incomplete) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Freihausviertel/Wieden vibe + dense amenities + good transit suggests an easy, walkable daily life)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — excellent micro-area, but at ~€8.1k/sqm you should only pay up if the apartment is truly bright, quiet, and well-finished.

Negotiation strategy

Bottom line

You’re buying the location first — and this one is high-convenience, high-demand. At this price, it must “feel” premium inside (light, calm, layout, finishes) or you’re overpaying. If it faces a quiet courtyard and has good windows, it’s a strong lifestyle buy; if it’s street-facing/noisy/dim, walk away fast.

Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €507,000
  • Size: 81 m2
  • District: Vienna 4

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — livable, but budget/time for modernization (and the “inheritance + still furnished” situation) is the whole story.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (81 sqm / 3-room works, but you’ll want a clean renovation plan and noise-proofing assumptions verified) Investor: ➖ (location is strong; upside depends on renovation cost control + rentability/layout) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Wieden / Freihausviertel vibe + walkability is exactly the point)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location and classic layout, but you’re buying a renovation project and should be paid (in price) for uncertainty.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €470k–€490k (best-effort guess; I don’t have verified comps here). Anchor on modernization scope (electrics, plumbing, kitchen/bath, windows/insulation unknown), plus the inheritance-clearing friction. Push for either (a) an empty, fully cleared handover at seller cost, and/or (b) a meaningful price haircut tied to documented renovation quotes. Also: insist on building-level documents (reserve fund, recent works, planned façade/roof/lift projects, any known rising running costs) before you soften.

Bottom line

Two things are true at once: the area is a long-term winner, and the apartment is priced like it’s closer to “ready” than it sounds. If you want central Vienna life without tourist chaos, this is a strong bet — but only if the numbers after renovation still make sense. Treat it like a product: location premium in, renovation risk out.

Target price range (best-effort): €480k–€505k (comps unknown; this is a risk-adjusted range, not a market fact). Push hard on: proof of actual renovation scope (invoices/spec), roof/terrace/attic build-out approvals, building reserves + planned works, and summer comfort (overheating). If no lift / lots of stairs / weak insulation: demand a bigger cut.

Bottom line

You’re paying for Wieden + walkable city life — and that’s real value. But attic maisonettes are “love it or hate it”: amazing light, annoying heat/noise/stairs. If documents check out and comfort is good in July/August, it can be a killer lifestyle buy.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (District 4 is very livable, but fit depends heavily on unit size/layout and noise exposure) Investor: ➖ (new-build pricing can cap yields; upside hinges on exact unit, fees, and rentability) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, dense amenity + transit environment is the core value here)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — prime Wieden location vibes, but without sqm/rooms/floor/monthlies/energy you’re buying blind; only proceed once the unit economics and micro-noise story check out.

Negotiation strategy

Bottom line

Wieden + new-build/renovated-quarter positioning usually means “pay up for convenience.” The neighborhood stats (lots of food/drink + groceries + transit) scream daily-life quality, but also raise noise/foot-traffic risk. Get the exact unit sheet; this could be a great live-in buy or an overpriced trophy depending on sqm, floor, and orientation.

Move-in readiness

Depends — described as “well-kept” with modern finishes, but key due-diligence items (energy class, exact floor, building condition) are missing.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (63 sqm / 2 rooms can work for a couple + baby, but it’s tight long-term) Investor: ✔ (Wieden demand + strong transit + low-ish listed running costs; check rentability and any restrictions) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (very central, dense amenities, easy car-free living)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great district + walkability, but the listing reads salesy and has pricing inconsistencies; verify what you’re actually buying.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €500k–€525k (best-effort guess; no comps provided). Start by anchoring on (1) price mismatch in the text (€529k title vs €580k description), (2) missing energy performance data, (3) unclear “2 apartments” wording, and (4) unknown floor/sunlight/noise exposure. Make your offer conditional on clean documents (operating costs breakdown, reserve fund, minutes, energy cert, legal status).

Bottom line

Wieden is a high-convenience, high-liquidity neighborhood — you’ll use the city like your living room. The risk is not the location; it’s the ambiguity: what’s included, what’s the building like, and why the numbers don’t match. If the paperwork checks out, this is the kind of unit that stays easy to sell later.

Move-in readiness

Depends — marketed like a new-build attic project with decent specs (underfloor heating), but the listing data here is missing the one thing that decides readiness: the exact unit, size, and handover status.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (attic living can be great, but without sqm/room count and lift/floor info it’s a gamble) Investor: ➖ (District 4 is liquid, but unit economics depend on layout + total costs + energy/operating costs) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Wieden + high walkability amenities nearby is a strong “live here” pitch)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — location and “top-floor/new-build” story can be excellent, but at €529k you should not pay a premium until the exact unit details (sqm/rooms/floor/lift/terrace) are nailed down.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €495k–€520k (best-effort guess; true comps are unknown from the provided data). Use uncertainty as leverage: multiple units in the project + “new prices” messaging often means they’re actively testing demand. Ask for (1) the full price list of all 10 units, (2) total monthly running costs and reserve, (3) energy/technical spec sheet, then anchor below ask unless this unit is clearly the best one (largest terrace, best orientation, quietest).

Bottom line

Wieden is a high-conviction district for day-to-day living: groceries, transit, and food density look strong within a short walk. But this is a project ad, not a specific apartment: without sqm/rooms and floor/lift info, €529k is just a headline. Treat it like a shortlist candidate, not a “buy now”, until the concrete unit facts arrive.

Move-in readiness

Depends — marketed as “first occupancy / new build”, but delivery is stated for summer 2026, so your real move-in depends on build + handover timelines.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (47.6 sqm, 2 rooms, city-axis location) Investor: ➖ (strong micro-location demand, but price-per-sqm is punchy and delivery risk ties up capital) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (central 1040, high walk/transit density, loggia for “breathing space”)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — top location and new-build spec are attractive, but you’re paying a premium for small size + future delivery + unknown running costs.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €485k–€510k (best-effort guess; I don’t have verified comps for this exact project/unit). Main levers: (1) delivery timing risk (summer 2026 is not tomorrow), (2) missing hard numbers (final operating costs, reserves, energy), (3) “new price” implies room to move. Ask for: draft house rules + elevator/tech specs, building reserve plan (even if new), full cost sheet (BK estimate breakdown), and the exact orientation + loggia exposure.

Bottom line

This is a “pay for location + new-build comfort” micro-apartment on a major artery. It can be a great city base if it’s quiet enough and the building is executed well. If noise/orientation and costs check out, it’s a high-convenience lifestyle play — not a bargain.

Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €539,900
  • Size: 40.06 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Outdoor space: balkon
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 4

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — marketed as first occupancy, but completion is planned for end of 2025, so you’re buying a promise + specs more than a finished apartment.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (40 sqm / 2 rooms is tight long-term) Investor: ➖ (strong location + new build appeal, but pricing per sqm is aggressive and delivery risk exists) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (if you want a small, modern base near Belvedere/Hbf with a real balcony)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — excellent micro-location + new-build comfort, but the price per sqm is punchy and you carry timeline/spec risk until handover.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €495k–€520k (comps not provided, so this is a best-effort pressure test based on size, new-build premium, and delivery risk; adjust once you see true comparable €/sqm). Concrete lever: tie your offer to a verifiable uncertainty — ask for the binding building spec + what’s included (kitchen, shutters, AC prewire, bathroom grade), plus a delivery/penalty clause or compensation if handover slips. Two ways this deal can go wrong: (1) construction/permit delays push handover, trapping your capital and messing up your living plan; (2) west-facing balcony + street/rail/city noise (depending on tract) turns “nice outdoor space” into “can’t use it at night” — orientation to street vs courtyard matters.

Bottom line

You’re paying for: location convenience + new-build systems (floor heating/heat pump) + a big balcony relative to the unit. Make it worth it by negotiating on price and hardening the contract around delivery date, included finishes, and which side of the building you’re actually on. If the unit is on the street tract and you’re noise-sensitive, price needs to reflect that — otherwise hold out for a quieter line or courtyard stack.

#143
Urbanes Investment am Naschmarkt - Eigentumswohnung mit freiem Mietzins (Urbanes Investment am Naschmarkt - Eigentumswohnung mit freiem Mietzins)
€550,000 · 75.66 m2 · 2 rooms · Rechte Viennazeille, Vienna, 04. District, Wieden
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €550,000
  • Size: 75.66 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Lift: Yes (as stated)
  • Heating: Oelheizung
  • District: Vienna 4

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (on paper): designer fit-out + sensible 2-room layout; biggest unknown is building/services quality beyond the listing.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (great centrality, but 2 rooms + Naschmarkt intensity isn’t kid-first) Investor: ✔ (free-rent regime + ultra-liquid location; easy to tenant, easy to exit) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (if you want city energy, walkability, and instant culture/food)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — location is A+, but 1964 building + oil heating + “right on Wienzeile/Naschmarkt” noise risk means price must be sharp.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €505k–€535k (best-effort guess; I don’t have comps for exact micro-street/building right now). Ask for: (1) recent operating cost statement + reserve fund status, (2) lift/roof/facade renovation history, (3) windows spec + measured indoor noise, (4) proof/details of “free rent” status and any constraints, (5) confirmation of optional parking terms. Use oil heating + likely noise exposure as leverage; if courtyard rooms are quiet, push hard on value but insist on documentation.

Bottom line

You’re paying for one of Vienna’s most “always in demand” micro-locations: insane transit, restaurants, and resale liquidity. The trade-off is intensity: traffic/noise/air quality risk and a mid-century building with oil heating. At the right discount, it’s a strong hold; at full ask, it needs to be flawless on windows + building capex.

€550,000 · 75.66 m2 · 2 rooms · Rechte Viennazeille, Vienna, 04. District, Wieden
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €550,000
  • Size: 75.66 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Lift: Yes (as stated)
  • Heating: Oelheizung
  • District: Vienna 4

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (on paper): modern fit-out + functional 2-room layout; the real question is street noise vs. your tolerance.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (great connectivity, but it’s a loud/late-night micro-location and only 2 rooms) Investor: ✔ (free rent setting + A-location demand is the core story here) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (if you want “step outside into Vienna” and can live with Naschmarkt intensity)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — prime address and courtyard sleeping orientation are strong, but you’re paying for location and you inherit the trade-offs (noise, tourist/footfall, oil heating).

Negotiation strategy

Start by treating this as a “location premium” asset, then try to claw back price for the non-sexy bits: older (1960s) building economics, oil heating optics, and the fact that the living room faces the market corridor. Target price range: €510k–€535k (best-effort; I don’t have verified comps for this exact street/building, so validate with 2–3 recent 4th/1st/6th district closings for similar size + condition).

Bottom line

This is a high-demand micro-area with insane amenity density and transit. It will rent, and it will resell — but it won’t ever be “quiet Vienna.” Buy it for the city-at-your-door lifestyle or the rent flexibility, not for serenity.

#145
Smarte Terrassenwohnung im (Smarte Terrassenwohnung im Erstbezug)
€550,000 · 39.73 m2 · 2 rooms · Vienna, 04. District, Wieden
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Address/map mismatch flagged (likely approximate pin).
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €550,000
  • Size: 39.73 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Outdoor space: terrasse
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 4

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (first occupancy after renovation; modern systems/finishes implied).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (39.7 m² / 2 rooms is tight long-term) Investor: ➖ (great location + terrace demand, but small-layout liquidity and new-build pricing cap yield) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (big terrace + central Wieden + transit density)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — premium micro-location and rare ~29 m² terrace, but you’re paying “new + design story” pricing for a very small interior.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €510k–€535k (comps unknown from the provided data; range assumes you push back on terrace-premium + small sqm pricing). Levers: (1) ask for proof of building reserve and planned works post-renovation (old shell risk), (2) clarify cooling spec—“air-conditioned” can mean anything from a real split system to minimal provision; price it accordingly, (3) confirm orientation/noise exposure (near Hauptbahnhof/arterials can kill terrace usability), (4) parking is optional and pricey—use “we don’t need a space” as a clean concession trade.

Bottom line

This is a terrace-first apartment: the outdoor space is the product, the 39.7 m² interior is the compromise. Buy it if you’ll actually live on the terrace 6–8 months/year and want Wieden/Hbf mobility. Pass if you’re sensitive to noise or you need flexible resale to families (you won’t have it).
  • How this goes wrong #1: terrace ends up functionally loud/windy or shaded (orientation + rail/road noise), and the “wow factor” evaporates.
  • How this goes wrong #2: post-renovation surprises in an older structure (settling, moisture, common-area defects) drive special contributions despite the “first occupancy” vibe.

Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €550,000
  • District: Vienna 4

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — it reads like a new-build project, but we’re missing the specific unit details (size, floor, finish level, completion/hand-over timing).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (Wieden can work well, but suitability hinges on unit size/layout + quietness) Investor: ➖ (prime-ish location helps liquidity, but yield math is unknown without sqm/costs) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (high walkability + transit density screams “easy Vienna daily life”)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — District 4 is structurally strong, but with no sqm/rooms/floor/energy data you’re buying a brochure, not a unit; you need specifics before paying “new-build premium.”

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €515k–€545k (best-effort guess; comps are unknown because sqm/rooms are missing). Ask for (a) exact unit sqm + floor + orientation, (b) total monthly running costs + reserve, (c) energy/heat spec, and (d) a clear finish-spec list. Use any uncertainty (handover date, construction stage, optional upgrades, parking/storage costs) to push price or get concessions (upgrade package, storage, closing-cost contribution).

Bottom line

Wieden is a buyer-friendly base: dense transit, lots of daily-life amenities, and enough parks nearby to keep it livable. At €550k, the only question is “what exactly am I getting?” — without sqm/floor it could be a steal or wildly overpriced. Treat it as a location win with unit-level risk until the facts arrive.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (revitalized building + apartment reported as fully renovated).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (great quality, but 60 sqm / 2 rooms is tight long-term) Investor: ➖ (strong tenant appeal, but price/sqm likely rich; depends on rentability + balcony premium) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (quiet side wing + big balcony + classic old-building feel in 4th)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the product is excellent (quiet, renovated, balcony), but the ask price likely bakes in the “period building + big outdoor space” premium.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €500k–€530k (comps unknown; treat as an opening corridor, not a valuation). Use a two-track offer: (1) price reduction if reserves/upcoming works or higher running costs show up; (2) same price but seller covers specific fixes/confirmations (balcony waterproofing/warranty, full renovation documentation). Concrete lever: ask for building reserve level + any planned facade/roof/lift works post-2020/21 revitalization—if anything remains, that’s immediate bargaining power.

Bottom line

This is a “buy the vibe” apartment: elegant old-building bones + modern finish, with a rare-sized balcony. The main bet is not the unit—it’s whether the price is already peak, and whether the balcony/side-wing quiet holds up in real life. If the balcony is truly usable and the side wing is genuinely silent, it’s a very compelling Wieden lifestyle play.

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (positioned as completed/sanierung finished; modern spec list suggests turnkey).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (51 m² / 2 rooms is tight long-term; terraces help but don’t solve layout). Investor: ✔ (strong transit node near Hbf + 2-room size is rental-friendly; watch micro-noise). Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (4th district vibe + renovated “factory” character + two outdoor spaces).

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — attractive micro-location and spec, but the €/m² is premium and the Hbf-adjacent zone can be noisy/less “charming” than inner-Wieden streets.

Negotiation strategy

Ask for the exact terrace orientation (street/courtyard), noise exposure, and what “Smart Living” actually includes (controls, ventilation, shading). Target price range: €530k–€555k (best-effort; I don’t have verified recent comps for this exact project, so treat this as a negotiation anchor, not a valuation).

Bottom line

This is a sleek, high-spec 2-room in a highly connected pocket of Wieden—great for city living and renting. If either terrace faces a busy corridor, you’re paying luxury pricing for compromised calm; if both are quiet/courtyard, it’s a much cleaner buy. Budget mentally for the “all-in” (monthly costs + reserve + potential parking) before you fall in love with the marketing story.

Move-in ready (reads like a new-build/high-end spec with modern building tech).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms / ~52.6 m² is tight long-term) Investor: ➖ (premium price point; yield depends heavily on total running costs + rentability of a NE loggia unit) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (central, quiet courtyard orientation, “set-and-forget” comfort features)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong micro-location + modern comfort, but you’re paying a serious new-build premium for a small, north/east-facing outdoor space.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €535k–€565k (best-effort guess; without comps for this exact project/stack/floor, treat this as a negotiation anchor, not a valuation). Primary levers: (1) confirm the exact floor/stack and daylight quality; (2) clarify full monthly carrying costs (not just €137.68) and any reserve/fund contributions; (3) lock in what “smart heating/cooling / fan coils” means in real operating costs and noise.

Bottom line

This is a “premium small” Wieden apartment: you’re buying convenience, quiet, and building tech, not space. The deal goes wrong if the unit is darker/colder than expected (NE + courtyard) or if the real monthly costs jump once all components are disclosed. If the floor is low or the courtyard is tight, push hard on price—daylight and privacy are the whole game at this size.

Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)

Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €579,000
  • Size: 51.44 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Floor: ground floor
  • Outdoor space: balkon
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 4

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — it reads like a new-build/attic conversion with planned completion Summer 2026, so you’re buying timeline + execution risk, not keys.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (great centrality, but 51 sqm + attic/roof dynamics can be a grind with kids) Investor: ➖ (strong micro-location demand, but delivery/finish-risk and unclear running costs cap yields) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (quiet courtyard-facing balcony + walk/bike-to-center Wieden vibe)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — prime 4th district micro-area, but the ask is punchy for ~51 sqm and the off-plan/attic uncertainty should be priced in.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €535k–€565k (best-effort guess; without verified comps for similar new-build DG units on this street, confidence is medium-low). Anchor on (1) delivery risk + contractual milestones, (2) missing operating-cost clarity, (3) spec inconsistencies: listing says “individual heating” yet also promises heat pumps + underfloor heating. Concrete lever: request written confirmation of the final heating system, sound insulation spec, and cooling capacity; if any are “subject to change,” demand a price step-down or a retention/holdback until handover.

Bottom line

Wieden at this radius to Karls Square is hard to beat for daily life: transit density, groceries, parks, and real neighborhood energy. This deal wins if the unit is genuinely quiet (courtyard orientation) and the build quality lands. It goes wrong if roof/attic realities (heat, noise, maintenance, delays) are hand-waved in marketing and show up later in costs or comfort.

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (signals: “modern” positioning + underfloor heating), but verify building condition/renovation scope and any short-term cosmetic issues.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (2 rooms / ~51 sqm is tight for long-term family use) Investor: ➖ (location demand is strong, but the entry price per sqm is aggressive; returns depend on rentability + building fees) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, restaurant-dense, transit-rich micro-area; great “city living” footprint)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — prime central micro-location, but at ~€590k for ~51 sqm you’re paying a premium; make the deal hinge on building quality + noise + layout efficiency.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €520,000–€560,000 (best-effort guess; I don’t have direct comps for this exact street/building). Bottom line

If you want a compact, high-energy Vienna base with everything on foot, this is the right neighborhood. But you need it to be a “perfect 51 sqm” (quiet, smart layout, good light), otherwise the price feels like brag-tax. Treat it like a premium micro-apartment purchase: you’re buying location first, walls second.
