Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €598,000
  • Size: 88.58 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Outdoor space: balkon
  • Heating: Solar
  • District: Vienna 4

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — reads like a new-build / developer project with showcase photos and finish packages; verify what’s actually included for this exact unit.

Buyer fit

Family: ✔ (3 rooms, balcony/loggia access from bedrooms, new campus/parks nearby) Investor: ➖ (great transit demand, but new-build pricing + lots of similar units nearby can cap upside) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (fast Innenstadt access, walkable food scene, zero-car living is realistic here)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong “liveable city” location near Hbf with modern systems, but you’re paying developer-lifestyle premium and the micro-location can be noisier/more transient than classic Wieden.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €560k–€585k (best-effort; I don’t have direct comps for this exact project). Push on (1) specification certainty (which finish line is priced in), (2) timing/hand-over risk, and (3) orientation/noise. Ask for either a straight discount or developer concessions (upgrade package, storage, parking discount, closing cost support). Concrete lever tied to a verifiable uncertainty: get the building’s reserve/future operating cost assumptions + exact heating/cooling setup (district heating + PV + component activation) and the projected running costs for this unit in writing; if costs are higher than comparable stock, price needs to move.

Bottom line

You’re buying a convenience-forward, modern apartment in a transit supernode — great day-to-day, potentially mediocre “quiet Vienna charm.” Two ways this deal goes wrong: (1) you discover the unit faces a loud/active edge (traffic/rail/forecourt) and the balcony becomes decorative; (2) the marketed finishes/smart-home features are optional tiers and your all-in cost creeps up after signing. If the unit is high-floor, inward-facing, and the spec is locked, this is a very easy “move and live” play.

Move-in readiness

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (4th is great, but without size/room count this is a question mark) Investor: ➖ (location helps; underwriting is impossible without sqm + running costs) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Wieden + this micro-area density screams “walk-out-your-door Vienna living”)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the micro-location looks strong (groceries/parks/food/transit all close), but the absence of sqm/rooms/floor is a major pricing red flag at €599k until proven.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €540,000–€575,000 (best-effort guess; true comps are unknown because sqm/room count/floor/condition are not provided). If any of these come back “worse than implied” (low floor to street, no lift, high Betriebskosten, old windows/heating), push harder.

Bottom line

If the apartment is bright, well-laid-out, and quiet, this is the kind of 4th-district base you keep for years. But €599k is meaningless without sqm — you’re negotiating blind unless they disclose the fundamentals. Treat this as a location-led buy, not a “cute period building” impulse purchase.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (good size + courtyard bedroom, but tiny balcony and you’re near big-city arteries) Investor: ✔ (existing tenant + strong landmark location; just verify lease terms and costs) Lifestyle buyer: ➖ (Belvedere/Schwarzenberg vibe is premium, but you’re buying into traffic noise risk unless it’s truly courtyard-quiet)

Final verdict

Bottom line

You’re paying for “Vienna postcard adjacency” (Belvedere) with real urban convenience (Hbf nearby). The winning scenario is: mostly courtyard-facing rooms + quiet at night + clean building finances. If it’s street-loud, you should demand a meaningful discount — location doesn’t fix sleep.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (2 rooms, 67 sqm is tight for long-term family needs) Investor: ➖ (great micro-location demand, but at this price the yield may be thin unless rent potential is exceptional) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (central Wieden, walkable amenities, parks, and fast transit; good “live well” footprint)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong central 1040 + attractive DG/period building mix, but pricing is premium and you need to de-risk building/operating-cost details.

Negotiation strategy

Bottom line

Premium-feeling first-occupancy DG in a high-signal pocket of 1040: parks + restaurants + transit density all check out. At €599k for 67 sqm, you’re paying for location + “new + pretty”—make sure the building economics and DG details deserve that premium. If documents are clean and costs are reasonable, it’s a very livable city base; if not, you’ll find similar lifestyle utility nearby for less.

Terrassenwohnung mit Wintergarten - im Quartier Starhemberg (Terrassenwohnung mit Wintergarten - Erstbezug im Quartier Starhemberg)
€630,000 · 72.01 m2 · 3 rooms · Vienna, 04. District, Wieden
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Address/map mismatch flagged (likely approximate pin).
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €630,000
  • Size: 72.01 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Outdoor space: terrasse
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 4

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (newly renovated / first occupancy feel), but comfort depends on orientation + winter-garden heat management.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (works for a small family, but terrace size + likely city noise may limit “kid-friendly calm”) Investor: ➖ (strong rental area, but boutique project pricing + many similar new units can cap upside) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (great for someone who wants central, walkable, transit-heavy Vienna living)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — attractive micro-location and finish quality, but you’re paying for the “new + story + courtyard-factory” premium; push on price unless the orientation is exceptionally quiet.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €590k–€615k (best-effort guess; without true comps for this micro-project, treat as a starting anchor, not a guarantee). Key lever: clarify the building’s reserve fund status post-renovation and what elements are still “new risk” (façade/roof/courtyard glazing/garage systems); any uncertainty = price haircut or seller-paid closing costs. Also probe winter-garden summer overheating plan (shading/ventilation) and documented sound insulation—those are livability deal-breakers in this pocket.

Bottom line

If it’s quiet inside and the renovation is genuinely high-grade, this is a very livable 4th-district base. If you hear trains/arterial traffic or the winter garden turns into a sauna, you’ll resent the premium fast. Negotiate like a skeptic: you’re buying comfort, not just finishes.

€649,000 · 74 m2 · Vienna, 04. District, Wieden
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €649,000
  • Size: 74 m2
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 4

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (recent full renovation 2022–2023; finishes suggest turnkey).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (74m² + likely 1-bedroom layout reads tight for long-term family use) Investor: ➖ (great location liquidity, but attic + unknown running costs/energy cert add underwriting risk) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (bright top-floor vibe, central Wieden, strong walk-to-everything footprint)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the package is strong (1040 + renovation + micro-area amenities), but the price is “finished-product” money while key risk items (energy performance, top-floor/roof specifics, monthly costs) are not pinned down.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort guess, comps unknown): €610k–€635k. Anchor on (1) missing energy certificate, (2) individual heating operating cost uncertainty, (3) attic-specific risks (summer overheating, roof/common-part responsibility, noise/rain, any lift/top-floor access), and (4) unclear room count/floor info. Ask for Hausverwaltung pack: Betriebskosten + Ruecklage + planned works + roof history + chimney/heating service records.

Bottom line

If you want “central Vienna life” with a polished interior, this is exactly the kind of 1040 unit that stays liquid. But attic apartments are only a slam-dunk if the building/roof story is clean and the heat/cooling reality matches your tolerance. Don’t pay peak-price until you’ve de-risked energy, running costs, and top-floor comfort.

Move-in readiness

Needs work — marketed as “used / renovation-needed”; budget for a full refresh (surfaces, kitchen/bath, electrics check).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (good size/3 rooms + lift + quiet courtyard balcony, but renovation + only ~3 m² outdoor) Investor: ➖ (solid 4th district demand, but renovation scope + gas heating uncertainty for future exit) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (classic 1907 period building bones + balcony + park proximity; you can tailor the finish)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great Wieden/park-adjacent period building shell with lift and courtyard balcony, but “sanierungsbeduerftig” should materially move the price.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €590k–€625k (comps unknown; range assumes meaningful renovation). Push on renovation scope (bath/kitchen, windows servicing, floors), upcoming building works/reserve fund, and gas heating future-proofing; ask for last WEG minutes + Ruecklage + planned capex before bidding.

Bottom line

If you want “character + control”: this is a good canvas in a high-liquidity district. The lift + courtyard-facing east balcony are real quality-of-life wins. But you only win if the renovation risk is priced in — otherwise you’ll overpay for the privilege of doing works.

Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Address/map mismatch flagged (likely approximate pin).
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €660,000
  • Size: 70.11 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Outdoor space: balkon
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 4

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (first occupancy after renovation; modern spec + underfloor heating).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (3 rooms at ~70 m² works for 1 kid, but the compact inner-courtyard geometry and “urban-close” balcony feel may be a trade-off) Investor: ✔ (strong rental liquidity near Hbf/U1; 3-room layout is broadly rentable—watch micro-noise/overlook and all-in costs) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (industrial-heritage vibe, two balconies, restaurant/transit density—classic “city base” setup)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — Great location + fresh finish + two balconies, but the courtyard proximity/overlook risk and optional garage pricing mean you should push price and terms.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €610k–€635k (comps not provided, so this is a risk-adjusted bid based on size, district, and courtyard-facing trade-offs). Use these levers: (1) courtyard-facing “close balcony” / privacy value haircut, (2) 1st-floor positioning (often a discount vs higher floors), (3) push for extras instead of headline price if they resist (kitchen package, blinds, storage, closing cost contribution), (4) treat the parking space as a separate negotiation—do not overpay just to “complete the package.”

Bottom line

You’re buying “new-in-old”: charming industrial shell + modern internals. The real question is balcony quality (privacy/light) and day-to-day calm—not the brochure. If the courtyard feel works for you in person, it’s a very liquid 3-room in a high-demand pocket.

Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €666,000
  • Size: 70.36 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Floor: 4
  • Outdoor space: balkon
  • Heating: central heating
  • District: Vienna 4

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — the shell is positioned as premium/modern (post-2022 works, central HVAC/cooling), but exact unit condition/furnishing spec and handover status aren’t confirmed here.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (3 rooms at ~70 sqm can work for one child, but you’re paying heavily for location/service concept) Investor: ➖ (strong renter appeal near Belvedere, but no short-term rental and hotel-adjacent dynamics can cap upside) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (quiet central street + walkable parks/transit + “hotel services” is very on-brand for a low-friction city life)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location and modernized building concept, but price/sqm is steep and the hotel-serviced hybrid + recent redevelopment add uncertainty you should monetize.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €620k–€645k (best-effort guess; true comps for this exact project not provided). Push hardest on (1) documented operating costs forecast (BK + reserve + heating/cooling metering assumptions), (2) proof of post-2022 construction quality/defects liability, and (3) the exact separation rules vs the serviced-apartment/hotel tract. Negotiation lever tied to a verifiable uncertainty: request the latest building reserve status + planned works and a clear statement of how central cooling (fan coils + chiller) is maintained/renewed and how costs are allocated — if the numbers or maintenance plan look vague, that’s a discount argument.

Bottom line

You’re buying “Belvedere-adjacent convenience” more than square meters. If the unit is quiet, well-oriented, and the cost structure is predictable, it’s a strong lifestyle pick. If hotel adjacency bleeds into noise/traffic or the HVAC/cooling cost story is messy, this can age poorly as a resale.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — it’s being sold in a “design it after purchase” model, so timeline/quality hinge on the scope, specs, and execution discipline.

Buyer fit

Family: ✔ (4 rooms at ~93 sqm in District 4 is a real family footprint, but you must manage the renovation phase) Investor: ➖ (works if the capex plan is tight and exit/rent is strong; uncertainty risk if budgets drift) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (central, walkable, parks nearby; you can tailor layout/finishes)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great macro-location + 3rd-floor old-building upside, but pricing is a moving target because renovation is not fully baked into a fixed, fully-specified deliverable.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €620k–€655k (best-effort guess; I don’t have verified local comps or the full scope/spec list, so treat this as a negotiation anchor, not a valuation). Key lever: demand a fully itemized spec sheet (windows/glazing, floor build-up, plumbing/electrical scope, bathroom count, kitchen allowance, doors, sound insulation) and tie any “upgrade menu” to capped unit prices. Ask for: (1) written fixed-price contract with exclusions explicitly listed, (2) milestone-based payments, (3) completion date + penalties/retention, (4) documentation pack for the building (reserves, recent facade/roof/lift works, planned capex).

Bottom line

You’re buying location + a renovation project with a concierge wrapper. If you like choosing finishes and can tolerate build risk, it can be a strong Wieden home. If you need certainty, treat it like a construction contract first and an apartment second.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — “modern equipment” suggests low immediate capex, but maisonettes often hide wear points (stairs, bathrooms, roof/upper-level thermal comfort) that need a viewing to confirm.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (3 rooms/91 sqm can work, but maisonette stairs + inner-city noise/parking trade-offs) Investor: ➖ (good micro-location liquidity, but duplex layouts can narrow tenant/buyer pool; monthly costs matter) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (strong walkable 4th district vibe; restaurants + transit density is the selling point)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — compelling micro-area and size, but €690k for ~91 sqm hinges on building quality, upstairs comfort, and whether the duplex layout is genuinely “nice” versus awkward.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €635k–€675k (best-effort guess; I do not have comps for this exact street/building and that’s what should ultimately set the range). Push on (1) maisonette resale/tenant-pool discount, (2) any lift situation / stairs inside unit, (3) proof of recent refurb quality, and (4) monthly costs (€399.19) vs what they include.

Bottom line

You’re paying for Wieden walkability: food/drink density + transit suggests a daily-life win. Maisonette is either the “cool factor” or the deal-breaker — check layout flow, bedroom privacy, and summer heat upstairs. If the building and upper level feel premium/quiet, it’s a strong lifestyle buy; if not, don’t overpay just for the postcode.

WOHNEN MITTEN IM VIERTEN - Urbane Lebensqualitaet in Zentrumsnaehe - Grosszuegige Maisonette-apartment - Moderne Ausstattung (WOHNEN MITTEN IM VIERTEN - Urbane Lebensqualität in Zentrumsnähe - Großzügige Maisonette-Wohnung - Moderne Ausstattung)
€690,000 · 91.31 m2 · 3 rooms · Schaeffergasse 18-20, Vienna, 04. District, Wieden
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €690,000
  • Size: 91.31 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Heating: central heating
  • District: Vienna 4

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (modern fit-out, lift, central heating, A/C mentioned) — but validate roof/terrace-related maintenance typical for top-floor duplexes.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (3 rooms / 91 sqm works, but maisonette stairs + top-floor living can be annoying with kids) Investor: ➖ (strong 4th district demand, but cashflow likely tight vs €399/mo running costs + financing; depends on achievable rent) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, restaurant-heavy micro-area; “city living without being on a loud main street” vibe)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — great location + modern duplex appeal, but you’re paying a premium per sqm and the riskiest part of the building (roof/top floor) without clear reserve/roof history in the extract.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort guess): €640k–€670k (comps not provided here, so treat this as a negotiating anchor, not a valuation). Key lever: ask for proof of building reserve level and any recent/planned roof, façade, and lift works; if reserve is thin or roof works are upcoming, push hard on price or seller credits.

Bottom line

This is a classic Wieden “walk-everywhere” play: dense amenities (food/drink count is huge) and quick links to U1/U4. Two ways it goes wrong: (1) top-floor heat/noise issues (street vs courtyard + A/C adequacy) and (2) surprise building costs if roof/lift/common areas need work. If orientation is quiet and the reserve is healthy, it’s a very livable inner-city home — but don’t overpay just because it’s shiny and central.

NEU - ERSTBEZUG nach RENOVIERUNG - 6/7. LIFTSTOCK - new-build 1991 (2017 genealrenoviert) - KLIMAANLAGE - FERNBLICK - GARAGENOPTION (NEU - ERSTBEZUG nach RENOVIERUNG - 6/7. LIFTSTOCK - NEUBAU 1991 (2017 genealrenoviert) - KLIMAANLAGE - FERNBLICK - GARAGENOPTION)
€690,000 · 91.31 m2 · 3 rooms · Vienna, 04. District, Wieden
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €690,000
  • Size: 91.31 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Floor: 5
  • Heating: district heating
  • District: Vienna 4

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready: renovated (2017–2020) with A/C and modern building systems; just verify what “ nach Renovierung” exactly includes (receipts, warranties, what was replaced).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (good size and two-bedroom layout, but high-floor maisonette living and likely limited outdoor space) Investor: ✔ (District 4, strong amenity density, “free rent setting / no MRG” claim—confirm in writing) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (high floor, green/distant views, walkable restaurant/cafe ecosystem, strong transit access)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — compelling micro-location + high-floor views + renovation story, but the price per m² is punchy and you must de-risk the legal/rental status and renovation quality.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort guess): €630k–€670k for the unit (comps unknown), using (1) lack of hard energy/cert data, (2) maisonette liquidity vs simple single-level 3-room, (3) monthly costs, and (4) any aging-building items (lift/garage/common areas) as leverage. If they push “no MRG / free rent,” demand written proof (and factor it into your ceiling only after confirmed). Garage option at €55k: treat as separate negotiation; don’t let it anchor the apartment price.

Bottom line

If you want central Vienna living without tourist chaos, this is the right kind of District 4 pocket: dense amenities, great walkability, and strong transit. You’re paying for the combo of height + renovation + A/C in a modern (1991) building—make sure the renovation is real substance, not just cosmetics. Great candidate if you value day-to-day city life and hate old-building maintenance surprises, but don’t overpay without comp support.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (works for 1 kid / compact 3-room; no outdoor space mentioned) Investor: ➖ (strong location, but higher €/m² + likely more tenant wear/turnover) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Freihausviertel/Naschmarkt energy, top-floor feel, AC, walkable everything)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — prime micro-location and a clean 1991 building, but you’re paying near-peak convenience pricing and need clarity on DG quirks (heat, roof, noise, maintenance).

Negotiation strategy

Move-in readiness

Needs work — clearly renovation-led, not a “paint-and-move” situation.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (space is great, but renovation time/cost + 3rd floor specifics matter) Investor: ✔ (strong location story; upside if the building and layout cooperate) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (prime central-ish pocket near big-city connectivity + parks)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — location is legitimately premium, but “sanierungsbeduerftig” + older building risk should be priced in harder.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €620k–€660k (comps not provided, so this is a risk-adjusted bid around renovation uncertainty, energy performance, and old-building surprises). Push on: renovation scope ambiguity, gas heating modernization risk, energy value (HWB ~110), and any lift/common-area condition. Ask for: last building works, reserve fund status, any planned façade/roof/stairs/lift works, and a clear legal/tech pack before you spend on planning.

Bottom line

2–3 big wins: top-tier micro-location, classic old-building bones (high ceilings/windows), and a layout that can become “forever home” quality. 2–3 big risks: renovation can balloon, old-building building-services surprises, and the final value depends heavily on execution quality. If you can stomach a renovation, this is the kind of address that stays liquid — but only if you don’t overpay up front.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Needs work — “sanierungsbeduerftig” + small unit means you’re buying a project, not a turnkey home.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Too small at ~35 sqm. Investor: ✔ If the building/legal setup allows rentals and the reno budget stays disciplined. Lifestyle buyer: ➖ Great central-ish 9th-district vibe, but only if you actually want a renovation journey.

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location signals are strong (parks + transit + daily amenities), but the renovation risk on a micro-apartment can erase the “cheap” headline fast.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €135k–€150k (comps unknown; this is a risk-adjusted range based on size + stated condition). Push hard on: full renovation scope, heating system age/condition (individual heating can mean future replacement cost), and any building-level works (roof/façade/risers). Use the small size to your advantage: per-sqm pricing can look “OK” while absolute euros still leave room to negotiate.

Bottom line

- You’re paying for Alsergrund’s convenience: lots of parks/transit and enough food/drink density to feel lively without being pure tourist churn. - The only way this is a win is if the renovation is predictable and the building isn’t about to hit you with major shared-capex. - Treat it like a controlled flip: fixed budget, fixed timeline, and a walk-away number.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ✔ Lifestyle buyer: ❌

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location is strong, but the “can’t view + indefinite tenancy + lift not usable + balcony usage caveats” stack is exactly where buyers overpay if they don’t price risk.

#172
Charmante 2-rooms-apartment in Top-Lage in der Naehe der metro Volksoper (Charmante 2-Zimmer-Wohnung in Top-Lage in der Nähe der U-Bahn Volksoper)
€179,000 · 40 m2 · Vienna, 09. District, Alsergrund
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €179,000
  • Size: 40 m2
  • Heating: Gasheizung
  • District: Vienna 9

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — the unit itself sounds recently refreshed, but the existing fixed-term tenant blocks owner-occupation until end-2029.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (too small + you can’t move in for years) Investor: ✔ (if you’re underwriting this as a long hold, not a yield play) Lifestyle buyer: ❌ (ground-floor + tenant + tiny “sleeping cabin” layout)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location in the 9th, but the rental situation (very low rent and long fixed term) dominates the economics and liquidity.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €140k–€160k (best-effort guess; true comps unknown). Anchor on (1) fixed-term tenancy through 2029, (2) extremely low stated rent vs ownership costs, and (3) limited viewing access reducing buyer pool. One concrete lever: demand documentary clarity on the tenancy terms (indexation, rent components, deposit, any side agreements) and building reserve position; price your offer explicitly conditional on those documents matching the ad.

Bottom line

This is basically a “buy the location now, use it later” ticket — not a cashflow investment. Two ways it goes wrong: the tenant situation becomes hard/slow to unwind at expiry, and ground-floor courtyard orientation still ends up being darker/noisier than expected despite being “quiet.” If you’re fine parking capital and want 9th-district exposure, push hard on price and paperwork before you fall in love with the story.

€220,000 · 58.98 m2 · 3 rooms · Vienna, 09. District, Alsergrund
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View

  • Size: 58.98 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Floor: ground floor
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 9

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Needs work — it’s explicitly renovation-needed and currently has no bathroom.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (3 rooms help, but EG + full renovation + bathroom build is a heavy lift) Investor: ➖ (could pencil out near AKH/UNI demand, but layout + EG + capex risk can kill yields) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (if you want a classic 1880 shell to redesign and you’re fine with ground-floor trade-offs)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong 9th-district micro-location and south-facing rooms, but the “no bathroom + full sani” risk means you only win if the price meaningfully compensates.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €180k–€205k (best-effort guess; true comps unknown from provided data). Anchor hard on (1) missing bathroom (not cosmetic), (2) ground-floor privacy/noise/security discount, (3) gas-therme replacement risk + full electrical/plumbing refresh typical in this condition. Concrete lever tied to a verifiable uncertainty: demand the building’s reserve fund + last owners’ meeting minutes + any planned works (facade/courtyard lines/risers). If reserves are thin or risers/roof/courtyard works are pending, that’s immediate €€€ justification for a lower price.

Bottom line

If you can execute a clean renovation and accept EG living, this is a classic “buy the location, rebuild the interior” play. Your downside is renovation surprises and a resale ceiling if the EG feel is compromised (noise, sightlines, damp). Make the deal about capex certainty: price it like a shell, not a home.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — “stylish old-build” + missing interior details (condition, windows, electrics, bath/kitchen) means you budget for surprises until proven otherwise.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms/52.7 sqm is tight; works only as a compact starter) Investor: ✔ (District 9 liquidity + strong amenity/transit density; likely easy to rent if layout is clean) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, restaurant-heavy micro-area; classic Vienna “live-outside-your-apartment” zone)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location is doing a lot of work here, but the ad is light on the facts that usually drive period building risk and renovation cost.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €215k–€230k (best-effort guess; comps not provided). Anchor on unknown monthly costs, unknown floor/lift, and individual heating running-cost/maintenance uncertainty; request a full condition list and building paperwork before you move your offer up.

Bottom line

District 9 with this amenity density is the “easy mode” part. The hard part is period building reality: noise, windows, electrics, moisture, and the building’s capex plan. If the layout is efficient and the building is well-run, this can be a smart small-city-base buy; if not, it becomes a renovation tax.

Move-in readiness

Depends — looks freshly refurbished, but the current office zoning is the real gating item.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (45 sqm, 2 rooms, split over 2 levels) Investor: ➖ (strong area + possible furnished/short-stay angle, but zoning/permits add risk) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (compact “city pad” in a prime, walkable 9th-district pocket)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location and “period building charm”, but ground-floor + energy performance + zoning uncertainty should be priced in.

Negotiation strategy

Start by treating it as a high-risk/low-friction unit: push hard on (1) office zoning → ask for clear pathway, timeline, and written cost expectations for conversion; (2) ground-floor discount (noise/privacy/insurance perception); (3) energy value (HWB ~145, class D) and future heating/insulation costs. Target price range: €225k–€240k (comps unknown; adjust once you benchmark similar 40–55 sqm units in 1090 with residential zoning and comparable condition).

Bottom line

If you want a small, stylish base in 1090 with excellent walkability, this is interesting. If you need clean residential status and “boring” resale liquidity, the zoning is the big red flag. Buy it only if the legal/permit path is crisp — otherwise demand a meaningful discount.

Final verdict

If the courtyard/street orientation is quiet and the handover is contractually tight, it can be a great compact city base. Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong 1090/walkable node and a newly finished unit, but at ~€8.8k/sqm you’re paying “new inside” money for a 1900 shell + missing energy cert + tiny size.

For renting: likely very liquid. For living: you must love compact living and be okay with gas + potential period building quirks.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (likely minor cosmetic tweaks only).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (size is the limiter; works for a couple with a baby, not long-term family) Investor: ✔ (efficient 2-room + strong micro-location + likely easy to rent) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Servitenviertel vibes + Donaukanal proximity + walk-everywhere setup)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — Great 9th-district micro-location and practical layout, but 1970s building + top-floor/4th without clear orientation/light details means you should price the unknowns.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €275,000–€290,000 (best-effort guess; no direct comps provided). Push on: 1976 stock risk (pipes/electrics/common areas), monthly running costs + reserve, and any noise/heat exposure near the canal/arteries. Ask for recent building works, reserve status, elevator modernization, and any planned façade/roof/garage expenses.

Bottom line

Servitenviertel is a buyer-friendly micro-area: walkable, amenities-dense, and close to the 1st without paying 1st-district pricing. Layout sounds genuinely usable (separate WC, separate kitchen, two proper rooms) — that’s what keeps this liquid. Your leverage is the “unknowns” (orientation, noise, AC reality, building capex) — make the seller prove the story.

€319,000 · 48.2 m2 · 2 rooms · Vienna, 09. District, Alsergrund
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €319,000
  • Size: 48.2 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Floor: 3
  • Lift: Yes (as stated)
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 9

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (renovated, elevator, sensible 2-room flow) — but verify building-level items and gas heating details.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (48.2 sqm works for a couple + baby, but tight long-term) Investor: ✔ (U6/AKH proximity + 2-room layout tends to rent well; watch total monthly costs) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, park-heavy micro-area, quick transit, lots of daily amenities)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong location + fresh renovation, but U6 corridor trade-offs and period building building risk mean you want price discipline.

Negotiation strategy

List price implies ~€6.6k/sqm (319k / 48.2), which can be fair in the 9th depending on exact street noise, building condition, and renovation quality; comps are unknown from the provided data. Target price range: €290k–€310k (start lower if street-facing/U6 noise or if the building reserve/roof/façade work looks imminent; tighten toward €310k if it’s quiet, high-quality finish, and clean building finances). Use: (1) proof of renovation scope (invoices, warranties), (2) gas heating age/servicing, (3) building reserve + planned works, (4) window orientation/noise as your bargaining levers.

Bottom line

A classic “small period building, big location” play: easy daily life, strong connectivity, likely good liquidity. The only real enemies here are noise and future building capex — don’t buy blind on the building. If it’s quiet enough and the building is financially healthy, this is a solid, low-regret 9th-district starter home.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms / ~54 m² is workable for a couple + baby, but tight long-term) Investor: ✔ (9th district demand + transit/amenities density suggests resilient rental liquidity) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (park-view angle + “urban but quiet” is exactly the 9th district sweet spot)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location potential, but you should price the information risk (unknown floor/condition/energy).

Negotiation strategy

Bottom line

This is a “buy the location” 9th district play: parks + transit + food/groceries density screams livable and liquid. At €330k for ~54 m² it’s not crazy for 1090, but without floor/condition you’re paying blind. Proceed like a developer: verify noise/light/building capex first, then decide whether to pay up for the park-view premium.

Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View

  • Size: 36.33 m2

  • Floor: ground floor
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 9

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (reads like a high-spec renovation in a revitalized period building), but verify handover status + what’s actually included.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (36 sqm / 1 room is lifestyle-first, not a long-term family layout) Investor: ➖ (micro-location is strong, but entry price per sqm looks premium for a small unit) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (prime 1090 vibe, walkable, great transit, classic period building feel)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — top area, but this looks “developer-premium” pricing for a compact 1-room; buy only if the execution + building quality are genuinely A-tier.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort): €320k–€340k. I don’t have comps for this exact micro-street/building/finish level, so treat this as a negotiating anchor—not a valuation. Ask for: (1) full operating costs (Betriebskosten + repair reserve), (2) floor level + courtyard/street orientation + noise proofing, (3) what exactly was renovated (wiring/plumbing/windows), (4) lift status, (5) building-wide works history and remaining capex, (6) energy certificate + heating system details beyond “underfloor heating.”

Bottom line

You’re paying for “1090 + restored period building + shiny finish,” not for space. If the unit faces quiet courtyard and the renovation is truly end-to-end, it’s a lovely city base. If it’s street-facing or the building economics are unclear, this becomes an expensive studio with resale risk.

Depends — looks livable as-is, but electric night-storage heating + mixed flooring suggests you’ll likely want a systems/finish refresh.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ✔

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great 9th-district micro-location and classic period building upside, but pricing needs to reflect electric heating, missing energy certificate, and the “connected bedrooms” quirk.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €330k–€355k (best-effort guess; without verified comps this is primarily feature-risk pricing). Use (1) electric night-storage heating modernization cost + running-cost uncertainty, (2) absent energy certificate, (3) bedroom connectivity/resale friction, and (4) any upcoming building works (ask for reserve fund + planned renovations) as your leverage. Make your offer clean: quick financing proof, flexible closing, but price firm.

Bottom line

You’re buying location and “old Vienna” bones more than turnkey comfort. If the layout works for your day-to-day and you can budget a heating/efficiency upgrade, it can be a very strong long-term hold in the 9th. Don’t pay a ‘fully modernized’ price for a unit that still has electric night storage heating and unknown energy performance.

€385,000 · 45.8 m2 · 2 rooms · Rotenloewengasse 16, Vienna, 09. District, Alsergrund
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €385,000
  • Size: 45.8 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 9

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — sounds like a high-spec renovation/new-build-out inside a historic shell; great if delivered as promised, but execution risk until handover.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (size is tight; location and schools nearby help) Investor: ✔ (prime micro-area, 2-room liquidity; but pricing leaves less yield buffer) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Servitenviertel vibe + walkable restaurants/parks)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — micro-location is excellent, but this is “pay up for the story” pricing; you want protections on delivery/specs and a tighter entry price.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €355,000–€375,000 (best-effort guess; comps not provided, so treat as a starting anchor). Push value-based concessions if price won’t move: seller-paid closing costs, upgrade package confirmed in writing, or a retention/penalty if specs or delivery timing slip. Concrete lever: request documented building reserve status + final scope sign-off (what exactly is included: windows, sound insulation, heating/cooling, courtyard outdoor space allocation) and use any uncertainty to justify the discount.

Bottom line

You’re buying the combo of a historic building + boutique renovation + one of the most “liveable” pockets of the 9th. The deal goes wrong if the renovation details don’t match the marketing, or if the unit’s orientation/noise profile disappoints in real life. If you can lock down specs and timing (or price), it’s a strong long-term hold in a very liquid area.

Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €385,000
  • Size: 46.4 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 9

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — marketing reads like a high-end revitalization/new build-out in an 1890 shell; final finish + handover timing/quality will decide.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (great area, but 46.4 sqm / 2 rooms is tight unless it’s a pied-à-terre) Investor: ➖ (district + micro-location strong, but price-per-sqm and project-risk can compress yields) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Servitenviertel + walkable dining + classic-period building vibe with modern comfort is exactly the point)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — top micro-area, but you’re paying for “revitalized luxury” packaging on a small unit; make the contract/specs and total costs bulletproof.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €350k–€375k (best-effort guess; I don’t have direct comps for this exact micro-street + condition). Push hardest on: (1) any planning-stage uncertainty (plans dated 05/2025), (2) missing monthly running costs, (3) energy performance (HWB 97 is not “modern-efficient”), and (4) exact scope of refurbishment (common areas, risers, windows, sound insulation).

Bottom line

You’re buying the neighbourhood first (Servitenviertel delivers) and the product second. Small, premium-positioned 2-room: great for living well, less great if you need future flexibility. Do the boring diligence: specs, timelines, total building works, and monthly costs—this is where “luxury” deals can bite.

#190
Profitables Investment durch top vermietet Airbnb-Einheit im 9. district (Profitables Investment durch top vermietet Airbnb-Einheit im 9. Bezirk)
€410,000 · 72 m2 · 2 rooms · Vienna, 09. District, Alsergrund
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Address/map mismatch flagged (likely approximate pin).
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €410,000
  • Size: 72 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Floor: ground floor
  • District: Vienna 9

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — it sounds turnkey furnished, but it’s positioned as a rented, operator-run short-stay unit (so “move-in” hinges on tenancy + permitted use).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (ground-floor + tourism/operator setup is usually the opposite of calm, long-term family living) Investor: ✔ (clear rent story; area looks liquid; but you’re underwriting regulation + tenant terms) Lifestyle buyer: ➖ (could work if you can unwind the commercial/short-stay setup cleanly and you actually want EG)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong micro-location and a clean rental narrative, but the whole deal is basically “legal/usable Airbnb unit + tenant contract + ground-floor reality”; price needs to reflect those risks.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort guess): €360k–€395k (comps unknown; this range is mainly a risk discount for EG + tourism/commercial complexity + contract uncertainty). Negotiation levers: (1) Demand documentary proof that the current use is permitted and durable (building/condo rules + any approvals). If anything is time-limited or operator-specific, that’s a direct price haircut. (2) Tie price to tenancy certainty: either a clean vacant-handover agreement, or a tight long-term lease with clear indexation, deposit, and no “easy outs.” (3) Use the monthly running costs/replenishment fund uncertainty as a lever: ask for the last owner association statements and planned works; if there’s capex coming, price adjusts.

Bottom line

You’re buying an income story, not just 72 sqm. If the permissions/house rules don’t fully back the current operation, your yield can evaporate overnight. If they do, you still have EG liquidity/noise trade-offs — so don’t pay “prime 9th district, mid-floor” pricing.

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (modern bathroom, newer large-scale development amenities implied) — depends on actual unit condition and any defect list.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms / 58 sqm can work for a couple + baby, but will feel tight fast) Investor: ✔ (strong rental liquidity signal: 9th district + heavy transit + services density) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (gym/party/common rooms + parks + walkable food/drink cluster)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location and amenity stack, but you must confirm running costs, building finances, and the unit’s orientation/noise profile.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €390,000–€410,000 (best-effort; comps not provided, so treat as a starting anchor, not a valuation). Bottom line

This is a “newer amenity complex in a prime inner district” play: convenient, liquid, and easy to live in. The risk is not the address — it’s the cost structure and whether the unit faces quiet green space or a noisy corridor. If costs are sane and orientation is quiet/bright, it’s a solid buy in this bracket.

Move-in readiness

Depends — “timeless fittings” + fitted kitchen suggests you can move in, but individual heating and missing condition details mean you should expect some catch (service/age/noise/maintenance).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (good size for a small family, but 9th can be street-noisy; unknown floor/lift matters a lot) Investor: ✔ (3 rooms + metro proximity + food/parks density = rental demand; watch heating + building costs) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (quiet-leaning micro-location with lots of restaurants + parks; classic period building vibe)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location and “period building 3-room” formula is strong, but the missing basics (floor, lift, monthly costs, energy data, real condition) are exactly where expensive surprises hide.

Push for full monthly operating costs (incl. reserve), last WEG minutes, any planned works, and proof-of-upgrades (windows/electrics/heating). If individual heating is older or the building has upcoming works, price that risk in immediately.

Bottom line

This is the classic “right district, right size, right vibe” apartment. If it’s truly quiet and the building is healthy, it’s a very livable 9th-district buy. But you only buy it at a price that compensates for individual heating + the unknowns (floor/lift/costs/condition).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (3 rooms on ~68 sqm can work, but layout/bath size and renovation downtime are friction) Investor: ✔ (classic 1090 “period building + lift + courtyard-facing quiet” value-add play, if the building financials check out) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (quiet side street + walkable transit/restaurants; strong central vibe)

Final verdict

You’re buying a top-tier micro-location in 1090 and a quiet courtyard orientation. The apartment itself is a project: assume time, noise, and cash burn before it’s enjoyable. #195
Lage, Lage, beim Schottentor! Freier Mietzins! (Lage, Lage, Ruhelage beim Schottentor! Freier Mietzins!)
€449,000 · 61.5 m2 · 3 rooms · Maria-Theresien-Strasse 5, Vienna, 09. District, Alsergrund
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Address/map mismatch flagged (likely approximate pin).
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €449,000
  • Size: 61.5 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 9

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — building sounds well-managed and the flat reads “clean + functional,” but plan for checks around gas heating + postwar building quirks.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (works for a small family, but 61.5 sqm will feel tight fast) Investor: ✔ ("free rent" regime can be attractive, but only if the legal/rent status is truly clean and documented) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (quiet courtyard + you’re basically at Schottentor)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — top-tier location and rare quiet-for-central, but price per sqm is punchy for a 1950s rebuild and gas heating.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €410k–€430k (best-effort guess; I don’t have direct comps here, so treat this as a negotiation anchor, not a valuation). Push on: (1) gas/individual heating lifecycle + future regulation risk, (2) any upcoming building works despite “nothing planned,” (3) proof of the “freier Mietzins” claim and the exact legal basis, (4) courtyard orientation trade-offs (quiet vs. light/airflow view).

Bottom line

2–3 minutes to Schottentor with courtyard silence is the headline. You’re buying a location story; make sure you’re not overpaying for staging/AI furniture. If the paperwork on rent regime + building condition is solid, this can be a very livable city-base.

Wohnoase mit Garten (Wohnoase mit Garten)
€449,000 · 48.05 m2 · 2 rooms · Tendlergasse 3, Vienna
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Address/map mismatch flagged (likely approximate pin).
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €449,000
  • Size: 48.05 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: district heating
  • District: Vienna 9

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — marketed as a high-end revitalized period building, but the real “ready” status hinges on what’s actually delivered in Top 05 and the handover timeline/snags.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (great outdoor space, but 48 sqm / 2 rooms is tight long-term) Investor: ➖ (9th is strong, but the price per sqm + new-project premium can cap yield) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (inner-city + big terrace/garden vibe is rare, especially if courtyard-facing)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong 9th-district micro-location and compelling terrace/garden story, but you’re paying a premium and the downside risk is mostly “ground-level outdoor space + revitalization unknowns.”

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort guess): €415k–€440k. I don’t have true comps for Tendler Lane/this project, so anchor off (a) uncertainty around building reserves/common-area works post-revitalization, (b) courtyard/ground-floor exposure (security, damp, noise), and (c) missing running-cost transparency. Ask for: (1) repair reserve status + planned works for the first 24 months, (2) a clear statement of operating costs, (3) exact private outdoor rights (terrace vs garden, maintenance obligations, watering/drainage responsibility).

Bottom line

This is a “city apartment that lives bigger” thanks to ~20.8 sqm terrace and (claimed) garden — that’s the hook. The deal goes wrong if the outdoor space is less private/noisier than implied, or if the revitalization reveals expensive shared-building follow-ons. If the bedroom is truly quiet courtyard-facing and the outdoor space is legally clean + low-maintenance, it’s a very livable 9th-district setup.

Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View

  • Size: 54.97 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Floor: ground floor
  • Outdoor space: loggia
  • Heating: district heating
  • District: Vienna 9

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (assuming the “general renovation” is real and properly documented).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms / ~55 sqm is tight long-term) Investor: ✔ (strong district + clinic/university demand; but price-per-sqm needs rent reality check) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (classic Vienna character + central, walkable, café-heavy micro-area)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — great location and “old-world + modern” product, but the pricing likely bakes in the marketing gloss; make it win on terms, not vibes.

Negotiation strategy

List price is €479k (~€8.7k/sqm). Without verified comps for this exact street/building and floor, I’d still push for a target range of €450k–€470k, or hold €479k only if you get tangible value (better floor/quiet orientation, balcony, premium finishes, or seller covers key costs). Ask for proof: renovation scope, warranties, building works history, and the full running-cost picture.

Bottom line

You’re buying “renovated period building” in District 9: the lifestyle and resale story are real. The risk is overpaying for a shiny refurb in a project with many units—treat it like a product launch, not a rare one-off. If it’s courtyard-quiet with quality execution, it’s a very livable city base; if it’s street-noisy or finishes are mid, walk or discount hard.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (district is excellent, but without size/layout it’s impossible to call) Investor: ➖ (address + strong demand micro-area helps; economics unclear without monthly costs and unit details) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (high-walkability, restaurant density, parks, and quick transit access)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — location is a premium micro-area, but the listing is under-specified; only proceed after getting hard facts and use the info gap to negotiate.

Buyer fit

Final verdict

This is a “buy the bones” apartment: the proportions and ceiling height do the heavy lifting. The upside is real, but only if the building’s medium-term maintenance plan is clean and your renovation budget is disciplined. If the courtyard/street orientation is noisy or dark, the romance evaporates fast — verify before you fall in love.

#200
Traumhafter stylish period building mit Balkon - Altbaucharme mit modernem Komfort in Bestlage (Traumhafter Stilaltbau mit Balkon - Altbaucharme mit modernem Komfort in Bestlage)
€485,000 · 48 m2 · 2 rooms · Rotenloewengasse 16, Vienna, 09. District, Alsergrund
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €485,000
  • Size: 48 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Outdoor space: terrasse
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 9

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — reads like a high-spec renovation/new-build-out inside an 1890 building; great if delivery + finishes match, annoying if timelines/variants shift.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (great micro-area, but 48 sqm / 2 rooms is tight long-term) Investor: ✔ (Servitenviertel liquidity + small-unit rental demand; watch total monthly carry) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, food/drink dense, courtyard terrace vibe)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — premium address + courtyard outdoor space is real, but €485k for 48 sqm is priced like “perfect execution”; you want price protection against any delivery/quality/house-cost surprises.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €440k–€465k (best-effort guess; I do not have close-by closed-sale comps for this exact micro-location/finish level). Levers: 1) “Planning status 05/2025 + deviations possible” → ask for a written finish spec + penalty/retention (or price reduction) if key items change (terrace size, window type, flooring, bathroom brands). 2) Request the building’s reserve fund + planned works schedule post-revitalization; if reserves are thin or big items remain, push price down or demand seller contribution. 3) If it’s a developer sale with multiple units, negotiate on speed/clean offer (financing readiness) for a direct discount.

Bottom line

You’re buying the micro-area (Servitenviertel) and the “revitalized period building” story — both sell well later. The deal goes wrong if the courtyard terrace is less usable than imagined (light/noise/privacy) or if the building-wide costs/rules (reserves, WEG decisions, short-let limitations) bite after closing. If the terrace orientation + house economics check out, this is a high-liquidity 9th-district lifestyle unit — just don’t overpay for marketing copy.

Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €499,000
  • Size: 60 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Lift: Yes (as stated)
  • Heating: district heating
  • District: Vienna 9

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (modern-ish “new-build” vibe, functional 2-room layout, lift, garage; no obvious renovation flags in the text).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (works for a couple/new baby phase, but 60 sqm / 2 rooms caps longevity) Investor: ✔ (9th district liquidity + garage scarcity value; easy-to-rent profile near unis/medical) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (quiet bedroom + park adjacency + strong café/restaurant density)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — excellent micro-location + quiet hofseitig bedroom + rare underground parking, but the “mandatory” €41k garage add-on and running costs deserve a pricing check.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort guess; comps unknown): €470k–€495k all-in (apartment + TG), depending on orientation/light, actual building year/state, and reserve fund situation. Negotiation levers: (1) Make the TG price the battleground: it’s “obligatory” but still a separate value component—push for a discount on the flat if they won’t move on TG. (2) Ask for the latest building reserve statement + last owners’ meeting minutes; if upcoming works (lift/garage waterproofing/façade) exist, price that risk immediately.

Bottom line

This is a classic 9th-district “live well without suffering the city” setup: park nearby, transit strong, amenities dense. Two ways it can go wrong fast: garage-related building issues (leaks/repairs/special assessments) and a “bright” living room that’s actually street-noisy or north-facing once you see it. If the light/orientation checks out and the building finances are clean, it’s a very liquid, low-regret footprint for Vienna life.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (kitchen + appliances listed, furnished, separate WC), but budget for gas-therme check/service and possible cosmetic refresh.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (62 sqm / 3 rooms can work for 1 kid, but will feel tight long-term) Investor: ✔ (3-room + central access + transit stack; easy to rent, WG-appeal) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (quiet rear orientation + park + canal + strong tram/U4 connectivity)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great 1090 micro-location and quiet positioning, but 62 sqm at ~€8k/sqm with gas heating and limited “wow” factor should be priced with discipline.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €455k–€480k (best-effort guess; I do not have direct comps here). Anchor on: small-ish size for the price, gas individual heating/therme-in-kitchen risk, no stated energy class, and “4th floor half-stock” nuance; keep the upside (quiet rear, lift, transit, parks) as your reason you’re still serious.

Bottom line

This is the kind of 1090 apartment people actually live in happily: quiet, central, and absurdly well-connected. You’re paying for the neighbourhood and convenience, not for luxury finishes—so don’t overpay. If the building is well-run and the unit is truly quiet, it’s a solid buy at the right number.

Family: ➖ (District 9 can work, but unit layout + noise/orientation matter a lot and aren’t provided) Investor: ✔ (strong micro-location signals; but yields depend on exact unit + building fees) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, “city living” corridor with lots of daily-life infrastructure nearby)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — micro-area looks excellent, but right now this is a “concept listing”; price only means something once the exact unit, specs, and total monthly carrying costs are known.

Negotiation strategy

This is a prime “live like a local” pocket: groceries, parks, and transit density are all high. The risk is paying a premium for the label (new-build branding) without locking in the unit-level fundamentals. If the unit faces a loud axis or the monthly costs surprise you, the deal flips from “smart” to “meh” fast.

#205
Neubauprojekt im 9. district - Zuhause & Wertanlage (Neubauprojekt im 9. Bezirk | Zuhause & Wertanlage)
€509,000 · 53.22 m2 · 3 rooms · Augasse, Vienna, 09. District, Alsergrund
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €509,000
  • Size: 53.22 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 9

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — it’s a new-build project with start planned for H1 2026 and delivery around autumn 2027, so you’re buying a promise + spec.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (53 sqm / 3 rooms can work, but it’s compact; depends on layout + storage + courtyard/noise) Investor: ✔ (university campus catalyst + strong transit + small unit size tends to rent well) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable 9th vibe, transit-heavy, lots of daily-life amenities nearby)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location thesis is strong (Spittelau + campus impact), but the real risk is pricing a future delivery and unknowns in final specs/fees.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €475k–€495k (best-effort guess; I do not have recent closed comps for this micro-area/new-build tier, so treat this as a negotiating anchor, not a valuation). Levers: (1) ask for a written, itemized estimate of monthly running costs + reserve contributions and bake that into your offer; (2) push for contract protections: clear delivery milestones/penalties or buyer-friendly exit triggers if timelines/specs change. Two ways this deal can go wrong: (a) construction timeline slips (you carry opportunity cost + rent longer, mortgage terms can change); (b) the “quiet side street” claim fails in practice due to tram/arterial noise or orientation, and a 53 sqm unit has less margin to “escape” a bad bedroom/living-room exposure.

Bottom line

If the floorplan is efficient and the unit is oriented away from the noisiest edges, this is a classic 9th-district demand play. You’re paying new-build pricing for a 2027 move-in — make the contract and fee transparency do the heavy lifting. For livability, the balcony helps, but the real make-or-break is light/noise and whether the 3rd room is truly usable.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — reads like a high-end revitalized period building project, but the real question is construction status, handover timing, and what exactly is included vs. “render promises.”

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms / 56 m² can work for a couple + baby, but it’s tight long-term; garden helps) Investor: ➖ (9th is liquid, but boutique “luxury revitalization” pricing can cap yield; check rentability rules + total monthly costs) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (classic Vienna vibe + courtyard-facing bedroom + big private outdoor space is a rare combo)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location and a compelling product (period building + garden), but €509k for 56 m² demands proof on specs, total running costs, and any renovation/building-risk items.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €470k–€495k (best-effort guess; I don’t have comps for this exact revit project). Push on (1) missing monthly costs (BK/RL/heating), (2) energy/condition documentation, (3) any construction timeline risk, (4) ground-floor privacy/noise if applicable, and (5) terrace/garden legal allocation (exclusive use vs. ownership).

Bottom line

2 rooms + ~23 m² outdoor space in the 9th is the headline — that’s lifestyle gold if it’s quiet and private. This smells like “developer-grade marketing”: verify every finish, window spec, and what you actually get at handover. If the garden is truly usable and the bedroom is genuinely courtyard-quiet, this is a strong buy at the right number — not at any number.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (fresh renovation); just verify final punch-list items (new entrance/brandschutz door timing, meter boxing).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms is fine for a couple + baby, but 3rd floor no lift becomes a daily tax) Investor: ➖ (strong location demand, but high €/sqm + high HWB + no lift caps upside) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, cultural core, universities, cafés; great “Vienna daily life” zone)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — premium micro-location, but the no-lift 3rd floor + very old building + energy number suggest you should not pay “top of range” for 64 sqm.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €470k–€500k (best-effort guess; I don’t have direct comps for this exact street/back-facing stack). Use: (1) 3rd floor without lift as the anchor discount, (2) HWB 184 as a financing/operating-cost perception penalty, (3) “WEG in preparation” + missing finalized documents as a process-risk lever. Ask for: full renovation scope (what’s new behind the walls), window specs, heating age, planned house works, repair fund history, and confirmation the unit is indeed quieter (not on the main street).

Bottom line

Two big truths: the area sells itself; the building/stack doesn’t. If you’ll live here and value walkability over elevator convenience, it’s a strong lifestyle pick. Price it like a great location with real drawbacks—not like a turnkey new-build.

Move-in ready (renovated “first occupancy after refurbishment”), but confirm building-side works + final handover punch list.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms / ~65 sqm works for a couple + baby, tight for longer-term) Investor: ✔ (very liquid micro-location; strong tenant demand near universities/AKH) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (classic inner-district life: walkable, cafés, parks, transit)

Final verdict

You’re paying for the micro-area: near Schottentor/Votivkirche with serious amenity density. The biggest hidden swing factors are floor + lift + street noise + whether “sanierung” is cosmetic or truly end-to-end. If it’s in the non-lift staircase above mid-floor or faces a noisy stretch, push hard on price; if it’s quiet + good light, it’ll hold value well.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (good size for a couple/small family, but only 2 rooms and delayed possession) Investor: ✔ (prime micro-location + tenant in place; just underwrite the lease terms carefully) Lifestyle buyer: ➖ (fantastic neighborhood vibe, but the timeline kills spontaneity)

Negotiation strategy

Ask for the lease details (rent level, indexation, deposit, break clauses, maintenance responsibility) and price it like a “delayed-use” asset, not a dream-home purchase. Target price range: €485k–€510k (comps unknown; this range is mainly a discount for the 2026 handover + uncertainty on floor/light/noise).

Bottom line

You’re buying the neighborhood first — this is one of Vienna’s most livable central pockets. At ~€7.2k/sqm, it’s not cheap for a 2-room unit with mid-range energy use and delayed possession. If you can wait until 2026 (or you’re happy as an investor), it can make sense at the right price.

Move-in readiness

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms / ~55 sqm is tight long-term unless it’s a couple-with-baby plan) Investor: ✔ (9th district + AKH/MedUni gravity + small premium unit = strong rental demand profile) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (classic period building appeal + inner-city walkability; likely a very “Vienna” feel)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — excellent macro/micro location and “renovated period building” narrative, but you’re paying a premium €/sqm and the unknowns (floor, exact fit-out, monthly costs) are leverage.

Negotiation strategy

Ask for the full cost sheet (operating costs + reserve contributions), energy/tech details, and the exact unit spec (windows, insulation, heating distribution, soundproofing, courtyard orientation). Target price range: €490k–€515k (best-effort); comps are unknown from the provided data, so validate €/sqm against recent 9th-district renovated-period building sales before going firm.

Bottom line

This is a “pay-for-finish-and-address” apartment: if the execution is genuinely top-tier, it can be worth it. At ~€9.6k/sqm you want proof: quiet orientation, strong building maintenance plan, and real renovation quality — not just pretty staging. Great as a high-comfort city base or a long-hold rental in a medical/university catchment.

The hidden risk is not the area — it’s the unknowns (size, fees, condition) and whether the layout actually feels generous in real life. If the numbers check out, it’s the kind of place that stays liquid.

  • Rooms: 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Needs work — sold unsanitized, so budget/time for a full interior build-out.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (3 rooms / ~70 sqm can work, but renovation + city noise/parking trade-offs) Investor: ➖ (District 9 is liquid, but capex + unclear rent/tenant status can dilute returns) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (prime Alsergrund streetscape + cafés/parks/transit, if you want to design your own place)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location and building “project” appeal, but you’re paying a premium for potential while carrying renovation risk.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €520k–€555k (best-effort guess; without comps and unit-specific condition/floor/outlook, treat this as a negotiation anchor, not a valuation). Levers to push: (1) unsanitized sale = quantified renovation budget; (2) energy performance currently weak/unclear until window swap is done; (3) monthly building costs already ~€300 — ask what will happen post-works. Concrete check to weaponize: request the house reserve status + planned works schedule (windows/AC hookups/common areas) and whether special assessments are expected.

Bottom line

You’re buying a location-first shell in one of Vienna’s most livable inner districts. The deal goes sideways if renovation costs/time explode or if the unit’s floor/orientation is bad (dark/noisy) — those two variables decide whether this is “chic period building” or an expensive headache. If you can manage a renovation and you value the 9th, it’s a strong contender at the right entry price.

But the macro-location is polarizing—price has to reflect the Guertel adjacency, no matter how good the interior is. Don’t overpay for carpentry and staging; pay for layout, terrace orientation, and verified building health.

2-bed-in-1090 vibes with strong “walk everywhere” daily life. Your leverage is uncertainty: unknown floor/lift + unknown building health + individual heating. If the building is solid and the renovation is documented, this becomes a very defendable buy at the right number.

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (renovation appears comprehensive; kitchen/finishing items still “pending” per listing).

Buyer fit

Family: ✔ (4 rooms on ~82.5 sqm works if you value layout over big room sizes) Investor: ➖ (good tenant demand in this area, but heating choice + unknown building economics add risk) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (central, quiet street vibe, courtyard balcony = big quality-of-life win)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong “fresh renovation + courtyard outdoor space + central 9th” combo, but the heating system and missing running-cost/building details are leverage.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €545k–€575k (best-effort guess; I don’t have verified local comps/building fund data here). This reads like a classic “city access without the noise” apartment — the courtyard-facing balcony is the headline feature. You’re paying for the reset-to-new renovation story; make them document it like a new-build (paper trail, guarantees). If the building finances/checks come back clean, it’s a very livable 9th-district base with strong long-term desirability.

#218
Ihr neues Zuhause in Premiumlage (Ihr neues Zuhause in Premiumlage)
€619,000 · 53.23 m2 · 2 rooms · Tendlergasse 3, Vienna
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Address/map mismatch flagged (likely approximate pin).
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €619,000
  • Size: 53.23 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Outdoor space: balkon
  • Heating: district heating
  • District: Vienna 9

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (sounds like a high-spec, revitalized period building product) — but only after you confirm the exact completion status, handover scope, and what “revitalized” includes for the building systems.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (53 sqm / 2 rooms reads tight for long-term family life) Investor: ➖ (liquid micro-location, but yield vs. €619k needs a hard reality check) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (quiet courtyard orientation + classic period building vibe + strong 9th district day-to-day)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — the package is compelling (quiet courtyard + premium 9th + “revitalized” feel), but at ~€11.6k/sqm you’re paying for story and finish; make the building-level unknowns and true noise/light exposure your price discipline.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €565k–€600k (best-effort guess; I don’t have verified comps for this exact micro-pocket/finish level, so treat this as a negotiation anchor, not a valuation). Levers: (1) Demand clarity on building reserves + what was renewed (roof/riser pipes/electrics) and price the residual risk; (2) pin down balcony size + legal status and any maintenance allocation; (3) if completion/hand-over is pending, negotiate via payment milestones, snagging list, and retention.

Bottom line

This is a “quiet-courtyard, city-core-ish” lifestyle buy in a very liveable part of the 9th (parks + transit density read strong). The deal goes wrong if the revitalization is more cosmetic than systemic, or if the courtyard still carries noise/odor from gastronomy/service areas. If the light is good and the building systems are truly renewed, it’s a premium small home you’ll enjoy; if not, you just overpaid for parquet and marketing copy.

Move-in ready (renovated, underfloor heating, modern kitchen; verify scope of renovation + wiring/plumbing).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (good 3-room layout, but ~70 m² is “tight family” unless it’s a 2+1; building noise/3rd floor reality matters) Investor: ✔ (9th district demand + transit saturation; watch yield vs €625k entry price + running costs) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walk-everywhere micro-area: restaurants, parks, transit density; likely very “Vienna urban”)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — great district + renovated period building vibe, but price per m² is punchy for ~70 m² and the listing reads salesy; you want proof on renovation quality, building condition, and true floor/quietness.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort guess): €560k–€600k. Without comps this is an estimate; anchor on (a) small size at high ticket, (b) missing energy class, (c) period building building CAPEX risk, (d) “furnished optional” not adding much resale value. Ask for: last 10 years building works, reserve fund status, planned repairs, and a clean breakdown of the €303.42/month plus reserve allocation.

Bottom line

2) 9th district + heavy transit + tons of amenities = high day-to-day quality and strong liquidity. 2) Renovated + underfloor heating is the right comfort upgrade for an older building, but only if the renovation was done properly. 3) At €625k you’re paying for “ready + location” — make them prove it, or make them cut it.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (3 rooms is workable, but 79 sqm is tight if you need real kid/office separation) Investor: ➖ (location demand is strong, but monthly costs + heating setup can cap rent appeal) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, central, lots of food/parks/transit density — classic “live Vienna properly” pocket)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-area signals for 1090, but the price per sqm needs justification via condition, floor/lift, and building works.

If it’s a quiet orientation and the building is well-run, this is a “buy and hold” kind of apartment — otherwise, don’t overpay for the headline address.

Move-in ready (renovated, modern finishes; still verify workmanship + permits/receipts).

Buyer fit

Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View

  • Size: 79.09 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Floor: 1
  • Lift: No (as stated)
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 9

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — cosmetically it can look “done” fast, but the legal/technical side (ownership setup + protected building constraints) is what decides true readiness.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (great central location, but 1st floor no lift + only 79 sqm for long-term family growth) Investor: ➖ (rentability vs price/sqm and gas heating; strong tenant demand here, but check restrictions) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (quiet courtyard-facing period building near Schottentor with high walkability and culture)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — top micro-location and classic period building appeal, but you should price in no-lift reality, gas heating, and the “WE in preparation” execution risk.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €610k–€635k (best-effort; I don’t have verified comps for this exact courtyard-facing line, condition, and building). Use: (1) 1st floor without lift, (2) protected/landmark building limits on future changes, (3) energy performance (HWB 105), (4) gas heating, and (5) the fact that apartment ownership formation is still in preparation. Ask for the full document pack before signing anything: WE contract draft, allocation key, reserve fund history, recent maintenance, and any planned works.

Bottom line

If you want a central, “old Vienna” lifestyle and you value quiet over street buzz, this is a strong 1090 play. The biggest hidden swing factor isn’t the parquet/stucco — it’s paperwork + building obligations. Get clarity on ownership formation and upcoming building spend; that’s where you win (or lose) the deal.

€649,000 · 79 m2 · 3 rooms · Waehringer Strasse, Vienna, 09. District, Alsergrund
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Address/map mismatch flagged (likely approximate pin).
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €649,000
  • Size: 79 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 9

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — high-finish shell in a prime address, but you’ll likely need to budget/time for a full kitchen install and any “old building surprises.”

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (good 3-room size, but inner-city first-floor-in-palais vibe may trade privacy for prestige) Investor: ➖ (top location helps, but kitchen missing + landmark/building constraints can complicate capex and turnaround) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (historic building, walkable culture/uni/cafés; classic Vienna city living)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — excellent 9th district positioning and gorgeous building story, but the price needs to reflect missing kitchen + gas heating + typical Gruenderzeit maintenance/constraints.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €610k–€635k (best-effort without direct comp set; if recent in-building or nearby Palais/period building comps support €649k, then negotiate via kitchen allowance + condition items instead of headline price). Anchor on: no fitted kitchen included, first-floor privacy/noise risk on a major street, and potential limitations/costs from a historically protected building. Ask for: written clarification on planned building works/reserve fund, any restrictions on interior changes, and recent heating/utility consumption.

Bottom line

You’re buying the address and the building’s “Vienna story” more than you’re buying a turnkey apartment. If you want a plug-and-play home, this becomes attractive only if the kitchen/any upgrades are priced in. As a long-term hold in 1090 near Schottentor, the location does a lot of the heavy lifting.

Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
