Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €665,000
  • Size: 101.89 m2
  • Rooms: 4
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 9

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — structurally “presentable” but the kitchen/bath/installs likely want a real refresh to feel current.

Buyer fit

Family: ✔ (4 rooms + strong daily infrastructure) Investor: ➖ (capex risk + tenant/condo specifics can swing returns) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (inner-district, walkable, lots of dining/cafés nearby)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great inner 9th-district positioning and classic building character, but you’re buying into modernization scope and gas-heating risk.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €600k–€635k (best-effort; I don’t have verified comps for this exact micro-location/building condition, so treat this as a negotiation anchor, not a valuation). Press hard on (1) the true renovation budget for kitchen/bath/electrics/plumbing surfaces and (2) building financials and upcoming works. If the seller can’t document recent upgrades to core installs, price that uncertainty in.

Bottom line

If you want a large, classic inner-city apartment with upside, this is the right “shape” of deal — but only at a price that leaves headroom for modernization. Two ways this goes wrong: (1) hidden building-level spend (façade/roof/riser lines) hits after you close, (2) the renovation expands from “cosmetic” into wiring/plumbing/heating changes once walls are opened. Gas floor heating can become a headline risk over your holding period; factor that into both budget and resale positioning.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — marketed as luxury attic conversion with underfloor heating, but without the full listing text/energy data you’re buying the vibe more than the verified spec.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (2 rooms / ~55 sqm is tight) Investor: ➖ (premium price per sqm can cap yield; upside mainly if this is truly high-end and easy to rent) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Dachgeschoss + loggia in 1090 is classic “city-life” product)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location signals and a desirable product type, but the €689k ask for 55 sqm needs proof (finish quality, building condition, running costs) to justify.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €620k–€660k (best-effort guess; comps unknown). Anchor on missing hard facts (energy class, monthly costs, floor/lift confirmation), and push for clarity on roof/terrace waterproofing history, HVAC/cooling, and building reserve status.

Bottom line

Attic conversions sell on light, privacy, and terrace/loggia lifestyle — and 1090 can absolutely carry that. But at this size, you’re paying for finish + scarcity; if either is merely “nice” not “exceptional,” the pricing gets shaky. Micro-area looks strong for walkability (restaurants, groceries, parks, transit density), so if the unit checks out, it’s a high-comfort daily driver.

Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €689,000
  • Size: 54.16 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Floor: ground floor
  • Outdoor space: balkon
  • Heating: district heating
  • District: Vienna 9

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — marketed as revitalized/renovated, but “attic dream” + old building means final finish quality, waterproofing, and building systems matter more than the brochure.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (2 rooms, ~54 sqm; great for one/two, tight for family life) Investor: ➖ (prime district helps, but ultra-high €/sqm and small-unit liquidity depends on exact layout/terrace and running costs) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (central, café density, parks nearby, and likely strong “Vienna charm” factor)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location is objectively strong, but the price implies premium execution; verify the attic build quality and ongoing building costs before paying top-of-market.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €620k–€660k (best-effort guess; comps not provided, and attic/terrace value swings wildly by orientation, privacy, and finish level). Ask for (1) building reserve status and planned works post-revitalization, (2) full breakdown of monthly costs, and (3) documented specs for roof/terrace waterproofing, insulation, and any guarantees. Use any uncertainty (missing energy rating, unclear floor/lift, unknown monthly costs) to justify a price reduction or seller-paid closing/fit-out items.

Bottom line

This is a “pay for the vibe” 9th-district home: you’re buying micro-location, light, and outdoor space more than raw square meters. Two ways this deal goes wrong: the terrace/roof details are mediocre (leaks, heat load, noise) or the building’s post-renovation economics disappoint (high common charges, reserve gaps). If the outdoor space is genuinely quiet/private and the build spec is top-tier, it’s a very livable city base — but don’t overpay blind.

Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €690,000
  • Size: 78.42 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 9

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (reads like a refreshed unit with modern bath + fitted kitchen; confirm condition in viewing).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (78 sqm / 2 rooms can work for a couple + baby, but it’s not a “grow-into” family layout) Investor: ✔ (strong micro-location + “free rent” positioning helps flexibility, but check building capex + tenant/usage constraints) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (classic Viennese old-building feel + walkability + fast transit to center/uni/AKH)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great 1090 micro-area + period building charm, but you’re paying a premium €/sqm and you need clarity on building condition, floorplan efficiency, and any legal/WEG/maintenance baggage.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €630k–€670k (best-effort; comps not provided). Push on: (1) gas floor heating running costs + any upcoming conversion obligations, (2) building maintenance reserve + planned works, (3) noise/daylight to Hebra Lane side, (4) layout “tax” (separate kitchen + large anteroom can waste sqm). Ask for the full doc pack mentioned (WE contract, Nutzwertgutachten, owners’ meeting minutes) before making a clean offer.

Bottom line

2-room period building in a prime 1090 pocket is a liquid asset: easy to live in, easy to re-sell. Your upside is the location + character; your downside risk is building-level costs (old stock) and overpaying at the peak €/sqm. If it shows well in person (quiet, bright, good ceiling height), it’s worth chasing—but only at the right number.

Buyer fit

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great inner-8th location signal, but this is a high-variance “cheap for a reason” unit where one hidden issue can erase the discount.

Family: ❌ (39.4 sqm, souterrain layout, limited long-term flexibility) Investor: ➖ (good micro-location + low running costs, but rental/desirability depends heavily on daylight + dampness + heating bills) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (if you want central Josefstadt walkability and you’re fine with basement-level living)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location is strong, but souterrain + electric heat + mid-tier energy performance should be priced like a compromise, not like a typical 8th-district “easy sell” unit.

Negotiation strategy

Start by treating it as a “light, dry, quiet?” gamble: demand evidence (humidity readings, ventilation setup, any past damp remediation, window size/orientation, and a winter electricity spend estimate). Target price range: €155k–€170k (comps not provided, so this is a risk-adjusted range based on souterrain discount + heating/energy penalty; if it’s unusually bright/dry/quiet, you can justify moving toward ask).

Bottom line

You’re buying Josefstadt walkability, not “Vienna sunlight.” If it’s dry and bright, it’s a neat small-city base; if not, it becomes a resale/rental headache. Low monthly building costs are nice — but electric heating can quietly eat that advantage.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (1 room + not available to use) Investor: ✔ (indefinite tenant, modest gross yield, strong district) Lifestyle buyer: ❌ (you can’t move in; you’re buying a cashflow + location story)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location in the 8th, but the indefinite tenancy and low-ish yield cap your upside and your exit options.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €195k–€210k (best-effort; I don’t have true comps here). Anchor on: indefinite lease risk, limited rent-growth, “archive photos” (presentation risk), and gas heating / mid-90s building. Ask for: full lease terms, tenant payment history, indexed rent clause, any scheduled capex in the building, and precise running costs (operating + reserve).

Bottom line

You’re buying Josefstadt + stability, not flexibility. If you need optionality (own use / easy resale), this is the wrong shape of asset. If you want a small, low-drama rental in a premium area, it can work — but only at the right price.

Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €229,000
  • Size: 40.93 m2
  • Rooms: 1
  • Lift: Yes (as stated)
  • Heating: Gasheizung
  • District: Vienna 8

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — it’s currently unbefristet rented, so you’re buying a cashflow situation, not a home you can plan to occupy.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (1 room + indefinite tenancy) Investor: ✔ (clear rent figure + strong micro-location) Lifestyle buyer: ❌ (you can’t use it, and photos are archive from 2021)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — top micro-area, but the unbefristet lease and gas heating cap upside; price only works if the tenancy economics are truly favorable.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €195k–€215k (best-effort guess; I don’t have comps here). Your lever is the verifiable uncertainty: request the full lease package (contract, rent breakdown, indexation clause, deposit, any side agreements) + house financials (reserve fund/"Ruecklage", recent major works, planned works). If the rent is below market or indexation is weak, you’re buying a long-duration discount — push price down accordingly.

Bottom line

2 key strengths: Josefstadt walkability and liquidity (easy to re-sell later), and a tidy 1996 building with lift. 2 ways it can go wrong: (1) the “unbefristet” tenant becomes effectively non-removable at a rent that lags inflation, turning this into a low-yield bond; (2) building capex/reserves (or hidden lift/facade/roof work) hits your net yield hard. If you want a set-and-forget investment, this can work — but only at the right entry price and with the tenancy terms confirmed in writing.

Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €235,000
  • Rooms: 3
  • District: Vienna 8

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — the unit is described as very well kept and furnished, but the “business/office” designation + souterrain location can make real-world usability (and permissions) the deciding factor.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (souterrain + small format + designation mismatch risk) Investor: ➖ (can work, but liquidity/financing + resale pool are narrower) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (if you want a central base / studio / practice vibe and you’re fine with semi-basement living)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — prime Josefstadt micro-location and turnkey condition, but you’re paying “almost apartment money” for something that may be legally/financially treated as commercial and is in the basement.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €200k–€220k (best-effort guess; comps for souterrain + commercial-dedicated WE are very case-specific, so confirm with 2–3 nearby closed deals). Levers: (1) designation as Geschaeftslokal/Buero despite residential use — ask what is permitted today (and what would be required to legalize residential use, if that’s your plan); (2) basement risk (daylight, humidity, noise, radon/ventilation perception) — use an independent moisture check + window/courtyard orientation to justify price; (3) missing hard data (heating type, energy class) — treat as an uncertainty discount.

Bottom line

Josefstadt location + 3-room layout on ~52–56 m² is a nice “small but functional” plan. But souterrain + commercial dedication can hit you twice: harder financing now, smaller buyer pool later. If you buy it, buy it as what it legally is — and make the numbers work with a negotiation cushion.

#235
Helle Altbauflaeche zum selber gestalten, Naehe Schoenbornpark (Helle Altbaufläche zum selber gestalten, Nähe Schönbornpark)
€240,000 · 49.84 m2 · 2 rooms · Vienna, 08. District, Josefstadt
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €240,000
  • Size: 49.84 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Floor: ground floor
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 8

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — this reads like a shell/office-to-residential conversion + full fit-out, so timeline and budget risk are the whole story.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (size + likely layout constraints) Investor: ➖ (works only if conversion scope + total capex are tightly controlled) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (if you want Josefstadt + you enjoy designing, and can tolerate construction)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — prime-feeling micro-location and light are compelling, but conversion/ground-level risk needs a price that leaves margin for surprises.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €205k–€225k (best-effort; I don’t have true comps or the building’s reserve/condition). Anchor on (1) the unit not being a finished apartment yet, (2) uncertainty on what exactly the seller “abtrennung” includes, and (3) the cost/complexity of approvals + MEP (heating/plumbing/electrics) in an old building. Make your offer conditional on receiving: written proof of residential re-zoning, scope/spec of separation works, and a realistic capex estimate. Concrete lever: request the last 2–3 owners’ meetings + reserve fund status; if reserves are thin or planned works (roof/façade/riser pipes) exist, price comes down.

Bottom line

Josefstadt + strong walkability/transit is the upside; you’re buying optionality and location, not a turnkey flat. Two ways this goes wrong: permitting/condominium consent becomes messy (or limits layout), and the “slightly above ground floor” reality ends up noisier/darker than expected. If you can lock the legal/technical paperwork early and keep capex contained, it can be a smart entry into 1080 — otherwise it’s a money-and-time sink.

Move-in readiness

Needs work — it’s essentially a raw shell/old area intended to be converted into a flat.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (good district + parks, but ground-floor/plan-risk makes it less “easy-mode”) Investor: ✔ (if conversion is clean + rentability is strong in the 8th; exit liquidity decent) Lifestyle buyer: ➖ (cool period building vibe potential, but you’re buying a project, not a home)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — price can make sense for Josefstadt, but conversion risk + ground-floor discount + gas heating uncertainty mean you should only buy if the paperwork and building economics check out.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €210,000–€230,000 (best-effort guess; comps for “convertible shell” units are unknown, so treat this as a starting anchor, not a valuation). Levers: (1) conversion/permit + “what exactly is already rezoned to residential” in writing, (2) confirmed scope/costs of the planned build-out and timelines, (3) building reserve + upcoming works (facade/roof/lift) that could push monthly costs.

Bottom line

You’re paying Josefstadt money for optionality: high ceilings, big windows, and a location with real day-to-day convenience. The deal goes bad if the conversion turns into a budget/time sink or if ground-floor living (noise/privacy) ends up being a lifestyle mismatch. If you can’t get clarity on costs and legal/technical feasibility, it’s not a bargain — it’s a liability.

Move-in readiness

Needs work (this is essentially a conversion/renovation project, not a turnkey apartment).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (good district + parks/transit, but ground-level + construction timeline risk) Investor: ➖ (area is liquid, but permitting/spec risk and heating/energy uncertainty) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Josefstadt base with big-city convenience; if you enjoy designing your own layout)

Final verdict

Negotiation strategy

You’re buying location and ceiling height, not finish quality. Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (sounds recently modernized + very good stated condition; main unknown is finishes/tech specs inside the unit).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (1-room) Investor: ✔ (strong central-ish 1080 demand, efficient size, good transit) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, cafe density, quick access to inner city)

Final verdict

Negotiation strategy

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (renovated), but timing/quality of the “new kitchen to be installed” is the only real dependency.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ✔ Lifestyle buyer: ✔

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong location and fresh renovation, but Guertel-facing risk (noise/air) and €372/mo running costs need to be priced in.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €270k–€285k (best-effort guess; I do not have verified comparable sales for this exact line/street exposure). Anchor on: (1) Hernalser Guertel exposure likely reduces quiet-living appeal, (2) kitchen not yet delivered/installed, (3) monthly costs are punchy for 52 sqm. Ask for documentation: renovation scope/warranties, exact orientation (street vs courtyard), window specs, and a clear breakdown of €371.94 (incl. reserve vs operating vs heating).

Bottom line

If it’s courtyard-facing and the windows are good: it’s a very livable, low-hassle 2-room in a high-demand zone. If it’s Guertel/U6-facing: treat it like a “great on paper” unit that must be bought at a discount. Monthly costs are the silent killer—make them part of your offer math, not an afterthought.

Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €299,000
  • Size: 51.87 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Floor: 6
  • Heating: district heating
  • District: Vienna 8

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (renovated) but “depends” on the promised new kitchen actually being installed and finished well.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms/≈52 sqm works for a couple + baby, but it’s not a long-haul family layout) Investor: ✔ (renovated, strong transit/AKH/university pull; watch noise + running costs) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Josefstadt edge with quick city access, cafés, walkability)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — attractive 8th-district edge + fresh renovation, but Guertel frontage typically means noise/air trade-offs and the monthly costs look chunky for the size.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €270k–€285k (best-effort; I don’t have building comps/condition report, so treat this as a negotiation anchor, not a valuation). Make the offer conditional on: (1) kitchen installed before closing (with specs), (2) written confirmation of what the €371.94/month includes (operating + reserve + any heating components), (3) documented renovation scope (electrics, plumbing, waterproofing) + invoices/warranties if available. Push hard on Guertel-facing noise: request an evening viewing and use any window/insulation shortcomings as a concrete discount lever.

Bottom line

High-convenience city base near U6 and trams — that’s the real asset. The risk is micro-location externalities (Guertel noise/air) and cost structure, not the apartment itself. If it’s quiet enough inside and the costs are clean, it’s a solid buy at the right price — otherwise, treat it as a rental-grade asset and price it accordingly.

  • Size: 59 m2

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — likely fine if building systems are solid, but “courtyard-quiet” units can hide low-light / ventilation trade-offs.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (59 sqm / 2 rooms is tight long-term) Investor: ✔ (District 8 liquidity + strong amenity density) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, restaurant-heavy, quiet-at-home pitch)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location and “quiet” positioning, but you need clarity on light, floor, and actual condition to justify the price.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €300k–€320k (best-effort guess; true comps not provided). Use (1) missing floor info, (2) “quiet courtyard” potentially meaning darker exposure, (3) monthly costs, and (4) any renovation/finish uncertainty as leverage. Ask for energy performance data and recent building works (roof/façade/elevator/heating) before firming up.

Bottom line

District 8 is the kind of place you don’t regret living in — you pay for the address and the daily convenience. The real question is not “is it central?”; it’s “is it bright and pleasant at 2pm in winter?” If the light is good and the building is well-run, it’s a very defensible buy. If it’s dim, treat it as a value play only.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready: freshly renovated, modern kitchen/bath, no obvious “project” signals—verify workmanship and any hidden building-systems issues typical of 1900 stock.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2.5 rooms at 66 sqm can work for a couple + baby, but it’s tight long-term) Investor: ✔ (8th district demand + excellent transit; mezzanine/ground-adjacent risk is the main cap) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (cafés/restaurants density, walkability, and quick U2/U6 access are the point here)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong location and turnkey reno, but mezzanine living + gas heating + unknown floorplan details should be priced in.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €305,000–€320,000 (best-effort guess; without direct comps and an exact floorplan/layout, treat this as a negotiation anchor, not a valuation). Push hardest on mezzanine drawbacks (privacy, street noise, security), and ask for proof of renovation scope (invoices, warranties, plumbing/electrics work) plus building reserves/planned works.

Bottom line

2.5 rooms in Josefstadt at this ticket is a classic “buy the zip code” play. Mezzanine is the trade: you get centrality and value, but you must confirm light, noise, and privacy are acceptable. If the building is truly well-run (reserves + no major works looming), this can be a very sensible first Vienna buy.

Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View

  • Size: 63.5 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Outdoor space: balkon
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 8

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Needs work — this reads like a full renovation, not “paint + floors”.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (great location + layout, but renovation time/cost + gas heating risk) Investor: ➖ (works for WG/letting on paper, but capex + potential building works can kill the yield) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Josefstadt vibe + balcony + walkability, if you’re up for a project)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong 1080 micro-location and balcony, but the unknown renovation scope + gas individual heating + building-level uncertainties should be priced in.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort guess): €300k–€325k (comps not provided; this is a renovation-risk discount estimate for 63.5 sqm + small balcony). Levers to push: - Make the offer explicitly conditional on (a) a contractor walkthrough and (b) building documents: reserve fund level, planned works, last/next façade/roof/lift work. - Clarify the floor discrepancy (text suggests “2nd with lift” but later says “3rd floor”) and use any mismatch as credibility/effort leverage. - Price the gas heating risk: budget for potential system changes in coming years; ask for boiler age/service history and chimney/flue status.

Bottom line

You’re buying the postcode and the layout — not the current condition. This can become a very livable 3-room city base, but the deal goes bad fast if the building is heading into major works or if the renovation reveals damp/old electrics. Keep your offer disciplined and document-driven.

Move-in readiness

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms ~60sqm works for a couple + baby, but not a long-haul family layout) Investor: ➖ (strong rental location, but energy performance + monthly running costs compress yield) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Josefstadt near parks/transit/restaurants; quiet courtyard bedroom is the killer feature)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location and liveability for a 2-room, but the building era/energy score and running costs mean price discipline matters.

Bottom line

This is a “buy the bedroom-to-courtyard and the U6/tram access” apartment. If the building’s maintenance plan is clean and no big capex is looming, it’s a solid, low-drama home base. If major works are pending, you’re overpaying at list unless the seller moves meaningfully on price.

€372,000 · 30.31 m2 · 1 rooms · Fuhrmannsgasse, Vienna, 08. District, Josefstadt
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Address/map mismatch flagged (likely approximate pin).
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View

  • Size: 30.31 m2

  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 8

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (renovated/first-occupancy positioning), but the real test is building paperwork + snag list quality.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ✔

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — prime 8th district micro-location and “fresh period building” appeal, but you’re paying a premium for a 30 sqm one-room product where resale/rent is sensitive to noise, layout, and running costs.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort guess; comps not verified): €340 000–€360 000. Target price range (best-effort): EUR 340,000–360,000 (comps not provided, so treat as a positioning target, not a valuation). Ask for a price move based on verifiable uncertainties: (1) building reserve status + planned works, (2) final as-built specs/warranties for the renovation (flooring, windows, ventilation), (3) confirmed monthly running costs and heating operating model.

Bottom line

This is a “buy the neighborhood + the vibe” micro-apartment: great if you want Josefstadt life with minimal fuss. Deal goes wrong if the unit turns out to be street-noisy or the renovation is lipstick-deep (snags, moisture, ventilation issues). If documents check out and orientation is quiet/bright, it’s a strong lifestyle pied-à-terre — just don’t overpay for the marketing poetry.

#247

  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 8

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — sounds like a renovated/"re-imagined" historic build with modern heating, but key unit specifics (size/rooms/floor/costs/energy) are missing.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ✔

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location looks strong, but with no sqm/rooms/monthly costs you’re currently buying the marketing, not the asset.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort guess): €340k–€365k (comps unknown — treat this as a starting anchor, not a valuation). Ask for: exact unit sqm + room count, floor + lift, orientation (street/courtyard), monthly running costs + reserve fund, and what exactly was renovated (windows, electrics, risers, roof, common areas). Use any missing/unclear items as leverage.

Bottom line

Josefstadt is one of Vienna’s easiest districts to live in: central, civilized, and amenity-rich. Underfloor heating is a real quality-of-life win if the renovation is done properly. Don’t commit until you can price it per sqm and understand ongoing costs — otherwise you can’t tell “charming” from “overpriced.”

Charmante 2-rooms-Altbauwohnung - Bestlage der Josefstadt (Charmante 2-Zimmer-Altbauwohnung - Bestlage der Josefstadt)

Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €375,000
  • Size: 64.3 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Lift: Yes (as stated)
  • Outdoor space: balkon
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 8

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — marketed as “first occupancy / high-end fit-out”, but treat it like a refurbished unit in a 1900 building: verify workmanship, moisture/noise, and the gas heating setup.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms at 64 sqm can work for a couple + baby, but it’s not a long-term family layout) Investor: ✔ (prime 1080 micro-location, lift, clean 2-room layout; likely strong tenant demand) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (quiet street feel yet very urban; restaurants/transit density is exactly the Josefstadt sweet spot)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location does heavy lifting, but 1st floor + gas individual heating + unknown specifics of renovation/building status mean you want price protection.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €345k–€365k (best-effort guess; without true comps and exact street-facing orientation, treat this as a negotiation anchor, not a valuation). Push on: (1) 1st-floor discount vs higher floors, (2) gas heating capex/operating cost risk, (3) “first occupancy” wording—ask for renovation scope, warranties, and invoices, (4) building reserve and planned works.

Bottom line

This is classic “buy the micro-location” Josefstadt: walkable, transit-rich, and rentable. The main risk isn’t demand — it’s whether the renovation/building economics justify €375k. If it’s quiet (courtyard-facing) and the refurbishment is legit, it’s a strong keep-for-years apartment.

*PROVISIONSFREI*+*FREIER MIETZINS* - 3,5 ZIMMERWOHNUNG IN ABSOLUTER BESTLAGE (*PROVISIONSFREI*+*FREIER MIETZINS* - 3,5 ZIMMERWOHNUNG IN ABSOLUTER BESTLAGE)
€376,859 · 60 m2 · 3 rooms · Vienna, 08. District, Josefstadt

  • Price: €376,859
  • Size: 60 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Lift: Yes (as stated)
  • District: Vienna 8

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Needs work — marketed as a “fixer” and explicitly described as renovation-needed.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (size works; renovation + unknown building specifics can be a pain) Investor: ✔ (central micro-area, “free rent” positioning, easy tenant demand if layout is sane) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (8th district + huge nearby food/transit density; great daily-life convenience)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — prime Josefstadt micro-area is the real asset here, but the renovation scope + missing hard building/operating facts means price must reflect uncertainty.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €340k–€365k (best-effort; true comps unknown from the provided data). Anchor hard on renovation cost + risk buffer: “sanierungsbeduerftig”, unknown heating/energy, and unknown monthly costs/reserves. Concrete lever to push: confirm (in writing) what “new windows paid by the owners’ association” actually includes (scope + timing + whether your unit’s share is already funded); if unclear, treat it as a cost risk and discount accordingly.

Bottom line

You’re buying location and future optionality, not current comfort. If the renovation budget is controlled and the building finances are clean, this can be a strong 60sqm city base. If either the building reserves or the noise/orientation disappoint, you overpay fast in this price band.

  • Size: 65.2 m2

  • Lift: Yes (as stated)
  • Outdoor space: balkon
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 8

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready: marketed as first occupancy after full renovation; main caveat is the balcony is “approved” but not necessarily built yet.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms, 65 sqm can work for a couple + baby, but not a long-term family layout) Investor: ✔ (Josefstadt demand + renovation + lift; watch gas heating + tenant/exit strategy) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (very walkable, cafés/theaters, quick U6/U2 access, classic old-building vibe)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — attractive Josefstadt period building + lift + renovated, but you’re paying “fresh renovation / best location” pricing and the balcony value depends on real deliverability/cost.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort): €345k–€365k. If competing interest is real and the renovation quality checks out in person, stretch toward €370k, but insist on clarity around the balcony (scope, timeline, contractor, cost). Comps not provided here, so this range is a risk-adjusted guess.

Bottom line

You’re buying the neighborhood first (8th is a strong live-in district), then the building category (circa-1900 period building with lift), then the “newly renovated” interior. Don’t overpay for a balcony that exists only on paper—treat it like an option with execution risk. Given gas heating and inner-city old-building realities, focus your diligence on building reserves/roof/courtyard works and renovation documentation.

Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €399,000
  • Size: 52.6 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • District: Vienna 8

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — headline sounds “ready,” but you’re missing the key reality checks (condition, floor/lift, courtyard vs street exposure, terrace orientation).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms + ~53 sqm is tight long-term; terrace helps but it’s a compact footprint) Investor: ➖ (8th district rents well, but terrace premium can be hard to price; check building state + costs) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (central Josefstadt + terrace is a strong “liveability” combo if it’s quiet and sunny)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location and terrace, but the price looks “emotionally priced” unless the terrace is genuinely usable and the building is in strong shape.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €360k–€385k (best-effort guess; true comps unknown from the provided data). Anchor on: missing floor/lift info, potential street noise on Lerchenfelder Street, and any terrace limitations (north-facing, small depth, overlooked, water ingress risk). Make the offer conditional on viewing + document review.

Bottom line

If the terrace is sunny/quiet and the apartment isn’t a dark courtyard box, this is a very livable “Vienna core” buy. If it’s street-loud or low-floor without proper insulation, you’re paying prime-district money for daily annoyance. Monthly costs look reasonable on paper — but verify what’s included and whether capex (roof/façade/lift) is coming.

Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €399,000
  • Size: 65 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 8

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (renovated), but confirm what “renovated” includes (windows/electrics/bath) and the real noise level despite courtyard orientation.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms / 65 sqm can work for a couple + baby, but it’s tight long-term) Investor: ➖ (easy-to-rent micro-location, but check running costs + heating type + any Guertel discount on resale) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, transit-heavy, lots of food/parks; courtyard calm is the key swing factor)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong district + infrastructure, but Guertel-adjacent addresses often need a price haircut unless the courtyard truly delivers silence.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €365k–€385k (best-effort guess; comps and building condition/fees are unknown from the extract). Push on: missing monthly costs, reserve fund status, heating system age (individual heating), and any window/acoustic upgrades. If floor level is low or windows are older, demand a bigger discount.

Bottom line

Josefstadt is a “default good” district for living and renting — the micro-location metrics scream convenience. The address near the Guertel can be either a bargain (if hofruhelage is real) or a permanent resale drag (if noise/air quality seep in). Your decision hinge: courtyard quality + building finances + total monthly carrying costs.

#254
Renovierte 2-rooms-apartment in Hofruhelage mit Top-Infrastruktur - 1080 Vienna! (Renovierte 2-Zimmer-Wohnung in Hofruhelage mit Top-Infrastruktur - 1080 Wien!)
€399,000 · 65 m2 · 2 rooms · Hernalser Guertel 2, Vienna, 08. District, Josefstadt
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Address/map mismatch flagged (likely approximate pin).
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View

  • Size: 65 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 8

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — renovated and furnished, but the fixed-term tenant means you may not control move-in timing.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms/65sqm can work for a couple + baby, but not a long-term family layout) Investor: ✔ (already rented until 08.04.2028; strong transit + amenity density) Lifestyle buyer: ➖ (great inner-city connectivity; Guertel-adjacent address is a psychological/traffic-noise risk even with courtyard orientation)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location + courtyard quiet is compelling, but tenant lock-in and weak energy performance should be priced in.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €360,000–€385,000 (comps not provided; this range is a risk-adjusted bid for (a) fixed-term rental until 2028, (b) individual heating running-cost uncertainty, (c) older/inefficient energy profile). Push for: clear rental terms (net rent, deposits, indexation), documented renovation scope, and confirmation that “Hofruhelage” is real in practice (windows/courtyard exposure). If owner-occupier: treat any “tenant may move out” as non-binding unless contractually secured.

Bottom line

2-room in the 8th with strong daily-life convenience — the micro-area is doing heavy lifting here. The tenant situation is either a feature (investor) or the main deal-breaker (self-use). Energy/heat setup reads like higher running costs and future capex risk — don’t pay ‘premium-renovated’ pricing without proof and discounts.

#255
"Investieren in Wiener Altbaucharme - 136 m2 im 8. district - unbefristet vermietet - approx. EUR 3.000/m²!" ("Investieren in Wiener Altbaucharme - 136 m² im 8. Bezirk - unbefristet vermietet - ca. EUR 3.000/m²!")
€440,000 · 136.65 m2 · 5 rooms · Vienna, 08. District, Josefstadt
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €440,000
  • Size: 136.65 m2
  • Rooms: 5
  • Lift: Yes (as stated)
  • District: Vienna 8

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — physically it’s a big period building with lift, but functionally it’s “not move-in” because it’s unbefristet rented (since 1962).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (you can’t realistically plan to live there) Investor: ✔ (only if you accept long duration + low current yield) Lifestyle buyer: ❌ (you’re buying a financial instrument, not a home)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong micro-location and huge size, but the unbefristet tenancy + very low income relative to price is the whole story.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €370k–€405k (best-effort guess; no comps provided). Anchor hard on the long-term sitting-tenant risk, unclear reserve fund (Hausverwaltung change), and high HWB (202.6). Push for: (1) full tenancy file + rent details, (2) repair reserve statement once available, (3) building works plan + allocation (fassade), (4) confirmation of no “Weitergaberecht”-type agreements in writing.

Bottom line

This is a Josefstadt/new-build-edge land-banking play at ~€3.2k/sqm — cheap for 1080 on paper. But the cashflow is tiny (net rent ~€7.3k/yr) and the tenant duration risk is massive. Buy only if your horizon is long and you price the risk correctly; otherwise, it’s a value trap with a pretty period building label.

€449,000 · 43 m2 · 2 rooms · Fuhrmannsgasse, Vienna, 08. District, Josefstadt
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Address/map mismatch flagged (likely approximate pin).
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €449,000
  • Size: 43 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 8

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (newly renovated/first occupancy vibe), but confirm commissioning + snag list completion.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (43 sqm / 2 rooms is tight) Investor: ➖ (liquidity is strong in the 8th, but price-per-sqm and small-layout constraints cap upside) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (quiet courtyard + Josefstadt walkability = easy daily life)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location and “new-in-old” appeal, but you’re paying a premium and the key unknowns can bite (light, building finances, heating system reality).

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €415k–€435k (best-effort guess; true comps unknown from provided data). Lead with: (1) price-per-sqm premium for a small unit, (2) missing monthly costs/reserves info, (3) courtyard orientation/light uncertainty. Make the offer conditional on: documented Betriebskosten + Ruecklage, final as-built specs, and a clean handover protocol. Concrete lever: ask for the latest owners’ meeting minutes + reserve fund status; if reserves are thin after the refurbishment, that’s immediate discount fuel.

Bottom line

If you want “Josefstadt life on foot” and quiet nights, this is a strong candidate. But the deal can turn bad fast if the courtyard is dim/noisy or if the building economics (reserves, operating costs, pellet heating maintenance) are unfriendly. Pay for the location, not for marketing prose—make them prove the numbers.

Move-in readiness

Depends — missing key listing details (condition, floor, light, building state), but individual heating often implies older stock with variable upgrade needs.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ✔

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location is the product here, but the ad is data-poor; price only makes sense if the unit is bright, quiet enough, and the building + heating setup aren’t liabilities.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €420k–€445k (best-effort guess; no comps provided). Push hardest on unknowns: any pending facade/roof/lift works, reserve fund level, window quality, inner-courtyard vs street exposure, and individual heating age/service history. If any of those are weak, price needs to move.

Bottom line

You’re buying one of Vienna’s easiest daily-life districts: food density is huge, transit is strong, parks nearby. At 55 sqm, every flaw is amplified (noise, dark layout, bad storage). Proceed only if the apartment itself “feels” premium — otherwise you’re overpaying for the postcode alone.

€468,000 · 72 m2 · 3 rooms · Bennoplatz 1A, Vienna, 08. District, Josefstadt
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Address/map mismatch flagged (likely approximate pin).
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €468,000
  • Size: 72 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Floor: ground floor
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 8

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (fresh renovation), but I’d double-check heating system details and ground-floor noise/privacy before calling it “done”.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (good 3-room size, but ground floor + street-facing windows can be a lifestyle tax) Investor: ➖ (Josefstadt is liquid, but ground-floor resale/tenant profile + cap-rate sensitivity) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (beautiful classic-period building vibe + strong food/transit density)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong micro-location and “period building done right”, but ground floor + potential street impact means you should pay a discount versus comparable 1st/2nd-floor units.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €430k–€455k (best-effort guess; without true comps and exact condition docs, treat this as a negotiation anchor, not a valuation). Push on: (1) Erdgeschoss discount, (2) street-facing west orientation (heat/noise), (3) any mismatch between “floor heating” marketing and the stated individual heating, (4) building reserve/upcoming works. If they won’t move on price, ask for seller-covered closing/admin costs or a documented defects warranty.

Bottom line

2,400m-ish “pretty renovated period building” energy at a highly livable node: transit at the door, food everywhere, parks nearby. The single big question is ground-floor reality (privacy, noise, security, daylight) — if that feels fine on-site, it’s a very compelling Josefstadt home. Price needs to reflect that you’re buying charm + convenience, not elevation and quiet.

  • Price: €476,000
  • Size: 68 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Heating: individual heating

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Needs work — sold “renovation required”, plus balcony construction is about to start, so you’re buying into disruption.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (great micro-location, but 68 sqm/3 rooms + renovation + construction timing can be stressful) Investor: ➖ (8th is liquid, but unknown running costs/energy + renovation capex can erase yield) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Josefstadt walkability + two balconies is a rare quality-of-life upgrade)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong area and future outdoor space, but the unknown total renovation + gas heating/energy situation + active works create real price and timing risk.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort, comps not provided): €430k–€455k. Anchor with (1) renovation scope uncertainty, (2) missing energy certificate, (3) balcony works risk (noise, access, scaffolding, dust) and clarify who bears what costs and what exactly is delivered at handover. Concrete lever tied to a verifiable uncertainty: request written confirmation of balcony works scope, timeline, warranties, and the exact financial impact on the apartment (special assessments / reserve usage / ongoing maintenance). If it’s vague, that’s your discount.

Bottom line

You’re paying for Josefstadt and the “two balconies” story — but you’re also accepting renovation execution risk and near-term construction chaos. If the layout is efficient and the balconies are truly additive (orientation + quiet side), this can become a very livable long-term home. If not, you can easily overpay after renovation, especially with gas heating and unknown building economics.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (positioned as first-occupancy after renovation), but treat “newly renovated” as a quality-risk until you see invoices, warranties, and snag-list items.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (3 rooms on ~66 sqm can work, but layout + building lift/noise matter a lot and aren’t stated) Investor: ➖ (strong location liquidity, but electric heating + unknown energy metrics can cap rent appetite and resale premium) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Josefstadt, dense food/transit/parks within 300m—this is classic “walk everywhere” Vienna living)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — the micro-location is a weapon, but electric heating + missing essentials (floor, energy class, building condition) mean you should buy it only if the renovation is verifiably high-quality and the building checks out.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort): €435k–€465k (comps not provided, so this is a risk-adjusted bid accounting for electric heating + information gaps). Ask for: renovation scope/invoices, electrical load proof (heating), building reserve fund status, planned works, and a full operating-cost breakdown—then price the unknowns.

Bottom line

Josefstadt + 3 rooms is a rare combo that stays liquid. Electric heating is the silent value-killer unless the apartment is exceptionally insulated and the system is modern. This is a “pay for the location, discount the risk” deal—don’t pay top-of-market without documentation.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (fresh full renovation + new kitchen/windows), with a “depends” flag on running costs/comfort due to the heating + hot-water setup.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (3 rooms works, but 66 sqm is compact and building/floor details matter) Investor: ✔ (Josefstadt demand + transit density; but watch tenant acceptance of electric/infrared heating) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (central, walkable, lots of food/parks nearby, strong tram/U6 access)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — excellent micro-location and renovation story, but the electric hot water + infrared hybrid heating and unknown floor/building specifics are the leverage points.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €445k–€465k (best-effort; I don’t have true comps/building details). Push on (1) heating system type and expected annual electricity costs, (2) energy performance proof, (3) building condition/reserve fund, (4) floor/noise exposure despite triple glazing. If they won’t move on price, ask for seller-funded upgrades (e.g., documented energy assessment, heating optimization, or a cash concession).

Bottom line

If you want “Vienna inner-district living” without the 1st district premium, this is the kind of address that stays liquid. The renovation can be real value — but only if the building + running-cost profile checks out. Treat the heating/hot-water choice as the deal’s risk knob: price it in or walk.

€490,000 · 85 m2 · 3 rooms · Vienna, 08. District, Josefstadt
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €490,000
  • Size: 85 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Lift: Yes (as stated)
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 8

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Real-estate agent / developer-brain take: classic period building in Josefstadt with strong micro-location metrics; likely a “buy the layout + light, then upgrade” play.

Move-in readiness

Depends — livable, but kitchen “Altbestand” + individual heating screams near-term modernization if you want comfort/efficiency.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (good parks/walkability, but 3 rooms / one smaller hofseitig room + unknown floor/noise details)

Investor: ➖ (claimed ~4% net yield is interesting, but period building capex + tenant/renovation risk can eat it)

Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (high ceilings ~3.15m, big windows, dual aspect street/courtyard, proper 8th district vibe)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great area and “bones”, but you should price in renovations and the operational downside of individual heating.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort guess; comps not provided): €450k–€475k. Anchor on (1) kitchen/bath refresh scope, (2) any wiring/plumbing risk typical for Gruenderzeit stock, (3) heating system cost/comfort, (4) floor/lift reality + noise exposure if street-facing rooms matter to you. Ask for: recent building maintenance plan, reserve fund status, any façade/roof/stairwell works, and last major in-building upgrades.

Bottom line

This is a location-forward purchase: you’re buying Josefstadt walkability (food/drink density, parks, transit) and period building volume.

If you’re renovation-tolerant, it’s a strong long-term hold; if you want “plug-and-play”, you’ll overpay unless the condition is better than the text implies.

Do a noise check at rush hour + late evening, and confirm the exact floor/orientation before getting emotionally attached.

€498,000 · 77 m2 · 3 rooms · Piaristengasse, Vienna
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €498,000
  • Size: 77 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • District: Vienna 8

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — layout + light sound good, but the kitchen/bath/WC being “from the 80s” screams medium renovation unless you’re unusually tolerant.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (works for a small family, but 77 sqm + one room accessed via living room can be limiting long-term) Investor: ➖ (8th is liquid, but capex on wet rooms + kitchen can hurt yields) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Josefstadt + walkable food/market/transit mix is the whole point here)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong micro-location and a sensible 3-room size, but dated wet areas and some listing-cost ambiguity should translate into a real discount.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €450k–€475k (best-effort guess; without verified comps for similar 77 sqm 3-room units in 1080 with lift, treat this as a starting anchor, not a valuation). Push hard on (1) 80s kitchen/bath/WC replacement, (2) any upcoming building works, (3) clarified monthly running costs (operating costs vs reserve vs heating/Warmwasser advances). Ask for the energy certificate, reserve-fund status, and last owners’ meeting minutes before you move your offer.

Bottom line

Two big positives: 8th district walkability + 4th-floor-with-lift brightness/quiet courtyard kitchen. Two big risks: renovation budget creep in wet rooms, and “quirky” internal circulation (one room via living room) affecting resale and daily comfort. If the light is real and the building is genuinely well-run, this can be a very livable city base — at the right price.

Move-in readiness

Depends — looks well kept but expect selective updates and a layout decision (2-bed vs restore 3rd room).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (works if you rebuild the extra room; rooftop top-floor living can be love/hate with kids) Investor: ✔ (8th district + strong transit/amenities; layout flexibility helps liquidity) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (quiet street, top-floor light, walkable daily life, campus/medical hub vibe)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong location and “top-floor with elevator” appeal, but partial modernization + gas heating + 1990s rooftop build details should be priced in.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €460k–€485k (best-effort guess; true comps unknown from the provided data). Push hardest on: (1) modernization budget, (2) any rooftop/extension maintenance history, (3) energy performance, (4) prove the 3-room reinstatement cost/timeline in writing. Ask for building reserve status and recent major works to avoid surprise special assessments.

Bottom line

This is a “city-core, top-floor calm” play: you’re paying for district + convenience, not for turnkey finishes. If the living room feels genuinely bright and the bedroom is properly quiet, it’s a keeper. Buy it only if the renovation + ongoing costs still land you below the cost of a truly turnkey 8th-district alternative.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (3 rooms and 84.5 sqm works, but layout + noise tolerance near dense amenities will decide) Investor: ➖ (strong tenant appeal in the 8th, but heating setup and unknown energy data add risk) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (classic inner-district living: restaurants, parks, transit density suggests a very walkable daily life)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location and a “proper” 3-room size, but you should price in unknown renovation/operating-cost risk and use it as leverage.

Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking

  • Price: €499,000
  • Size: 103.84 m2
  • Rooms: 4
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 8

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — the “design freedom / old-building dream” language plus individual heating strongly suggests you should budget for meaningful modernization before it feels turnkey.

Buyer fit

Family: ✔ (4 rooms + 104 sqm in a highly walkable pocket is a real lifestyle win if layout works) Investor: ➖ (good demand area, but capex + old-building unknowns can crush near-term yield) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Josefstadt micro-location + restaurant/transit density = high daily quality-of-life)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the area is premium and the size is strong for the money, but the likely renovation scope and “old building” risk stack mean you should buy only at a price that leaves room for surprises.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort): €455k–€485k (comps not provided, so treat this as a renovation-risk haircut rather than a market-accurate comp-based value). Anchor on: individual heating (running cost/upgrade path), expected electrics/plumbing/windows work, and any building common-area/roof/façade backlog; ask for building reserve status and recent/proposed works and price accordingly.

Bottom line

Prime 1080 walkability signals (food, parks, transit) + a rare 4-room / ~104 sqm footprint. But the pitch screams “project,” so your win condition is buying it like a project: discount + contingency + disciplined scope. If you want turnkey, you’ll overpay emotionally here.

Move-in readiness

Depends — missing key facts (condition, floor, lift, heating/energy), but the price/sqm suggests it’s not a turnkey luxury flip.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (3 rooms and ~95 sqm works, but unknown floor/lift + green view could come with trade-offs like lower floor or courtyard) Investor: ➖ (strong district demand, but 3-room @ ~95 sqm is less liquid than smaller units; yield depends heavily on building costs/energy) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Josefstadt + “green view” + very high amenity density is the core value proposition)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — location fundamentals look excellent, but too many unknowns to pay full ask without tightening the fact base.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €450k–€480k (best-effort guess; comps not provided). Anchor on missing energy/heating data, unclear floor/lift, and any noise/light/privacy risks; treat the “green view” as valuable only after you verify it’s real year-round and not a future construction risk.

Bottom line

You’re paying for Josefstadt walkability: tons of food/drink, parks, and transit within 300m. At this size, the winner move is diligence: confirm building quality + operating costs before you fall in love with the “green view.” If the view is quiet + bright and the building is solid, this could be a long-term hold in a top-tier micro-area.

Move-in readiness

Needs work — this reads like a renovation project being sold with a plan, not a finished home.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (great size/room count, but renovation time/uncertainty is a real tax) Investor: ➖ (could work, but 8th district pricing + renovation risk narrows the margin) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (prime, walkable micro-area; best if you want to shape the layout/finish)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong location and a rare 4-room footprint, but the renovation scope + unknown building details should be priced in.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — the “prunkvoll period building” vibe can be gorgeous, but without condition/renovation details it’s either turnkey-boutique or lipstick-on-an-old-shell.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (48.7 sqm / 2 rooms is tight as a long-term family home) Investor: ➖ (strong micro-location demand, but price-per-sqm risk is real) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (prime inner-district living, walk-out-the-door amenities)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — stellar Josefstadt micro-area + underfloor heating, but 609k for 48.7 sqm needs justification (layout, light, building condition, and floor).

Negotiation strategy

Ask for proof-points: exact floor + lift, window orientation, noise (street/courtyard), renovation year/spec, building maintenance status, and any planned works. Comps are unknown from the data provided, so price confidence is limited; best-effort target range: €560k–€590k (offer anchored lower if it’s low-floor/street-facing or if the building has upcoming capex).

Bottom line

You’re paying for Josefstadt more than for square meters. If the layout is efficient and it’s quiet/bright, this can be an A+ city base. If it’s dark/noisy or the building is heading into major works, the price starts to look punitive.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ✔ Lifestyle buyer: ➖

  • Size: 31.09 m2
  • Rooms: 1

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — “modernised” is promising, but expect extra scrutiny on electrical heating + building condition (courtyard-facing can hide noise but also light issues).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (too small / 1-room) Investor: ✔ (U3 adjacency + courtyard-side = strong rental liquidity if the building/energy story is clean) Lifestyle buyer: ➖ (works if you want a compact base near transit; less so if you care about sunlight/space)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location and “hofseitig” helps, but 31 sqm + electric heating typically caps resale upside unless the price is sharp.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €175k–€188k (best-effort guess; I do not have comps for this exact street/building). Push on: (1) electric heating running costs, (2) any upcoming building works/reserve fund status, (3) light/quiet in a courtyard-facing unit, (4) rental restrictions and short-term letting rules, (5) proof of what “modernised” includes (electrics, windows, bathroom, floor, plumbing).

Bottom line

If the building is well-run and the unit has decent light, this is a clean “tiny-but-liquid” buy. Electric heating is the permanent drag: negotiate like it’s your monthly bill forever. Buy it for simplicity and transit access, not for spacious living or big appreciation.

  • Size: 41.65 m2
  • Rooms: 1
  • Heating: individual heating

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Needs work — this reads like a true renovation project (not just cosmetic), but the fundamentals (size, cost base, transit/amenities) make it a viable “fix-and-hold.”

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (1 room; this is a solo/couple-at-most layout) Investor: ✔ (renovation upside + strong everyday amenity density; rentability depends on building/legal constraints) Lifestyle buyer: ➖ (great if you enjoy a gritty, connected micro-area and can handle renovation logistics)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the upside is real, but “sanierungsbeduerftig” + unknown floor/building condition means you must price in risk and timeline.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €170k–€190k (best-effort guess; true comps unknown). Use a contractor walkthrough to itemize: wiring/plumbing, windows, floors, bathroom/kitchen, and heating system status. Push hard on any building-level capex signals (stairwell/roof/facade, damp) and on the uncertainty around floor/exposure. If the seller won’t move, only proceed if the renovation budget still keeps your all-in cost attractive versus renovated 3rd-district studios.

Bottom line

Good 3rd-district “bones” on paper: compact 41.65 sqm, low monthly costs, and lots of daily-life infrastructure nearby. The risk is hidden in what you can’t see yet (house condition, floor/light/noise, and the true renovation scope). Treat this like a numbers deal: buy only if your all-in (purchase + taxes/fees + renovation) still beats a ready-to-live alternative.

Move-in ready (likely): “modernisiert” + small 1-room usually means cosmetic refresh done, but verify electrics/plumbing + any damp/mold risk in courtyard-facing units.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (too small) Investor: ✔ (U3 adjacency + 31 sqm is classic rental/liquidity format) Lifestyle buyer: ➖ (quiet courtyard-side is nice, but it’s a compact studio)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — good micro-location and rental-friendly size, but the premium for “U3 + modernized” can be overstated; you want proof on building condition, heating, and true 2nd-floor access (lift?).

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €190k–€205k (best-effort guess; I don’t have comps for this exact pocket/building). Anchor with: (1) individual heating operating cost uncertainty, (2) any lift absence (2nd floor hurts liquidity), (3) courtyard-facing can mean less light, (4) monthly costs vs. rent ceiling for 31 sqm.

Bottom line

You’re buying a “Vienna studio near U3” product — usually easy to rent and resell. Courtyard-facing is a stealth advantage if street noise is real, but check daylight and any courtyard service noise. If the building/common areas look tired, price it like a building-risk asset, not a turnkey boutique unit.

€235,000 · 35 m2 · 2 rooms · Apostelgasse 17, Vienna, 03. District, Landstrasse
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Address/map mismatch flagged (likely approximate pin).
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €235,000
  • Size: 35 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — marketed as “first occupancy after full renovation”, but the listing data you have is missing the hard proof points (energy/heating, floor/lift, orientation, actual scope of works).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (35 sqm / 2 rooms is functionally a compact couple’s place at best) Investor: ➖ (great district + small ticket, but high monthly costs for size and unknown rental/legal setup) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (if you want a small, central-ish base near Rochusmarkt and don’t need quiet/space)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — good location and “freshly renovated” can be a sweet spot, but at 235k for 35 sqm you’re paying a premium while key risk variables are blank.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort, comps unknown): €205k–€225k. Use the missing facts as leverage: ask for heating type + energy certificate, floor/lift, window orientation/street noise, and exact breakdown of the €203.52 monthly costs (incl. reserve fund and any special levies). If any of those come back unfavorable (electric heating, noisy street, weak reserves), push to the low end or walk.

Bottom line

Small, renovated 1030 units can resell well — but only if the building is solid and the apartment isn’t a noise/heat trap. Two ways this goes wrong: (1) you discover it’s a loud street-facing unit (Apostel Lane can be fine, but you need orientation) and sleep quality tanks; (2) the building’s reserve fund is weak / there’s a looming repair (roof/façade/lift) and your “cheap” buy becomes expensive via levies. The €203.52 monthly costs on 35 sqm is the eyebrow-raiser: either the building is cost-heavy or the reserve contributions are chunky — get the components and let that steer your offer.

Move-in readiness

Depends — no condition/specs given; treat as “unknown until you see photos + finish level + building state”.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (32.74 sqm / 2 rooms is tight, resale pool is narrower) Investor: ✔ (small unit + District 3 + high amenity/transit density reads like rental demand) Lifestyle buyer: ➖ (great urban base, but very small; noise/light/privacy can make or break it)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the micro-location signals are strong, but the listing is information-light (costs/energy/heating/condition), which is exactly where overpriced “invest & profit” units hide risk.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €245k–€265k (best-effort guess; comps unknown, so this is mainly an “info-risk discount”). Push for: (1) full operating costs (incl. reserve contribution), (2) heating system + hot water type, (3) energy certificate, (4) building reserve status + planned works. If any red flags (high monthly costs, weak reserves, upcoming facade/roof/lift work), ask for a concrete € reduction tied to those numbers.

Bottom line

This is a “location-first” micro-apartment: it can be a clean little cashflow/exit play, or a headache if the building economics are ugly. Your upside is liquidity (small units move) — your downside is hidden monthly costs and compromised liveability (noise/overheating/dark courtyard view). If the building is healthy and the unit is bright/quiet, it’s a defensible buy; if not, it’s a pass fast.

  • If the pin is not confirmed, treat nearby-amenity context as approximate and verify the exact micro-location.

Fit (who this suits)

€296,000 · 43.01 m2 · 1 rooms · Vienna, 03. District, Landstrasse

  • Size: 43.01 m2
  • Rooms: 1
  • Heating: Elektroheizung

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — “modernised” sounds close, but key unknowns (floor, building condition, windows, wiring, bathroom/kitchen scope) can flip this.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ✔

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — great transit + dense amenity pocket, but 43 sqm / “1.5 rooms” + electric heating means you should be price-disciplined.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €270k–€285k (best-effort guess; comps not provided). Push hardest on (1) electric heating running costs + tenant/exit liquidity concerns, (2) missing floor info and any noise exposure (U3 proximity), (3) building reserve / upcoming works, (4) exact modernisation year + invoices. If the building is freshly refurbished, higher end of range; if street-facing/noisy or weak building finances, push below.

Bottom line

2–3rd district + U3 access is a real quality-of-life cheat code. This reads like a high-convenience city base, not a forever-home. Make the deal only if noise, heating costs, and building health check out — otherwise keep shopping; there are many similar units competing for the same buyer.

#286
"Village im Dritten - PARK HOMES" provisionsfreies Eigentums-Mikroapartment (!) Top 62 ("Village im Dritten - PARK HOMES" provisionsfreies Eigentums-Mikroapartment (Erstbezug!) Top 62)
€299,000 · 36.28 m2 · 1 rooms · Vienna, 03. District, Landstrasse
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Address/map mismatch flagged (likely approximate pin).
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €299,000
  • Size: 36.28 m2
  • Rooms: 1
  • Heating: Erdwaerme
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (new build / first occupancy signals), but verify handover quality and final snag list.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (too small / studio format) Investor: ✔ (high-liquidity micro-unit in a dense amenity + transit pocket) Lifestyle buyer: ➖ (great city-base; can feel tight as a primary home)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong location fundamentals, but micro-apartment pricing is unforgiving and exit depends on building quality + resale demand.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €270k–€290k (best-effort; comps not provided, so treat this as a positioning range, not a valuation). Push hardest on (1) developer incentives (fixtures upgrade, storage inclusion, closing cost contributions) and (2) any uncertainty you can document: final operating costs, building reserve funding, or handover defects.

Bottom line

This is a “city-core micro” play: you’re paying for walkability, park access, and transit density. If the building is well-run, it’s an easy-to-rent, easy-to-exit unit. If monthly costs or building management turn out heavy, the resale math gets ugly fast at this size/price.
  • Area signal: Very strong everyday convenience (lots of food/drink and groceries nearby), plus parks and solid transit density — you’ll live mostly on foot.
  • Product signal: Micro-apartment + first occupancy often means efficient layout and modern systems; it also means you must inspect noise, ventilation, and finishing tolerance carefully.
  • Two ways this deal can go wrong:
    1. Operating costs land higher than expected (common with new developments: management fees, reserve contributions, shared amenities), compressing rental yield and resale appeal.
    2. Noise/comfort mismatch (street orientation, neighboring units, or building acoustics) makes the space feel smaller and increases tenant churn or forces discounting.
  • One concrete negotiation lever: Make the offer conditional on receiving the full breakdown of monthly operating costs + reserve funding plan; if either is above your threshold, you either re-price or require concessions (fixtures upgrades / storage / closing cost support).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (44.6 sqm, 2-room) Investor: ✔ (liquid format, strong transit access; watch rent-cap/regulation + heating perception) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (quiet courtyard-side + dense daily amenities)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — good “quiet-but-central” setup in 1030 at a digestible ticket size, but electric heating + unknown building/energy details should be priced in.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €270k–€290k (best-effort without comps; if condition is truly turnkey and building is solid, you may end up closer to ask). Push for (1) proof of recent electrical/heating updates, (2) clear monthly running-cost breakdown, (3) any planned building works (roof/façade/risers), and (4) noise/light confirmation despite courtyard orientation.

Bottom line

Quiet courtyard-side in a very convenient micro-area is the headline. Electric heating is the value-killer unless it’s a well-executed setup (insulation/windows matter more than brochures). At this size, resale liquidity is usually good — but only if the building story checks out.

Depends — likely fine cosmetically, but period building + individual heating means you must verify plumbing/electrics/heating age and upcoming building works.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (45 sqm / 2 rooms is tight long-term) Investor: ➖ (good location liquidity, but unit size + heating system + unknown energy data can cap rent/exit multiple) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, lots of transit + parks + daily errands nearby)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — location fundamentals look strong, but the “beautiful old building” headline can hide expensive building-level maintenance and comfort/utility trade-offs.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €270k–€290k (best-effort guess; comps not provided, so treat this as a negotiation anchor, not a valuation). Ask for: repair reserve status, planned works (roof/façade/stairwell), and recent minutes from owners’ meetings.

Bottom line

This reads like a classic “small period building jewel” in a very livable part of 1030. Your real risk isn’t the apartment — it’s the building and the heating setup. If documentation is clean and no major works are pending, it’s a solid buy at the right number; otherwise, price it like a project even if it looks pretty.

Family: ❌ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ✔

Move-in readiness

Needs work — “sanierungsbeduerftig” plus unknown energy rating suggests a real renovation, not paint-and-go.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (4 rooms on ~72 m² can work, but renovation + likely tighter room sizes) Investor: ➖ (good demand node near U3, but capex + heating uncertainty can eat returns) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Donaukanal + dense amenities + transit = strong daily-life convenience)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — location + U3 is a durable base, but the renovation scope and building economics are the whole deal.

Negotiation strategy

Ask for a full “what exactly is broken” package: recent operating cost breakdown, reserve fund status, planned works, and any recent quotes/photos of the defects. Target price range (best-effort guess): €260k–€290k (comps unknown; this range assumes meaningful renovation spend and some pricing for uncertainty). One concrete lever: tie your offer to verifiable building costs—if reserve fund is thin or major works are scheduled (roof/facade/riser pipes), price that risk in immediately.

Bottom line

You’re buying the map-pin: near Donaukanal with strong transit/amenity density. The trap is underestimating the renovation + “individual heating” implications (comfort, running costs, future system upgrades). If the building finances look weak, this can flip from bargain to money pit fast.

€320,000 · 52.46 m2 · 2 rooms · Ljuba-Welitsch-Promenade 22, Vienna, 03. District, Landstrasse
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €320,000
  • Size: 52.46 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: district heating
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — reads like a newer "project" style flat; without floor/condition/specs it’s hard to call turnkey.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms / ~52 sqm is tight unless it’s just a couple + baby) Investor: ✔ (size/liquidity good; transit-heavy micro-area helps rentability) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (parks + decent daily-life amenities, low tourist pressure)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the area signals are strong (parks + transit) but missing key listing facts (floor, running costs, energy rating, condition) = price risk.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €295k–€315k (best-effort guess; comps unknown). Ask for (1) exact floor + orientation, (2) full operating costs incl. repair reserve, (3) building year + elevator, (4) energy certificate, (5) heating/hot water split for district heating, and negotiate on any uncertainty.

Bottom line

This is the classic "easy-to-live-in" 2-room play: parks nearby, lots of transit, not touristy. The danger is paying "new-build marketing" pricing without getting the boring-but-critical numbers. If floor/orientation and monthly costs check out, it’s a very defendable buy at the right entry price.

€330,000 · 34 m2 · 2 rooms · Schnirchgasse, TrIIIple Vienna, Vienna
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €330,000
  • Size: 34 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: district heating
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — likely turnkey as a newer branded complex, but the listing data is missing key condition/fit details.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (34 m² + 2 rooms reads more like a compact couple/single setup) Investor: ✔ (high-liquidity micro-unit near transit; but yield depends heavily on monthly costs + building fees) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (rooftop pool + shared amenities + lots of nearby food/transit)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — the “TrIIIple / rooftop pool” premium is real, but €330k for 34 m² is only defensible if running costs and resale liquidity check out.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €300k–€315k (best-effort guess; no comps provided, so treat this as a starting anchor). Ask for (1) full monthly operating costs incl. repair reserve, (2) proof of reserve fund health + upcoming capex, (3) rules/fees for amenities (pool, common rooms) and any special assessments. Use missing data (floor, monthly costs, energy class) as your leverage: “price can’t be final until the cost stack is transparent.”

Bottom line

You're buying a lifestyle-branded micro-apartment, not space. This deal works if you value amenities + location more than €/m² optics. It goes sideways if the building-fee stack is heavy or the unit’s orientation is noisy/hot.

  • Size: 68.59 m2

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — “well-kept older building” is promising, but unit condition is unknown and individual-floor heating often hints at uneven renovation quality.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (68.6 sqm can work for a small family, but layout/building specifics matter a lot) Investor: ✔ (3 rooms + strong transit + everyday amenities; watch heating/maintenance risk) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (lots of food/drink, parks, and transit nearby = easy daily life)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location fundamentals look strong, but you need to validate the apartment’s condition, heating setup, and building reserves before paying “nice listing” pricing.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €300k–€320k (best-effort guess; I don’t have recent comparable sales here). Anchor on (1) individual-floor heating and its operating/upgrade cost risk, (2) unknown floor/light/noise exposure, and (3) monthly running costs vs. what’s included. Ask for: recent building maintenance history, reserve fund status, any planned major works, and last heating service/age.

Bottom line

• This is a very livable pocket on paper: transit density + groceries + parks = low-friction day-to-day. • The price-per-sqm is plausibly “not crazy” for the district, but condition uncertainty is the swing factor. • Treat it as a value play if the unit is tidy and quiet; pass fast if it’s dark, street-noisy, or needs heating/window upgrades.
