#296
Top aufgeteilte Altbauwohnung mit Eigengarten! Zwischen Rochusmarkt und Kardinal Nagl- Square! (Top aufgeteilte Altbauwohnung mit Eigengarten! Zwischen Rochusmarkt und Kardinal Nagl-Platz!)
€341,000 · 45.74 m2 · 2 rooms · Hainburger Strasse 47, Vienna, 03. District, Landstrasse
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Address/map mismatch flagged (likely approximate pin).
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €341,000
  • Size: 45.74 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: district heating
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — older-building charm plus a private garden can hide moisture/insulation and sound issues; condition matters more than the photos.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (size is tight) Investor: ➖ (great location, but small-unit pricing and resale liquidity depend on layout/light + garden usability) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (city-frictionless living with an outdoor escape)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong micro-location and rare garden for the size, but the “older building + ground-level outdoor space” risk stack needs a price that compensates.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €315k–€332k (best-effort guess; true comps for garden units here are unknown from the provided data). Levers: (1) Ask for documented building reserve level + next 24-month plan (façade/roof/riser lines) and price any upcoming special payments in. (2) Require clear disclosure on garden rights/maintenance obligations and any damp-proofing work history; if unclear, negotiate a holdback or discount. (3) If the unit is effectively ground-floor, push on noise/privacy and security costs (blinds, grilles, garden gate).

Bottom line

This is a “location + outdoor space” play in a very convenient pocket. It wins if the garden is truly usable and the unit has good light + quiet orientation. It loses if damp, traffic noise, or weak building finances show up after you’re emotionally attached.

Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €346,000
  • Size: 54.9 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: district heating
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — “well-laid-out” + district/location signal good, but floor/condition/energy data are missing.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms/55 sqm can work for a couple + baby, tight beyond that) Investor: ✔ (liquid size + strong micro-area amenities; watch yield vs €6.3k/sqm) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, lots of food/parks/transit; likely a classic Vienna “live outside your apartment” area)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — attractive 1030 micro-area and sensible size, but you should price the unknowns (floor, noise, building state, operating costs, energy).

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €325k–€345k (comps unknown; this is a risk-adjusted opening range given missing floor/energy/condition details). Anchor with: (1) floor not stated (value swings a lot), (2) energy class not stated, (3) period building building-capex risk (roof/facade/common areas), (4) confirm green view permanence. Try to win concessions: seller pays for minor fixes / furniture / faster handover rather than only price.

Bottom line

If the “green view” is real and the apartment is quiet, this is a very livable 1030 two-roomer. Your whole decision hinges on three checks: noise (street vs courtyard), floor/lift situation, and the building’s upcoming maintenance. At ~€6.3k/sqm you’re paying for location and charm — make sure you’re not also buying future capex surprises.

Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €349,000
  • Size: 46.01 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — new-build with completion in 2027 means you’re buying a promise + contract terms, not a finished apartment.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms / ~46 sqm is tight long-term; works for a couple with a baby at most) Investor: ➖ (rental demand in 1030 is strong, but 2027 delivery + unknown monthly costs/energy specs add risk) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (garden + central-ish 1030 + lots of parks/transit nearby is a very “Vienna city-with-breathing-space” play)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — attractive concept (garden in 1030, good mobility/green signals), but you need price discipline because timelines/specs/carrying costs are currently opaque.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort, comps unknown): €315k–€335k. If the developer won’t move on price, push hard for value: upgraded specs (flooring/sanitary), guaranteed storage/bike room terms, or buyer-friendly contract clauses. Negotiation lever tied to a verifiable uncertainty: monthly running costs + reserve and the exact garden ownership/use model (exclusive use vs true ownership; maintenance obligations) — these are checkable in the contract/exposé and can justify concessions.

Bottom line

You’re paying for rarity: private outdoor space in a dense district. The deal can be great if the garden is truly usable (sun, privacy, noise) and the contract is clean. The deal can be painful if 2027 slips, costs balloon, or the “garden” behaves like a ground-floor noise funnel.

Move-in readiness

Depends — likely straightforward if this is a newer “Baufeld/Urban Daheim” build, but the listing data is missing key deal-breakers (energy/heating, running costs, floor/lift).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Too small at ~50 sqm / 2 rooms. Investor: ➖ Works on paper (good district, liquid rental market), but only if building fees + energy situation are clean. Lifestyle buyer: ✔ Strong fit if you want an easy, compact city base with parks + transit nearby.

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — nice micro-location signals (parks/transit, low tourist pressure) and a functional layout (winter garden + separate WC), but price-per-sqm feels “new-build confident” and the missing monthly costs/energy data can swing the true value hard.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €330k–€345k (best-effort guess; I don’t have comps from the extract, so treat this as a negotiating anchor, not a valuation). Bottom line

2-room + winter garden in the 3rd can be a great “liveable small” package. This deal is won or lost on building economics (fees/reserve/energy) and whether the winter garden is genuinely usable year-round. At ~€352k for ~50 sqm, you’re paying for convenience + location — make the seller prove it with numbers.

Move-in ready — “” usually means you’re buying a fresh unit, but budget time for snagging/handovers.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (46.47 sqm / 2 rooms is tight long-term) Investor: ✔ (new-build demand + high amenity density can rent well if the micro-details check out) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, park-heavy, lots of food/drink nearby)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong location signals and modern heating, but the price-per-sqm is “new-build premium”, so the deal hinges on orientation/noise and building running costs.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort): €335k–€350k (comps not provided, so this is a risk-adjusted new-build discount ask). Levers: (1) ask for clarity/guarantees on monthly building costs (BK/Ruecklage) before signing; (2) snag list + written timeline for fixes/handovers; (3) if it faces a busy street or has limited daylight, price that in.

Bottom line

This looks like a “pay for convenience + new-build polish” unit in a very walkable part of 1030. Two ways it goes wrong: you discover it’s noisy/dim (courtyard vs street really matters), or the monthly costs/reserve come in higher than expected. If the orientation is calm + bright and the building costs are sensible, it’s a clean lifestyle/investment pick; otherwise, demand a discount or walk.

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready — the language screams “recent full renovation,” but confirm what exactly was renewed (wiring/plumbing/windows) vs just surfaces.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ➖

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — attractive “small but premium” product in a good district, but the price/m² is punchy and the unknown building/reserve story can flip this from gem to headache.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €340k–€355k (best-effort guess; true comps unknown from provided data). Anchor on: missing floor info, unknown monthly costs, unknown energy class, and any courtyard/street noise risk. Concrete lever: ask for the building reserve status + planned works (roof/facade/lift) and tie your offer validity to receiving those documents.

Bottom line

You’re paying for “renovated period building + big outdoor space” more than for raw m². This deal is great if the loggia is genuinely usable/quiet and the building finances are healthy. It goes bad fast if it’s noise-exposed or if the house is heading into expensive common-area works.

(Ein Haus mit Geschichte - Ein Zuhause mit Zukunft! Exklusives TOP-Projekt in 1030 Wien! 14 Wohnträume / Sanierter Altbau + Dachgeschossausbau! Hochwertig. Historisch. Heimelig.)

  • Price: €367,000
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — marketed as renovated historic building + attic conversion, but your unit-level finish/specs and handover timeline are unknown.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (district is strong, but without rooms/sqm it’s hard to judge livability) Investor: ➖ (project branding helps, but rentability/yield can’t be assessed without size/costs/energy) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (3rd district + parks/transit nearby usually means very livable day-to-day)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — appealing micro-location signals and “historic + upgraded” story, but too many hard unknowns (sqm, rooms, floor, monthly costs, energy) to pay full ask blindly.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €330k–€360k (best-effort guess; comps unknown). Ask for the exact unit sheet (sqm, floor, orientation, ceiling height, outdoor space), full running costs, and what is actually included in “high quality” (windows, heating system, sound insulation). If it’s a developer project with multiple units, push for incentives: kitchen package, closing-cost contribution, storage, or a headline discount on less-preferred floors/orientations.

Bottom line

You’re buying the “feel” (renovated old building / cozy vibe) plus a solid 3rd-district baseline. But without the unit basics, this is a brochure, not a decision. Treat it like a project purchase: verify specs, timeline, and total monthly burn before committing.

Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View

  • Size: 47.52 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — physically likely fine (modern kitchen + underfloor heating), but you can’t move in until 10/2027 due to fixed-term tenancy.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ➖

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — good micro-location near canal + transit, but the “rented until 10/2027” constraint should buy you a meaningful discount.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €330k–€355k (best-effort guess; comps and current rent are unknown). Push hard on (1) inability to self-use until 10/2027, (2) any Dachgeschoss heat/noise risk, (3) lack of disclosed running costs/energy data in the extract. Ask for: current rent (net + gross), deposit, tenant payment history, indexation clause, handover condition, and whether there’s any early termination option.

Bottom line

If you’re an investor who likes stable urban demand: this can work — but only at the right price. As a self-user: you’re speculating on 2027, and paying today’s money for delayed utility. Micro-area looks strong (groceries + transit + parks density), so don’t overpay for “nice on paper” finishing.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (could work, but without size/room count it’s a gamble) Investor: ✔ (District 3 + strong transit signal; new-build tends to rent easily) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (high park density + decent food/drink nearby; not touristy)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — attractive macro-location and “easy-living” spec (underfloor heating), but missing core facts (sqm/rooms/floor/timeline) means you should treat the price as an opening number, not a conclusion.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €350k–€370k (best-effort guess; comps are unknown because sqm/room count isn’t provided). New-build in the 3rd is usually a “liquidity” play: easy to sell/rent later if the micro-location is clean. The immediate risk here is not the neighborhood — it’s buying blind on fundamentals (size, floor, facing, timeline). If the unit is small, this price can be fine; if it’s larger or poorly oriented, it’s overpriced fast.

  • Size: 45.43 m2
  • Rooms: 2

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — it’s a 2027 completion, so you’re buying a promise + specs, not a finished reality.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms / ~45 sqm can work for a couple + baby, but it’s tight long-term) Investor: ➖ (new-build can rent well, but pricing + future HOA costs can compress yield) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable “urban village” vibe: parks + transit density looks strong)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — attractive micro-area signals and sensible size for a single/couple, but off-plan delivery risk + unknown running costs mean you should only proceed at the right terms.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €355k–€370k (best-effort guess; comps for this exact project/building are unknown from the provided data). Key asks: lock in a clear spec sheet, penalized delivery timeline, and explicit inclusions/exclusions (finishes, storage, kitchen, outdoor space, cooling). Make your offer conditional on receiving (a) a full cost breakdown (HOA/reserves), (b) energy/heating details, and (c) the exact orientation/floorplan.

Bottom line

This is a “buy the location + masterplan” play: lots of parks nearby, decent groceries, and solid transit within 300m suggest daily life convenience. The deal can go sideways on (1) handover delays / spec downgrades and (2) future monthly costs landing higher than expected for a new development. If the developer/seller can’t give clean documents early, treat that as pricing leverage — or walk.

Move-in readiness

Depends — “comprehensively renovated” suggests low immediate capex, but individual heating can hide future costs/complexity.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms + ~66 sqm is doable for a couple + baby, tight beyond that) Investor: ✔ (transit-heavy 1030 + manageable size; rentability depends on heating + building costs) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (dense daily-life amenities + parks + strong transit)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the micro-location looks excellent and the size is practical, but you want price leverage for individual heating and any unknowns around the “renovation” scope.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €355k–€375k (best-effort guess; true comps not provided). Monthly costs ~€244 help, but don’t let that distract from building-level capex risk.

Bottom line

2-room at ~66 sqm in 1030 with heavy transit + groceries + parks nearby is a very livable setup. The upside is convenience and future connectivity; the downside risk is hidden building/technical realities (heating + what was actually renovated). Negotiate like you’re buying the building’s problems too, not just the flat’s fresh paint.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (works for a couple with one child max; compact layout risk) Investor: ✔ (liquid asset class if the micro-area stays strong; watch total monthly carrying costs) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, park-heavy, good transit density)

Final verdict

If the layout is genuinely efficient and the balcony is quiet, it’s a solid small premium buy in District 3.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (new-build first occupancy), but “ready” depends on what’s included/finished (kitchen, shading, lighting) and handover quality.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (46 sqm / 2 rooms reads as tight for anything beyond 1 adult + occasional guest) Investor: ➖ (strong location liquidity, but price-per-sqm and any rental/HOA details can make returns thin) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (3rd district, high amenity + transit density, parks nearby = easy daily life)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — appealing micro-location and new-build comfort, but at this size the deal lives or dies on total monthly costs, layout efficiency, and building-level quality.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €385k–€405k (comps unknown, so this is a risk-adjusted “try” range rather than a market-anchored valuation). Ask for either a straight price cut or value-adds (parking/bike storage terms, kitchen package, storage unit, closing cost support). Concrete lever: demand written clarity on building reserves/ongoing building costs and what exactly is delivered at handover (kitchen, shading, floor finish, smart-home, warranties) — uncertainty here is monetizable.

Bottom line

You’re paying for the 3rd district + new-build energy system + turnkey convenience. At ~46 sqm, small layout mistakes (dark orientation, awkward kitchen wall, tiny storage) punish resale and renting. Win this by forcing transparency on monthly carrying costs and the real “all-in finish” level.

  • Size: 77.91 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — “new-build” vibe suggests low immediate maintenance, but the unknown floor/orientation + individual heating can swing comfort and running costs.

Buyer fit

Family: ✔ (3 rooms / ~78 sqm + lots of parks nearby reads family-friendly) Investor: ➖ (good liquidity area, but heating setup + exact micro-noise risk matter) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (close-in 3rd district, walkable amenities, still marketed as quiet)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong location + sensible layout size/price, but you’re buying partially blind without floor, sunlight/noise orientation, and building-cost specifics.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €395k–€410k (best-effort guess; true comps not provided). Anchor with uncertainties you can verify: (1) individual heating type/age + last service/renewal cycle, (2) building reserve level and any planned works, (3) real noise exposure (rail/arterials/construction) based on which side the unit faces.

Bottom line

Land Street near Aspanggruende/Eurogate is the kind of “liveable central” that holds value. The deal goes sideways if it’s a low-floor street-facing unit or if the heating system is old/expensive to replace. Make the seller earn the “quiet” claim with orientation + window spec + a proper noise check at rush hour and late evening.

  • Rooms: 3
  • Heating: district heating
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — likely fine to live in, but value hinges on courtyard orientation quality + the “balcony possible” claim actually being executable.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (good size for a small family, but courtyard quiet + schools/daycare logistics and exact layout matter) Investor: ➖ (3rd district is rentable, but resale/rent upside depends on balcony feasibility and building governance) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable micro-area with lots of food/drink/parks; courtyard calm is a strong daily-life perk)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — nice Land Street micro-location and likely quiet green outlook, but you should price it as “no balcony” until permits/approvals are proven.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort guess): €415k–€435k (comps not provided, so this is uncertainty-priced). Ask for: written proof of balcony build feasibility (owner approvals, any prior permits/architect concept, structural constraints), building reserve status + planned works, and a clear breakdown of monthly costs.

Bottom line

You’re buying the combo of 1030 walkability + courtyard peace — that’s real quality of life. The swing factor is the balcony: if it’s real, you’re underpaying; if it’s marketing, you’re overpaying. Lock the downside first, then negotiate hard off uncertainties.

Final verdict

  • Size: 52.11 m2

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — likely fine if it’s a finished unit, but with the listing giving almost no spec (floor, energy class, condition) you need a hard fact-check before assuming turnkey.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (2 rooms / ~52 sqm is tight) Investor: ✔ (strong transit + amenity density; likely easy to rent if building/HOA rules allow) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, lots of food/drink, parks nearby; good “city living” footprint)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — good micro-location and size for a 2-room, but the info gap is big and the €/sqm looks punchy; only pay up if the building quality + unit spec justify it.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €420k–€440k (best-effort guess; true comps unknown from provided data). Push for: exact floor/orientation, noise exposure on Landstrasser Haupt Street, building completion/renovation scope, HOA reserve status, and any short-term rental restrictions. If any of those are weak, treat that as your discount lever.

Bottom line

This is a “buy the address” play: high convenience, easy daily life, likely resilient demand. Main risk is boulevard noise + paying a premium for branding/new-build vibes without seeing the unit details. At €449k for ~52 sqm, you need exceptional execution (layout, light, insulation, common areas) — otherwise negotiate hard or walk.

Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €449,000
  • Size: 58.72 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — new-build vibe signals (underfloor heating, DG maisonette) suggest “easy living”, but the listing extract is missing hard checks (roof/terrace condition, cooling, building reserves).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (2 rooms / ~59 sqm is tight long-term) Investor: ➖ (3rd district liquidity is good, but DG/terrace adds maintenance + tenant-profile constraints) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (DG maisonette + huge loggia is the whole point)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — good micro-location + outdoor space can be a winner, but you’re paying for “DG charm”; make the price reflect summer heat risk, roof/terrace upkeep, and any building capex.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €415k–€440k (comps unknown from the extract; adjust once you see condition, lift status, and recent sale €/sqm nearby). Push hardest on: (1) DG overheating / lack of cooling, (2) any terrace waterproofing history, (3) building reserve + upcoming roof/façade work, (4) total monthly running costs incl. any repair reserve.

Bottom line

If the loggia is truly “useable” (privacy, sun angle, no wind tunnel) this is a lifestyle buy in a strong district. But DG maisonette is a comfort/maintenance trade: verify heat management and the building’s roof/terrace responsibilities before you fall in love. Don’t overpay just because it has outdoor space — price it like a 2-room with risk, not like a unicorn penthouse.

Move-in readiness

Depends — likely a charming, older building core with unknown interior condition; budget for surprises until you see the exact renovation scope.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (69.8 sqm / 2 rooms works for a couple + baby, but not a long-haul family layout) Investor: ➖ (3rd district is liquid, but 2 rooms + older-building risk and unknown energy data can cap yields) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable micro-area with lots of food/drink + parks + solid transit density)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location is doing heavy lifting, but “balcony possible” and missing hard facts (floor detail/condition/energy) are exactly where people overpay.

Negotiation strategy

Bottom line

You’re buying 1030 walkability: restaurants/cafés everywhere, parks nearby, transit options. Two ways this goes wrong: (1) the “south/sunny” claim is diluted by courtyard shading or street canyon; (2) balcony never gets approved or costs far more than expected, killing the lifestyle premium you thought you were paying for. Best lever: make your offer conditional on clear written confirmation of balcony feasibility (building consent / status) and a quick check of building reserves + planned works — if either is weak, your price drops.

€459,900 · 91.41 m2 · 2 rooms · Vienna, 03. District, Landstrasse
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €459,900
  • Size: 91.41 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: Sonstige
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — the “classic style / history / potential” phrasing strongly hints at an older unit that may be livable but benefits from modernization (kitchen/bath/electrics), and we’re missing floor/lift/energy data.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (91 sqm is workable, but only 2 rooms; layout efficiency will decide) Investor: ➖ (good transit + amenities, but 91 sqm / 2 rooms can be a resale/rent niche depending on layout) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (corner-apartment charm + 550m to U3 + lots of parks/amenities nearby)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — location signals are strong for District 3, but the listing screams “needs capex and proof”: you should price in renovation risk and demand clarity on floor/lift/condition.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €420k–€445k (best-effort guess; without comps and without a condition report, I’d push hard on uncertainty + renovation scope). Anchor with: missing floor/lift info, no energy class, “Sonstige” heating ambiguity, and any signs of older windows/electrics. If the apartment is truly renovated or in a top period building building, expect less room.

Bottom line

You’re buying the micro-location here: strong parks count, decent food/drink density, and solid transit signal. The risk is hidden building/apartment condition (and any building-level costs) — get those answered before you fall in love with the vibe. At this size/price, layout quality matters more than the room count: if it can be sensibly reconfigured, it’s a winner; if not, it’s an expensive 2-room with dead corners.

€469,000 · 105 m2 · 3 rooms · Vienna, 03. District, Landstrasse
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €469,000
  • Size: 105 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Heating: central heating
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — the fundamentals look livable (central heating, loggia, big layout), but the listing data is thin (no floor/condition/energy class), so treat it as “viewing required to confirm.”

Buyer fit

Family: ✔ (105 sqm + 3 rooms + nearby parks reads practical) Investor: ➖ (good district, but high monthly costs and unknown energy/condition cap the yield story) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Fasanviertel vibe + walkable food/transit + loggia)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great size in a highly livable 1030 pocket, but unknown building details + €450.9/month running costs mean you should price in risk and press for full documentation.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €430k–€455k (best-effort guess; comps not provided). Anchor on (1) missing energy class/condition info, (2) €450.9 monthly costs, (3) any upcoming building works (roof/facade/lift) and reserve-fund status. Make the offer conditional on clean paperwork (energy cert, operating cost breakdown, reserve fund, planned refurbishments).

Bottom line

Big 3-room footprint in 1030 is the headline — this is “stay for years” real estate. The micro-area signals are strong (parks + transit + enough daily life within 300m). Your risk is hidden building economics (running costs + unknown energy/renovation cycle) — negotiate like that’s the real product, not the staging.

#325
Wiener Altbaucharme auf hoechstem Niveau - Erstklassige Generalsanierung (Wiener Altbaucharme auf höchstem Niveau - Erstklassige Generalsanierung)
€499,000 · 60.57 m2 · 3 rooms · Landstrasser Hauptstrasse 144, Vienna, 03. District, Landstrasse
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €499,000
  • Size: 60.57 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (if the renovation is truly recent and properly documented).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (3 rooms but only ~60.6 sqm; workable for a young family, tight for long-term) Investor: ➖ (strong rental demand in this corridor, but value hinges on renovation quality and running costs) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (central, walkable, lots of food options + transit nearby)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — attractive micro-location and “fully renovated” story, but you must verify building condition, noise, and ongoing costs before paying a premium.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €450k–€480k (best-effort guess; I do not have comparable sales for this exact street/building, so treat this as a negotiation anchor, not a valuation). Ask for: full renovation scope + invoices, warranties, photo log (before/after), plumbing/electrics documentation, building reserve status, planned works, and a clear breakdown of monthly charges.

Bottom line

You’re buying the renovation and the walkability as much as the apartment. If the building’s common parts are tired (roof/façade/stairwell), expect future special payments. Noise and street-facing windows can flip this from “great” to “annoying” fast—check at rush hour and late evening.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (new-build first occupancy), but final quality depends on handover and snagging.

Buyer fit

Family: ✔ Solid 3-room + balcony at ~71 sqm if the layout is efficient Investor: ➖ New-build pricing can cap yields unless rent levels are exceptional Lifestyle buyer: ✔ Park-heavy micro-area; good daily convenience without feeling touristy

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — attractive “new + park-adjacent” package in the 3rd, but you want price protection for any delivery/quality uncertainties and operating-cost unknowns.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €470k–€495k (best-effort). If true comps for this exact project/micro-location are unavailable, say so explicitly to the seller and anchor on (1) unknown monthly costs, (2) energy/operating assumptions, and (3) handover risk. Bottom line

New-build, provision-free, balcony, 3 rooms in Land Street is a strong “liveable Vienna” setup. The deal turns on: real all-in monthly costs, orientation/noise, and whether the delivered finish matches the brochure. If the unit faces a quiet side and the cost sheet is clean, it’s a credible buy at the right number.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (3 rooms but only ~60 sqm; workable for a couple + child, tight for long-term) Investor: ✔ (District 3, strong transit/amenities density; easier tenant demand story) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, lots of food/drink, parks nearby; good urban convenience)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location signal, but the €/sqm is premium for the size and the listing is missing key facts (floor, spec, energy, condition).

Negotiation strategy

Bottom line

This is a classic “pay for location + branding” unit: small but in a very livable, well-served pocket of the 3rd. If the unit is quiet (not Landstrasser Haupt Street-facing) and the finish is solid, it’s a strong buy. If it’s low-floor on the main road, treat it as a rental-grade asset and don’t overpay.

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (new-build first occupancy; main risk is snagging/handovers, not “hidden old-building” issues).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms / ~55 sqm works for a couple + baby phase, not a long-haul family layout) Investor: ✔ (liquid product in a prime inner district; rentability depends on building fees/financing and exact micro-location) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (balcony + new-build comfort + Land Street access is a strong liveability combo)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — desirable new-build in 1030, but €534k for ~55.5 sqm is “premium pricing”; without floor/fees/energy rating you’re buying a brochure, so push for clarity and concessions.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €505k–€525k (best-effort guess; true comps and building operating costs are unknown). Bottom line

This is a “pay extra to live without renovation drama” apartment. Balcony + geothermal heating signals modern comfort and lower hassle, but you need the missing basics (floor, fees, energy data) to know if it’s smart value. At this price point, the view/noise/orientation and monthly costs make or break the deal.

#329
Drei rooms Gartenwohnung im mit Waermepumpe und bester Ausstattung. (Drei Zimmer Gartenwohnung im Erstbezug mit Wärmepumpe und bester Ausstattung.)
€555,000 · 73.59 m2 · 3 rooms · Klopsteinplatz, Vienna, 03. District, Landstrasse
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €555,000
  • Size: 73.59 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (new-build first occupancy), but final snagging quality + any shared-building commissioning issues decide the real day-1 experience.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (good size for a small family; garden helps, but check noise/privacy) Investor: ➖ (new-build appeal, but pricing + vacancy/rentability in 1030 at this entry point must pencil out) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (garden + walkable amenities density; strong “live-now” setup)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — attractive spec (first occupancy + heat pump + underfloor heating) and good micro-location signals, but garden-ground-floor risk and “new build unknowns” mean you should buy only at the right number.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort guess; comps not provided here): €525,000–€545,000. Anchor below ask on (1) ground-floor/garden trade-offs (privacy, noise, resale buyer pool), (2) commissioning/snags risk in first occupancy, (3) uncertainty around operating costs/reserves despite low monthly costs shown. Concrete lever to demand clarity: ask for written confirmation of building reserves + a split of the €183.81 monthly costs (what’s included/excluded) and documentation of heat-pump/UFH setup (responsibility, service intervals, warranty, metering). If any ambiguity: price chip or retention until issues are fixed.

Bottom line

This is a “new-build comfort” play in a solid district: good day-to-day livability, likely easy resale if the garden is genuinely usable. The two ways this goes wrong: garden turns into a noise/privacy compromise (neighbors, street, shared paths) and the first-occupancy handover reveals latent defects/unfinished common areas that drag on. Pay for certainty: orientation, boundaries, and who pays for what in the building—then negotiate like it’s ground-floor (because it is).

Family: ➖ (2 rooms can work for a couple + baby, but it’s tight long-term) Investor: ➖ (good “easy to rent” profile, but entry price/sqm may compress yield) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (balcony + central-ish 1030 vibe if the micro-location is pleasant)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — nice, liquid product in 1030, but at ~€563k for ~59 sqm you’re paying a premium; make the premium earn its keep (orientation, noise, building quality).

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €520k–€550k (best-effort guess; true comps for this exact project/line are unknown). Negotiation levers: 1) demand clarity on the total monthly carry costs (operating + reserves) before you move a cent; 2) noise/orientation risk—ask for exact balcony orientation + setback from traffic/rails and use that uncertainty to push price; 3) new-build snag list + handover timeline—any defects or delays become direct € leverage.

Bottom line

This is the kind of apartment that’s easy to live in and easy to sell later—if the quietness/orientation checks out. Your two biggest enemies here are (a) paying for “marketing gloss” and (b) ending up with a balcony that’s technically there but functionally noisy/windy/shady. If the building is well-run and the unit is calm, it’s a solid 1030 lifestyle buy; if not, it’s an expensive compromise.

Agent assessment — property (ad-anchored)

This note sticks to what the seller actually states in the ad text and what is present as structured fields. If the ad is thin, treat specifics as unknown until you verify them.

What the seller emphasizes:

  • No strong seller emphasis could be reliably extracted from the text.

What is explicitly stated:

  • Key building/usage details are not clearly specified in the ad text.

What is not stated (so assume unknown):

  • Exact floor level / storey
  • Elevator / lift
  • Outdoor space (balcony/loggia/terrace/garden)
  • Parking / garage availability and terms
  • Energy certificate figures (HWB / fGEE)
  • Tenancy status (vacant vs rented, end date)
  • Furnishing included/excluded

Caveats / how to read this ad:

  • If the pin is not confirmed, treat nearby-amenity context as approximate and verify the exact micro-location.

Fit (who this suits)

  • Best fit: buyers who are comfortable doing verification at the viewing (building condition, fees, exact floor/lift, what is included).
  • Less ideal if: you need full upfront disclosure in the listing text before investing time.

Micro-area (local guide)

As stated in the ad, no specific nearby landmarks or transit lines are named. For this apartment, that matters because you should rely on on-the-ground checks (evening noise, walk times) and the report’s generic OSM proxy signals rather than assumed venue proximity.

Macro-area (local guide)

General background (not source-verified): Vienna 3 can vary block-by-block. The best version of central Vienna living is quiet-at-home but walkable-to-everything; the trade-offs are tourism gradient, event-driven crowding, and late-night noise. Treat courtyard vs street exposure and window quality as the real differentiators.

Stilvoll wohnen, Ruhe geniessen - Ein Altbaujuwel mit Loggia! mit Stil! Rundum saniertes Altbauhaus + Ideale Raumaufteilung! Jetzt zugreifen! (Stilvoll wohnen, Ruhe genießen - Ein Altbaujuwel mit Loggia! Erstbezug mit Stil! Rundum saniertes Altbauhaus + Ideale Raumaufteilung! Jetzt zugreifen!)
€566,000 · 66.6 m2 · 3 rooms · Trubelgasse, Vienna, 03. District, Landstrasse
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €566,000
  • Size: 66.6 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (positioned as first occupancy after a full building renovation), but verify workmanship + building handover docs.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (3 rooms in ~66.6 sqm works for a young family, but it’s tight long-term) Investor: ➖ (1030 is liquid, but price/sqm needs discipline and operating costs are unknown) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (period building charm + loggia + quiet-street pitch in a central district)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — attractive product on paper (renovated period building + loggia in 1030), but the €566k ask (~€8.5k/sqm) needs justification via floor level, light, and true “quiet” plus running costs.

Negotiation strategy

Anchor hard on unknowns: floor/light/exposure, actual noise at rush hour, building common areas after renovation, and individual heating implications (typically higher capex/maintenance + gas-price sensitivity). Target price range: €520k–€545k if the micro-details are merely “good”; up to ~€555k only if it’s genuinely bright, quiet, and the renovation quality is premium. If comparable sales/rents are unknown, say so and demand evidence (recent transactions in the immediate 1030 pocket).

Bottom line

This is a classic “renovated period building, small-but-smart 3-room” play: great if the layout is efficient and the loggia is usable. The area signals are solid (parks + transit nearby), but the listing is missing key cost and energy data — don’t pay peak pricing without those basics. If the building renovation was done well, it’s a high-enjoyment home; if not, you’ll be subsidizing someone else’s flip margin.

  • Size: 72.81 m2
  • Rooms: 3

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (new-build first occupancy), with the usual snagging/punch-list risk.

Buyer fit

Family: ✔ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ✔

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — solid 3rd-district new-build with good parks/transit access, but the €571k price for ~73 sqm needs evidence (comps, monthly costs, energy data) before you pay the “fresh-build premium.”

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €535k–€560k (best-effort guess; comps and building-level details unknown). Ask for: exact floor/orientation, balcony size, sun exposure, noise profile (nearby roads/rail), total monthly operating costs + reserve fund, energy certificate, and a clear handover/spec sheet. Use missing cost/energy/heating info + any timeline pressure on the developer as leverage; push for either a price move or upgrades (kitchen package, storage, parking/bike room terms, closing cost contribution).

Bottom line

New-build in 1030 is usually an easy “liveable bet” and resale-friendly. The micro-area looks practical (parks + transit) rather than touristy, which is good for day-to-day life. But don’t buy blind: the building/stack (floor, orientation, noise) will decide whether this is a gem or just expensive square meters.

  • Size: 71.77 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — it’s a 2027 completion, so you’re buying a promise + timetable, not a finished apartment.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (3 rooms works, but delivery risk + future neighborhood noise during build-out) Investor: ➖ (new-build pricing caps yield; resale liquidity depends on how the whole quarter lands) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (south balcony + modern spec + “village” masterplan vibe)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — good-sized 3-room with south outdoor space, but you need price/terms that compensate for 2027 risk and unknown running costs.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €550k–€575k (best-effort guess; I don’t have verified comps for this exact micro-area/new-build phase, so treat this as a negotiation anchor, not a valuation). Levers: (1) lock in developer-paid upgrades (flooring/AC prep/shading), (2) push for clearer delivery/penalty clauses on delays, (3) get written clarity on expected monthly building costs + reserves + any district heating/contract commitments, (4) if parking/storage is sold separately, bundle discount.

Bottom line

• At ~72 sqm / 3 rooms, this is a “liveable modern Vienna” layout size — not tiny, not luxury-large. • The deal can be great if the quarter matures well; it’s annoying if construction drags and the vibe stays ‘permanent building site’. • Your risk isn’t the apartment — it’s timeline, total ownership costs, and how quiet/sunny it truly is in real life.

Moeglichkeit zur Zusammenlegung im period building mit Eigengarten! Zwischen Rochusmarkt und Kardinal Nagl- Square! (Möglichkeit zur Zusammenlegung im Altbau mit Eigengarten! Zwischen Rochusmarkt und Kardinal Nagl- Platz!)
€590,000 · 78.53 m2 · 3 rooms · Hainburger Strasse 47, Vienna, 03. District, Landstrasse
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €590,000
  • Size: 78.53 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Heating: district heating
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — likely livable, but “possibility to merge” + old-building + garden usually hides coordination/reno scope.

Buyer fit

Family: ✔ (3 rooms + private outdoor space in 1030 is a rare quality-of-life combo) Investor: ➖ (garden + period building can sell well, but merge/reno uncertainty + unknown running costs dilute clean yield math) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Rochusmarkt vibe, dense food scene, strong transit; garden is the flex)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — micro-location is excellent, but you’re paying for “potential”; make price reflect the unknowns (merge feasibility, garden rights, building finances).

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €540k–€575k (best-effort guess; comps unknown, so anchor on risk discount). Levers to push: - Merge feasibility: require clear documentation (what exactly can be combined, approvals needed, current unit boundaries, any easements/servitudes). - Garden reality check: confirm exclusive-use rights vs shared, access route, maintenance obligation, and whether it’s legally tied to the unit. - Building money & future pain: ask for reserve fund status + planned works (facade/roof/stairwell/courtyard) and use any upcoming projects to justify a price cut.

Bottom line

This is a high-signal 1030 “live well” play: market + transit + walkable density, with the rare private garden kicker. The deal goes bad if the merge is a mirage (or expensive/bureaucratic) and if the garden is more complicated than the headline. Price it like a great location with optional upside — not like the upside is guaranteed.

#339
Anleger aufgepasst! 2 rooms auf 70 m2 - apartment mit attraktivem Gruenblick und POOL am DACH (Anleger aufgepasst! 2 Zimmer auf 70 m² - Wohnung mit attraktivem Grünblick und POOL am DACH)
€603,185 · 71.74 m2 · 2 rooms · Vienna, 03. District, Landstrasse
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €603,185
  • Size: 71.74 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — likely turnkey/new-ish given underfloor heating + “roof pool” amenity vibe, but key facts (condition, floor, running costs) are missing.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ➖

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — attractive micro-location signals and amenity appeal, but the pricing looks “premium” for a 2-room and the listing data is incomplete (floor, monthly costs, energy, condition).

Negotiation strategy

At €603,185 for 71.74 m² (~€8.4k/m²), treat this as a premium ask. Without comps, I can’t anchor precisely, but I’d aim for a target range of €540k–€585k unless the building is genuinely top-tier (new build, high floor, low HOA, strong energy performance). Ask for: operating costs/repairs reserve, rental history (if any), exact floor/orientation, and whether the pool/roof facilities carry special fees.

Bottom line

This is the kind of District 3 “easy-to-sell later” product—if the building is modern and fees are sane. The roof-pool narrative can support demand, but it can also hide higher ongoing costs. Buy it for location + building quality, not for the marketing line—verify the boring numbers first.

€624,000 · 70.33 m2 · 3 rooms · Vienna, 03. District, Landstrasse
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €624,000
  • Size: 70.33 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (on paper) — but “first occupancy” usually means you must budget time for snagging/defect fixes and final punch-list items.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (3 rooms in ~70 sqm can work, but the lifestyle/amenity premium may not be the best €/utility) Investor: ➖ (could rent well in 1030, but rooftop pool + higher building overhead can compress yield) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (skyline/view + rooftop pool is exactly the “pay extra for vibes” product)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — attractive 1030 lifestyle unit, but missing operating costs + amenity-heavy building risk means price only works if the monthly total and house economics check out.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €580k–€610k (best-effort; comps unknown) — anchor your offer on the unknown monthly running costs and the long-term cost of maintaining the rooftop pool/amenities; if the seller can document low total monthly burden + solid reserve, you can move up.

Bottom line

You’re buying a “new-build amenity tower” experience: views + rooftop pool are the headline, and you’ll pay for it forever via building costs. If you’re planning to live there and actually use the amenities, it can be worth it; if you’re optimizing for value per sqm, it’s probably not. Two ways this goes wrong: the total monthly cost comes back ugly (pool + lift + common areas), or the view/noise/wind reality doesn’t match the marketing when you stand there at rush hour/evening.

Move-in readiness

Depends — smells like a developer/new-build or high-end renovation concept (“Legacy”), but you’re missing the key facts (completion status, handover date, finish spec).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (good 3-room size, but Landstrasser Haupt Street can be loud; depends on courtyard orientation + floor) Investor: ➖ (3rd district rents well, but pricing is punchy per sqm; yield depends on exact micro-location + HOA/capex terms) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (central-ish, strong connectivity, easy city living if the unit is quiet/bright)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — attractive 3rd-district address and sensible 3-room size, but at this ticket you should demand clarity on noise/orientation, building condition/amenities, and what “monthly costs” actually include.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €570k–€610k (best-effort guess; comps unknown from the provided data). Anchor on missing floor/orientation, any main-road noise risk, and uncertainty around “Legacy” positioning (marketing premium) unless specs prove it.

This is a classic “could be great, could be a headache” 1030 buy: location works, but the street can punish you if you’re facing it. At €625k for ~74 sqm, you’re paying for story + district — only do it if the unit is genuinely quiet, well-laid-out, and the building finances are clean. If it’s a courtyard-facing, higher-floor unit with solid windows and good light, it can be a strong long-term hold.

  • Size: 69.21 m2

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (new-build first occupancy), but the real “readiness” hinges on what’s already included/finished (kitchen, lighting, bathroom spec) and handover quality.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (great size for a couple + one kid, but depends on layout + storage + stroller logistics) Investor: ➖ (new-build pricing can cap yields; works if this micro-area rents strongly and rules allow easy letting) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (3rd district + park/transit density reads as very livable day-to-day)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong location fundamentals and modern heating, but you’re paying a premium and key risk items are missing (floor/orientation/costs/energy data).

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €610k–€635k (best-effort guess; no comp set provided). Push on the info gaps: (1) exact floor + orientation + noise exposure, (2) building reserve + operating costs, (3) handover scope (what “” actually includes), and (4) any developer incentives (storage, parking, upgrade credits). Concrete lever: make your offer conditional on documented monthly running costs + reserve contribution and a snag-list/defect protocol.

Bottom line

This looks like a “buy the area + buy the newness” deal: convenient micro-area (parks/transit) and modern systems. Two ways it goes wrong: you end up facing a loud axis/rail line or a gloomy courtyard, and/or monthly costs + reserve land higher than expected for a new complex. If the balcony orientation is quiet/sunny and costs are sane, it’s a very defensible lifestyle purchase in 1030.

Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €662,400
  • Size: 70.33 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (new-build first occupancy vibe), but the “real” readiness depends on what’s actually included (kitchen, lighting, wardrobes) and handover quality.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (70 sqm / 3 rooms can work for a young family, but it’s tight unless the layout is excellent) Investor: ✔ (new condition + floor heating is attractive; rentability depends on exact micro-location + monthly running costs) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (compact 3-room in District 3 can be a very livable city base if orientation/noise is good)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — price-per-sqm is premium for a compact unit; could still be justified if building quality, orientation, and common areas are genuinely top-tier.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €610k–€645k (comps not provided, so this is a best-effort range based on “new-build premium” risk and unknown monthly costs). Use uncertainty to your advantage: ask for the full operating-cost breakdown, reserve-fund status, and the exact handover/inclusions list; if any of those are unfavorable or vague, push for a price reduction or seller-paid closing-related items. Negotiation lever tied to a checkable unknown: the monthly running costs + reserve fund (and what they include) — if they’re higher than expected, your net yield and resale pool shrink.

Bottom line

You’re paying for “new + hassle-free.” Make sure you’re not also paying for “marketing.” Two ways this can go wrong: (1) the layout is chopped up and the 3 rooms feel like a compromise; (2) noise/overheating issues (street-facing, poor shading) make the “vacation feel” disappear fast. If the orientation is quiet and the inclusions are complete, it’s a strong buy-at-the-right-price District 3 lifestyle/investment hybrid.

Fit (who this suits)

#347
ERSTBEZUG: Der ultimative Ausblick! Freuen Sie sich auf einen modernen Lifestyle mit vielen Annehmlichkeiten! (ERSTBEZUG: Der ultimative Ausblick! Freuen Sie sich auf einen modernen Lifestyle mit vielen Annehmlichkeiten!)
€681,600 · 71.74 m2 · 2 rooms · Vienna, 03. District, Landstrasse
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €681,600
  • Size: 71.74 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Real-estate agent + developer-brain take: this reads like a new-build, amenity-heavy “lifestyle” apartment where you pay for finish level and outlook more than raw square meters.

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (first occupancy + underfloor heating suggests a turnkey spec, assuming handover is complete and snagging is clean).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (71.7 sqm and 2 rooms can work for a couple + baby, but it’s not a long-horizon family layout)

Investor: ➖ (strong area liquidity, but price-per-sqm and “premium narrative” can cap yield)

Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable dining/transit density + “view/amenities” positioning)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the area fundamentals look solid, but the “ultimate view + amenities” premium needs proof (floor height, exposure, noise, and HOA-level monthly costs).

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €630k–€665k (best-effort; I do not have local closed-sale comps for this exact micro-location/spec). Push for (1) documented handover quality / snag list resolution, (2) clear monthly building costs and reserve fund, and (3) any incentives (kitchen/wardrobes, parking/storage, closing-cost contribution). If the view is truly exceptional and protected, concede upward; if it’s a “marketing view,” hold your line.

Bottom line

You’re buying into a convenience-heavy, urban lifestyle pocket with lots of transit and daily services.

The risk is overpaying for branding and outlook without getting the quietness/light and operating-cost transparency to match.

Great if you’ll actually use the neighborhood and amenities; less great if you’re price-sensitive on resale multiple.

Final verdict

Bottom line

Amenity density and transit look excellent — this is the kind of spot that feels convenient every single day. The risk is paying premium pricing for a unit that ends up street-noisy, light-poor, or layout-inefficient. If the unit faces quiet side and the building’s finances are clean, it’s a credible buy; otherwise, it’s a negotiate-or-walk situation.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — marketed as near completion, but your real timeline hinges on handover date, snag list, and what “finished” includes.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (good size for 3 rooms, but location likely means more street noise/traffic trade-offs) Investor: ➖ (3rd district is liquid, but new-build pricing can cap yields) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (central-ish, lots of parks and everyday amenities nearby)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong area fundamentals and modern heating, but price looks “new-build premium” and the missing operating costs / energy rating info is leverage.

Negotiation strategy

Ask for full cost sheet (all monthly running costs + reserve fund), exact handover date, and a written specification list. Use the absence of energy class + missing monthly costs to justify a discount. Target price range: €650k–€680k (best-effort guess; I do not have comparable sales for this exact micro-location/building).

Bottom line

You’re paying for 3rd-district convenience and “almost new” condition. Make sure the building logistics and street exposure match your tolerance (noise, dust, traffic). If the paperwork is clean and finishing quality checks out, this can be a solid long-term hold — just don’t overpay for the marketing headline.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ✔ Lifestyle buyer: ➖

Final verdict

Negotiation strategy

If it’s truly a normal 32.7 sqm residential unit with decent light and quiet orientation, it’s a sharp starter/investment play in Leopoldstadt. If it’s ground-floor, mixed-use, or restricted use, expect harder financing, higher hassle, and weaker resale. Your two make-or-break checks are legal use classification and noise/daylight reality.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — it’s marketed as a new-build project, but without unit-level details (handover date, finish spec, floorplan) this could be anything from near-ready to years out.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (could work only if larger units exist; this price looks like a small starter unit) Investor: ✔ (2nd district + transit/amenities density can rent well, but only if the micro-unit economics and building fees check out) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (lots of food/drink + parks + transit within 300m suggests a very livable daily loop)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong location signal, but the listing reads like a lead-capture teaser; until you see the exact unit, total costs, and timeline, you’re negotiating blind.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €145k–€160k for the cheapest/smallest unit (best-effort guess; comps and exact sqm/room count are unknown). Push for: (1) a fixed-price spec sheet (what’s included vs upgrade packs), (2) binding delivery date + penalties/clauses, and (3) an itemized breakdown of all one-time buyer costs (developer fees, parking/storage, contract/admin).

Bottom line

This smells like “from €157k” marketing: the attractive number may correspond to the least desirable unit (tiny, low floor, courtyard/shadow, or high €/sqm). The area looks very convenient on paper (transit + parks + restaurants), which is exactly why unit orientation/noise and total running costs will make or break it. If you can lock the true all-in cost and timeline, it can be a smart entry point into a strong district; if not, you’re buying a brochure, not a home.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — likely livable as-is, but the key risk is hidden building/flat condition (small units can mask expensive fixes).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ➖

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location near transit/parks with strong rental logic, but 25.7 sqm + electric heating means price sensitivity and higher operating-cost risk.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €150k–€162k (comps unknown, so this is a risk-adjusted guess). Press on (1) electric heating running costs + any planned switch/upgrade, (2) building reserves and upcoming works (lift/roof/façade), and (3) whether the 5th-floor access and light/noise orientation justify the ask.

Bottom line

This is a “location-first micro-unit”: easy to rent, easy to resell if priced right. Two ways it goes wrong: electric heating makes it unattractive vs. similar units; or the building hits you with special assessments (lift/roof) that blow up returns. If the building paperwork is clean and the unit is quiet/bright, it’s a solid small-ticket play — but don’t overpay for the headline proximity to Prater/U1.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (48 m² / 2 rooms is tight as a long-term family base) Investor: ✔ (2nd district liquidity + small unit size + good amenity/transit counts) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable daily life: groceries/parks/transit density suggests easy car-free living)

Final verdict

2nd district + small, efficient 2-room can be a very liquid “always in demand” format. Your main hidden cost risk is heating and building efficiency; your main quality risk is floor/light/noise. Negotiate like you’re buying the uncertainty, not the marketing copy.

Move-in readiness

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (good parks + groceries nearby, but without rooms/sqm it’s impossible to judge true family usability) Investor: ✔ (2nd district + transit/amenities density usually rents well; unit details and fees will decide) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (high food/drink density + multiple parks nearby reads like easy, walkable daily life)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the micro-location looks strong (parks + amenities), but the listing is currently underspecified; price could be fine or totally off depending on sqm/fees and which exact unit this is.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €220k–€235k (best-effort guess; comps are unknown from the provided data). Ask for: exact sqm + room count, floor + exposure (courtyard/park/street), balcony/loggia, monthly operating costs + reserve, heating type, energy certificate, and a full spec sheet for the project. If any of those are vague, use that uncertainty to push the price down or win concessions (kitchen package, storage, parking, closing timeline).

Bottom line

2nd district near parks is a proven “liveability” bet — you’ll actually use the neighborhood daily. But without sqm/rooms/floor, you’re negotiating blind: the unit could be a great starter home or an overpriced micro-apartment. Get the missing unit facts first, then decide fast if it’s a quiet-facing unit; those tend to be the keepers.

  • Less ideal if: you need full upfront disclosure in the listing text before investing time.

Micro-area (local guide)

As stated in the ad, no specific nearby landmarks or transit lines are named. For this apartment, that matters because you should rely on on-the-ground checks (evening noise, walk times) and the report’s generic OSM proxy signals rather than assumed venue proximity.

Macro-area (local guide)

The best version of central Vienna living is quiet-at-home but walkable-to-everything; the trade-offs are tourism gradient, event-driven crowding, and late-night noise. Stuwerviertel - Moderne Wohnungen im revitalisierten period building (Stuwerviertel | Moderne Wohnungen im revitalisierten Altbau)
€250,000 · 32.2 m2 · 1 rooms · Stuwerstrasse 60, Vienna, 02. District, Leopoldstadt
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €250,000
  • Size: 32.2 m2
  • Rooms: 1
  • Heating: central heating
  • District: Vienna 2

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — “revitalised old building” usually means the shell is older but the unit may be newly finished; with no floor/energy/monthly-costs disclosed, assume there are still unknowns.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (32.2 sqm / 1 room) Investor: ✔ (micro-studio size, strong transit + amenity density) Lifestyle buyer: ➖ (great for a city-base, but small; depends on noise/light/floor)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location/micro-area is strong, but at this size you’re buying “precision real estate”; without operating costs and specs, price risk is the main enemy.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €230k–€245k (best-effort guess; no direct comps provided). Ask for: full Betriebskosten breakdown + reserve fund status, exact floor/courtyard vs street orientation, completion scope of the revitalisation, and heating cost allocation. Use missing/unclear monthly costs + absent energy data to justify a discount or incentives.

Bottom line

2nd district near Prater/Messe vibes: convenient, walkable, and liquid for resale/rent. But 32 sqm lives or dies on layout + light + noise — a bad floorplan makes it feel like a hotel room. If it’s street-facing on Stuwer Street, negotiate harder; if quiet/courtyard-facing, it’s a much cleaner buy.

Buyer fit

  • Heating: central heating

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — “revitalised old building” can mean anything from turnkey to “nice common areas, but units still need finish decisions”; without unit-level specs it’s hard to call.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (micro-area is great for city life, but fit depends heavily on unit size/layout + noise exposure) Investor: ✔ (2nd district + strong transit + food/park density is typically liquid; details decide yield) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Stuwerviertel is one of the best “walk + park + city” pockets in 1020)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location signal is strong, but the listing reads like a project umbrella; only buy once the *specific unit* (sqm/rooms/floor, courtyard vs street, condition, costs) checks out.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €225,000–€245,000 (best-effort guess; comps unknown and sqm/rooms missing). Push for (1) exact unit pricing per m², (2) clear scope of “revitalisation” (what’s new, what’s not), and (3) full running costs + reserve fund. If it’s street-facing on Stuwer Street or low floor, negotiate harder.

Bottom line

This is a premium micro-area: parks + transit + a real neighborhood feel, not just postcard Vienna. The biggest risk is buying the *marketing story* instead of the unit (light, noise, floorplan, monthly costs). If you can secure a quiet orientation (courtyard) and clean building economics, it’s a very defensible 1020 buy.

Move-in readiness

Depends — “bright + loggia + 4th floor with lift” reads like a solid base, but missing renovation/energy details is a real unknown.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms / ~54 sqm is tight unless it’s a couple + baby) Investor: ✔ (2nd district liquidity + loggia + lift-floor usually rents well if layout is clean) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, lots of transit + parks nearby; hofseitig loggia suggests quieter outdoor space)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the micro-location looks strong and the “quiet-side loggia + higher floor” combo is attractive, but the lack of hard facts (condition, energy, floorplan) means you should buy only at the right number.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €235k–€250k (best-effort guess; no direct comps provided). Use the information gaps as leverage: ask for (1) exact floor + orientation, (2) year built + building condition plan, (3) recent renovations (windows/electrics/plumbing), (4) reserve fund level and any planned works, (5) energy cert. If any “soon” capex shows up (facade/roof/lift/heating), push toward the low end.

Bottom line

Two big positives: quiet-side loggia and a higher floor with a lift — that’s quality-of-life and resale. The neighborhood stats scream “easy daily living” (transit + parks + food density), which protects downside. But you’re currently bidding blind on condition/efficiency — price it like a ‘maybe needs work’ until proven otherwise.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (30.39 sqm / 1 room is functionally a studio) Investor: ➖ (micro-unit demand is strong, but entry price per sqm is punchy and exit liquidity depends on exact micro-location + floor) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (if you want a compact city base in a very walkable, café-and-market-heavy pocket)

Final verdict

Move-in readiness

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (could work for a small household, but the market-side micro-location can be noisy and you haven’t provided size/layout) Investor: ✔ (strong rental liquidity signal near Karmelitermarkt; watch building rules/short-let limits) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (high walkability: lots of food/drink + transit + parks in ~300m)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the micro-area is excellent for daily life, but without unit specs (sqm/rooms/floor/orientation) you’re buying the “project story” more than a validated apartment.

Negotiation strategy

Bottom line

Karmelitermarkt proximity is a lifestyle cheat code: eat well, walk everywhere, easy transit. This deal goes wrong if your unit faces the wrong side (market/late-night noise) or if the “new-build” spec is thinner than the marketing. If the unit details check out, it’s a high-liquidity pocket in the 2nd district—just don’t overpay for branding.

  • Heating: central heating

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — “revitalised older building” + central heating can be great, but the ad data you have is missing the unit’s floor, energy rating, and monthly running costs.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (33 sqm / 2 rooms = tight) Investor: ➖ (works if total monthly costs + building health are clean) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (high walkability signals; small, urban base)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — nice micro-location and a polished concept, but at 275k for ~33 sqm you’re paying “product” pricing; you need proof the building finances + monthly costs won’t kill the deal.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €245k–€265k (comps unknown from your data; this is a risk-discount range for missing facts + small-unit liquidity). Push for: (1) full breakdown of monthly costs (operating + reserve fund), (2) reserve fund balance and planned works schedule, (3) exact floor + orientation + window quality. Negotiation lever (verifiable uncertainty): if the reserve fund is thin or there are upcoming façade/roof/lift works, price needs to adjust immediately.

Bottom line

The area reads strong: lots of parks nearby and solid transit density — that’s quality-of-life and rental demand. The apartment reads “small premium unit in a refreshed older shell” — great if quiet and efficient, painful if it faces street noise or has high shared-building costs. Your win condition: low all-in monthly costs + a healthy building plan; otherwise you’re buying stress at a boutique price.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (31 sqm studio is functionally a one-person home) Investor: ✔ (prime micro-location for liquidity + rental demand, but watch yields vs total monthly costs) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (if you want a walkable, food-heavy neighborhood and don’t need space)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great Karmelitermarkt micro-area, but at ~€9.2k/sqm you’re paying for location/branding; verify floor, light, noise, and building spec before paying “Pearl” pricing.

You’re buying the neighborhood more than the apartment — and this is one of Vienna’s strongest “daily life” micro-areas. For a studio, daylight and noise decide everything: get the exact orientation, floor, and window situation before you fall for the listing name. If it’s quiet and bright, it’ll be very easy to live in or re-sell; if it’s dark/street-noisy, it becomes an overpriced crash pad.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ✔

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location is legitimately prime (Karmelitermarkt pocket), but the €/sqm is premium for a tiny unit and key risk fields are unknown.

Negotiation strategy

Bottom line

This is a micro-apartment in a top micro-area: you’re paying for the map pin. The deal goes wrong if it’s noisy/dark (bad orientation/floor) or if the building has chunky upcoming capex that crushes net yield. If those check out, it’s a clean, easy-to-rent unit in one of Vienna’s most walkable pockets.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — no condition/energy/heating/costs info provided; budget for unknown building-item surprises.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (2 rooms / ~54 sqm is tight long-term) Investor: ➖ (good rental profile if building costs + heating are sane; verify attic/loft liquidity) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (compact, urban, next to a major park, lots of daily-life amenities nearby)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — attractive micro-location near Augarten + strong amenity density, but too many blanks (running costs, heating, energy class, floor/access) to pay “clean” pricing.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €315k–€335k (best-effort guess; true comps unknown). Push for: (1) full monthly running costs, (2) heating type + age, (3) energy certificate, (4) building reserves + planned works, (5) proof the loft space is fully approved/recorded, (6) noise/heat performance under summer sun.

Bottom line

You’re buying the park-adjacent lifestyle and walkability, not square meters. If the building is well-run and the loft is properly documented, it’s a very livable 2-room city pad. If costs/heat/noise are bad, resale can get sticky fast at this size/category.

Depends — likely fine structurally (district heating, lift building), but “bright + loggia” listings often hide dated kitchen/bath; verify renovation year and electrics.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ Investor: ✔ Lifestyle buyer: ➖

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — good micro-location and size for the money, but monthly running costs are chunky and the value hinges on condition + building reserves.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €315k–€335k (best-effort guess; without comps this is cost/condition-driven). If it needs a full cosmetic redo, treat that as an explicit discount line item.

Bottom line

2nd district near the Prater is a legit “Vienna quality-of-life” pocket: green space + transit density is hard to fake. At €345k it’s plausible value for 71 sqm, but only if the building is financially healthy and the apartment isn’t a renovation trap. Make the seller pay (in price) for uncertainty: condition, reserves, and any planned building works.

Move-in readiness

Depends — “fully furnished” suggests immediate usability, but the listing gives you almost no hard signals on condition, floor, lift, or building state.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ✔

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location and sensible size, but the info gaps (floor/lift/condition/energy) + furniture premium risk mean you should only proceed at a price that bakes in uncertainty.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €320k–€340k (comps unknown from provided data; range is an uncertainty haircut + furniture skepticism). Treat the furniture as near-zero value unless it’s genuinely high-quality and documented. Ask for: building year/renovations, exact floor + lift, orientation (street/courtyard), noise, window type, heating costs, reserve fund, upcoming works, and a clear breakdown of monthly costs.

Bottom line

This is the classic “buy the location” play: lots of food/groceries/transit within 300m and parks nearby — you’ll use that every day. The risk is hidden building/floor/noise issues; in 1020 those can swing livability more than the furniture ever will. Price it like an unfurnished, average-condition 2-room until proven otherwise.

Move-in readiness

Depends — currently rented until 03/2027, so it’s “ready” only as an income-producing unit, not for self-use.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (size + delayed occupancy) Investor: ✔ (fixed tenancy + “free rent” positioning) Lifestyle buyer: ➖ (great area vibe, but you’re waiting ~1 year+ to move in)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — the micro-location near Vorgartenmarkt is strong, but the dated/unknowns (floor, energy class, building condition) + tenant-in-place should be priced in.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €315k–€335k (best-effort guess; I don’t have building comps here, so treat as a starting anchor, not a comp-backed valuation). Emphasize: (1) occupancy until 03/2027 limits buyer pool, (2) individual heating risk (often gas, higher running-cost/upgrade uncertainty), (3) missing core facts in extract (floor/lift/condition/energy). Ask for rent amount, contract terms, and any planned building works (roof/facade/lift/risers) before moving off your anchor.

Bottom line

2nd district + this pocket’s daily-life convenience is a real win (markets, transit, parks). But this is primarily an investor product today; for an owner-occupier it’s a patience play. Pay for the location, not for a “sunny 2-room” headline — the tenancy and heating type are the real drivers.

€345,000 · 40.6 m2 · 2 rooms · Stuwerstrasse 60, Vienna, 02. District, Leopoldstadt
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €345,000
  • Size: 40.6 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: central heating
  • District: Vienna 2

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — “revitalised old building” often means nicer finishes, but the real story is the building’s common areas + reserves.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (40.6 sqm / 2 rooms is tight) Investor: ➖ (good location signals, but small-unit pricing can be unforgiving) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, parks/transit density, likely great weekday convenience)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong micro-location (parks + transit + food options), but at this size the price needs to be justified by floor/orientation/quality and the building’s financials.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €315k–€335k (best-effort; I don’t have comps). Anchor on the unknowns that materially change value: exact floor + street vs courtyard orientation, noise exposure, and the building’s reserve fund / upcoming works after “revitalisation.” If they can’t show clean reserve/maintenance planning, treat that as a direct €10k–€20k discount lever.

Bottom line

This is a “tiny but high-convenience” Leopoldstadt play: you’re paying for a daily-life location, not for space. Two ways it goes wrong: (1) street-facing noise/traffic makes it feel smaller and harder to rent/sell; (2) post-revitalisation surprises (lift/stairwell/courtyard work, facade, heating system allocations) hit you via higher monthly costs. If it’s quiet (courtyard-facing) and the building finances are clean, it can be a sharp lifestyle buy; if not, the price needs to move.
