#444
Top City Lage! period building im 7.district! (Top City Lage! Altbau Erstbezug im 7.Bezirk!)
€498,000 · 71.3 m2 · 3 rooms · Vienna, 07. District, Neubau
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/Parking
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €498,000
  • Size: 71.3 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 7

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — “period building ” suggests fresh renovation, but with no floor/lift/energy details you must treat it as a renovation-shell risk until you see the specs + viewing.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (good size, but 7th can mean noise/parking trade-offs; unknown floor/lift matters a lot) Investor: ✔ (high-demand micro-location, easy rental story; watch heating/energy + monthly costs) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walk-everywhere central living; restaurants/transit density is the whole point)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location is strong, but “individual heating” + missing building facts (floor, lift, energy class) are classic pricing/comfort levers.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €460k–€485k (best-effort guess; without comps and without floor/lift/energy data this is a leverage-based range, not a valuation). Use (1) individual heating running costs/comfort, (2) lack of energy class info, (3) any noise/first-floor exposure, and (4) any period building building capex signals (roof/façade/cellar) to justify a haircut. Ask explicitly what was renovated (wiring, plumbing risers, windows, sound insulation) and whether the building has upcoming major works.

Bottom line

2-bedroom-ish size in the 7th with very high amenity density is a lifestyle cheat code. The risk is “period building + individual heating + unknown floor/lift” turning into heat bills, summer comfort issues, or resale friction. If the renovation quality is real (and quiet), this could be a keeper — but don’t pay prime-floor pricing without proof.

  • Size: 88.14 m2
  • Rooms: 3
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 7

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — “period building ” suggests fresh renovation, but budget for the typical old-building unknowns (noise, wiring, moisture, heating performance) until you’ve seen docs and the building common areas.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (good size, but 7th can be lively/noisy; floor/lift unknown) Investor: ➖ (great demand area, but price-per-sqm is rich and old-building CapEx risk) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, restaurant density, parks, transit — strong day-to-day quality)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the micro-location is excellent, but the price looks “prime 7th + fresh-reno premium” and you’re missing key risk variables (floor, lift, building condition, renovation scope, energy performance).

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €540k–€575k (best-effort guess; I don’t have comps for this exact micro-location + renovation level). Push hard on (1) renovation specifications and warranties, (2) building reserve fund + upcoming works, (3) heating system age and running costs, and (4) any noise/usage constraints. If it’s a courtyard-facing quiet unit with strong building finances, you can justify moving closer to ask.

Bottom line

You’re buying the 7th district lifestyle first — the OSM counts scream “walk everywhere, never bored.” The main risk is old-building uncertainty + missing basics (floor/lift/energy), which can flip this from “dream flat” to “annoying daily friction.” If the building is well-managed and the renovation is truly high-quality, it’s a keeper — but only at the right number.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Needs work — “unrenovated old-building shell” + floor unknown implies real refurbishment risk before comfortable living.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms/56m² can work for a couple + baby, but renovation + unknown floor/lift make it harder) Investor: ➖ (Josefstadt demand helps, but refurbishment budget + individual heating + unknown running costs cap returns) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (top-tier inner Vienna micro-area; if you want Josefstadt walkability and can handle a project)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location is doing heavy lifting; you’re buying potential, so price must compensate for renovation uncertainty.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €245k–€260k (best-effort guess; comps unknown). Make the offer contingent on (1) detailed renovation scope/costing, (2) house condition review (roof/facade/riser pipes), (3) clear monthly costs and reserve fund status. Use the “unrenovated” framing to push for a discount that covers risk + time-to-move-in.

Bottom line

If you want Josefstadt and can stomach dust + contractor management, this could be a great long-term hold. But don’t pay “ready-to-live” money for a raw shell. The single biggest swing factor is the building’s state (and the unit’s floor/lift) — get those clarified early or walk.

Move-in readiness

Needs work — explicitly “sanierungsbeduerftig”; budget/time for a full renovation is part of the purchase.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (great size, but construction phase + unknown layout/functionality until renovated) Investor: ➖ (strong micro-location, but renovation risk + older-building surprises can crush yield) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (prime Josefstadt vibe + U6 access; you’ll end up with a custom home)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — excellent 1080 micro-area + 104 m² upside, but only at a price that clearly covers renovation risk.

Bottom line

This is a classic inner-city “value is in the address” buy. If the building is healthy and the layout can be modernized well, it can become a top-tier 1080 apartment. But don’t overpay for romance: renovation + older-building unknowns must be priced in up front.

Move-in readiness

Depends — likely turnkey on finishes, but attic-level risk items (roof/insulation, heat management, terrace waterproofing) need verification.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2-room / ~65 sqm is tight long-term) Investor: ➖ (high entry price; works only if rentability + low capex are proven) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (bright top-floor feel + big terrace + quiet courtyard balcony is the whole point)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great micro-location and “penthouse-lite” features, but you must price in terrace/attic maintenance and ensure the heating/operating costs won’t annoy you.

Negotiation strategy

Bottom line

You’re paying for the 1080 lifestyle plus outdoor space — that’s the win. Attic apartments can be phenomenal, but only when the building envelope and terrace detailing are boringly well-documented. If the paperwork is thin, negotiate harder or walk — fixes get expensive fast.

Move-in readiness

Buyer fit

Family: ✔ (true 3-room / ~101 sqm works if layout is sane) Investor: ➖ (nice area, but older-build risks + heating system can cap yield) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Josefstadt + Hamerling Square vibe is walk-first, café-first)

The garage spots are a major sweetener in 1080 — but only if the paperwork and ongoing costs check out. The main job: force clarity on building money, heating, and noise before you pay “bestlage” pricing.

Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Address/map mismatch flagged (likely approximate pin).
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View

  • Size: 55 m2
  • District: Vienna 9

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — listing details are thin (no condition, floor, heating, or energy data), so assume “unknown until viewing”.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (55 sqm / 2-room profile, likely tight long-term) Investor: ➖ (good 9th-district demand signals, but yield depends on building condition + running costs) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, amenity-rich micro-area near a known local hub)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location looks strong for daily life, but the information gap is big enough that price should reflect uncertainty.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort): €345k–€365k. Comps are unknown from the provided data, so treat this as a risk-adjusted anchor, not a market-validated valuation. Ask for: floor level + lift status, renovation year/specs, heating type, energy cert, and any planned building works; use any “needs refresh” items to justify the discount.

Bottom line

Micro-location seems excellent: lots of restaurants/cafes, groceries, parks, and solid transit density in a 300m radius. At €375k for 55 sqm, you’re paying for the neighborhood — make sure the building and apartment condition don’t quietly erase that premium. If it’s a well-kept older building with decent light and quiet exposure, this could be a very livable long-term base in the 9th.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (positioned as “high-quality renovated” + underfloor heating suggests modernized interior).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms / 56 sqm works for a couple + baby, but it’s not a long-haul family layout). Investor: ✔ (9th district liquidity + near-transit micro-location; easy-to-rent unit size). Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (high-walkability node: lots of food/drink, groceries, parks, and heavy transit within 300m).

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong district + hyper-connected spot, but you need clarity on floor/building quality/monthly costs before paying “exclusive” pricing.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €360k–€375k (best-effort guess; comps not provided, and the exact floor/building condition can swing value a lot). Use missing essentials as leverage: ask for floor, lift, building year/condition, reserve fund status, and full operating costs; if any are weak/unclear, price follows.

Bottom line

This is a classic “urban convenience” buy: you’re paying for being plugged into the city. If the building is solid and the monthly costs are reasonable, this is a clean, rentable, easy-living 2-room. If it’s a compromised floor/building situation, you negotiate hard or walk—location won’t fix nuisance.

#452
SERVITENVIERTEL- TERRASSENERSTBEZUG auf " GARTENEBENE" - HIGH END ALTBAUWOHNUNG - STILJUWEL - 2 rooms + NR - RUHELAGE (SERVITENVIERTEL- TERRASSENERSTBEZUG auf " GARTENEBENE" - HIGH END ALTBAUWOHNUNG - STILJUWEL - 2 ZIMMER + NR - RUHELAGE)
€479,000 · 45 m2 · 2 rooms · Vienna, 09. District, Alsergrund
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €479,000
  • Size: 45 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 9

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — marketed as “first occupancy / high-end” but the real question is whether the terrace + “garden level” setup is properly waterproofed/approved and actually quiet in practice.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (45 sqm / 2 rooms is tight long-term) Investor: ➖ (strong micro-location, but terrace/garden-level liquidity is more taste-driven) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Servitenviertel vibe + walkable amenities + parks, if the unit truly feels calm)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — great area and “stylish old-building” positioning, but garden-level + terrace is exactly where hidden building-physics and legal-detail risks live.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €445k–€470k (best-effort guess; comps not provided, so treat as a negotiation anchor, not a valuation). Levers to push: - Terrace specifics: ask for documented build-up (waterproofing layers), warranty, drainage detail, and who maintains/owns it (exclusive use vs property). Any uncertainty = price haircut. - Building finances: request reserve fund level + recent/forecast works; “old building + high-end refurb” can still hide expensive common-area projects. - Noise/orientation: insist on a daytime + evening visit; if “quiet” is overstated (courtyard logistics, ground-level foot traffic), that’s immediate leverage.

Bottom line

You’re paying for micro-location and vibe — the numbers only work if the terrace is truly usable and the garden-level doesn’t feel dim or damp. Two ways this deal goes wrong: (1) moisture/waterproofing problems show up after a couple of seasons, (2) the “quiet” claim collapses once you experience evening activity and ground-level acoustics. If the paperwork and on-site feel check out, this is a strong lifestyle buy; if not, walk fast — fixes here get expensive and annoying.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (44.76 sqm / 2 rooms is tight long-term) Investor: ➖ (great micro-location, but terrace + is often priced rich; run rent yield + monthly running costs) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (quiet courtyard orientation + big terrace near canal/Servitenviertel is a very livable combo)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — top micro-area, but the €/sqm is premium for a small 2-room; you want either price relief or strong proof of build quality and low future capex.

Negotiation strategy

Bottom line

Courtyard-facing + large terrace in the 9th is the headline: calm at home, city energy outside. This is a “buy the location + outdoor space” play — just don’t overpay for the word . Get clarity on heating system economics, building condition (or renovation scope), and total monthly carrying costs before committing.

Move-in readiness

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (35 sqm / 1 room) Investor: ✔ (4th district, strong amenity/transit density, smaller unit tends to rent easily) Lifestyle buyer: ➖ (great city base, but 1-room limits long-term comfort/storage)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — location + renovation story are strong, but at ~€7.7k/sqm for a small studio you need proof on building quality, operating costs, and rentability to justify the price.

Negotiation strategy

Bottom line

Wieden is a high-liquidity micro-market: you’re buying convenience and demand, not space. If the unit is truly quiet/green-facing and the building is financially healthy, it’s a clean “city base / rental” asset. If it’s street-facing or the building has lift/reserve surprises, the premium becomes hard to defend.

Your risk is hidden costs + a layout that only works on paper for “WG” — insist on the floorplan and a quietness check (street orientation/windows).

Move-in readiness

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (53.8 sqm / 2 rooms reads tight for anything beyond a couple + baby) Investor: ➖ (prime micro-location helps liquidity, but entry price per sqm is punchy) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walk-everywhere, restaurants/transit density, plus courtyard-side quiet)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location is excellent and the courtyard/balcony/high ceilings are real value, but the €/sqm looks premium enough that you should press on price and terms.

Negotiation strategy

Ask for: detailed spec sheet (brand list), warranty/defect handling process, and a clear statement of monthly running costs + reserve fund.

Bottom line

This is a “central Vienna, live-on-foot” play: micro-area energy outside, calmer courtyard inside. You’re paying for scarcity (4th district + balcony + new finish + 3.3m ceilings), so don’t overpay without hard proof of build quality. If you love the vibe and plan to live there, negotiate hard and move fast; if you’re purely yield-driven, be skeptical at this ticket price.

€207,000 · 42 m2 · 1 rooms · Vienna, 02. District, Leopoldstadt
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Address/map mismatch flagged (likely approximate pin).
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View

  • Size: 42 m2
  • Rooms: 1
  • District: Vienna 2

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready (claimed fully renovated), but the “how well” depends on what exactly was replaced (wiring/plumbing/windows) and whether it was permitted.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (1 room, 42 sqm) Investor: ✔ (small unit + low-ish absolute price can rent easily if the building/legal setup is clean) Lifestyle buyer: ➖ (nice city base; depends heavily on micro-location noise and internal courtyard/street orientation)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — attractive size/price for 1020 and strong walkability signals, but too many unknowns (floor, heating, energy, renovation scope) to pay “full ask” confidently.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort): €188k–€199k. Comps are unknown from the provided data, so treat this as a leverage-based range, not a market-validated one. Push for a discount tied to: missing energy/heating info, proof of renovation quality (receipts + warranties), and building financials. If the building has upcoming lift/roof/facade works, widen the ask.

Bottom line

This is a classic “small renovated period building unit” play: easy to love, easy to overpay. The deal is good if the building is financially healthy and the renovation is structural (not just cosmetic). Your worst-case is buying a pretty apartment inside a money-pit building with noisy exposure and surprise monthly cost increases.

Depends — likely livable day-one, but “old building + gas heating” usually means you should budget for upgrades/refresh unless the renovation is proven.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (64 sqm / 3 rooms can work for a small family, but it’s a squeeze long-term) Investor: ✔ (strong transit node + walkable amenities = stable renter demand) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Praterstern + Volkertmarkt area is practical, lively, and very city-useful)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — good location fundamentals and a very liquid unit type, but price/sqm and “period building unknowns” mean you want concessions unless condition is clearly top-tier.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €315k–€335k (best-effort guess; I don’t have comps or the full condition/energy data). Push hardest on: missing running costs disclosure, any upcoming building works, and gas-heating risk/modernization.

Bottom line

2nd district near a major hub is the kind of place that rents easily and keeps resale liquidity. At ~€5.4k/sqm you’re paying for location — make sure the building + apartment condition earns it. If the “bright WG period building” pitch is doing heavy lifting, verify noise, layout efficiency, and building maintenance before you fall in love.

Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)

  • Size: 49.42 m2

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — new-build / “exclusive units” often means turnkey finishes, but with empty description you must verify specs, handover status, and what’s actually included.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (49.4 sqm / 2 rooms can work for a couple + baby, but it’s tight long-term) Investor: ✔ (2nd district + high amenity/transit density is rental-friendly; liquidity usually solid) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, parks + restaurants nearby; good “city life” setup)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — attractive micro-location signals in 1020, but pricing looks punchy for 49 sqm and the listing data is missing key cost/building details.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €345k–€370k (comps unknown, so this is a pressure-test range based on size + typical 1020 pricing sensitivity). Anchor with missing facts: monthly running costs, reserve fund status, and exact building/finish spec. If they can’t document these cleanly, you have leverage.

Bottom line

If it’s truly a high-quality, quiet unit with good orientation and a healthy building fund, it can be a clean “buy-and-live” 1020 starter. This deal goes sideways if it’s noisy (street/courtyard mechanics), or if the building economics (repairs/reserves) are under-disclosed. Don’t pay “exclusive” money until the boring PDFs back it up.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ Investor: ➖ Lifestyle buyer: ➖

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location signal is excellent, but €399k for 51 sqm needs confirmation on building quality, floor level, noise, and operating costs.

Near WU can mean great liquidity, but also student churn and noise — verify orientation and windows. At this price point, you’re paying for the 2nd district premium; don’t overpay without hard facts on the building and the flat’s condition.

Move-in readiness

Depends — likely livable fast, but “old building + unit-level heating” can hide cost/comfort issues.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (31 sqm / 1 room is a hard ceiling) Investor: ➖ (works on paper, but building costs + heating type can wreck yield) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (great as a first Vienna base if the layout/light/noise check out)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — good location signal near the U3 with lots of daily-life amenities, but too many unknowns (floor, operating costs, energy, building condition) to pay “clean” pricing.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €190k–€205k (comps unknown; this is a risk-adjusted bid based on size + uncertainty). Anchor lower if the monthly costs/reserve are high, if the unit faces any rail/road noise, or if heating is old/expensive. Concrete lever: demand the building reserve status + recent/next major works (roof/facade/riser pipes) and re-price immediately if reserves are thin or works are scheduled.

Bottom line

2nd/3rd district fringe near the U3 can be a sweet “live like a local, commute like a tourist” pocket. This can be a clean little city pad — or a perpetual bills-and-maintenance story if the building is underfunded. Your make-or-break is: total monthly carrying cost + heating comfort.

Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €285,000
  • Size: 43.44 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — small 2-room period building with individual heating can be perfectly livable, but condition and running costs hinge on the actual state of the unit + heating system.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (size is the limiter) Investor: ➖ (strong renter demand near U3, but watch building condition + heating/operating-cost optics) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (quiet pocket + walkable amenities + fast U3 access is a very Vienna-friendly combo)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location signal is strong (U3 + dense daily-life amenities), but the unknowns (floor, monthly costs, energy/condition) are exactly where period building deals get won or lost.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €260k–€275k (best-effort guess; comps not provided). Anchor on missing disclosures (monthly costs, energy class), individual heating risk, and any building CapEx red flags (roof/façade/stairwell). Ask for: Betriebskosten+Ruecklage split, last 3 years WEG minutes, planned works, and proof/service history of the heating.

Bottom line

2 rooms / ~43 sqm near U3 in the 3rd is a classic “easy-to-live-in, easy-to-rent” format. Your upside is lifestyle convenience in a quieter setting; your downside risk is period building surprises + heating economics. If the building is well-managed and the unit is renovated sensibly, it’s compelling at the right price.

  • Price: €427,900
  • Size: 50 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: Deckenheizung
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — “first occupancy” + fitted kitchen suggests turnkey, but it’s currently time-limited rented, so your move-in timing is not fully in your control.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (2 rooms / ~50 sqm, high-floor tower living) Investor: ✔ (already rented; modern building + strong transit/amenities) Lifestyle buyer: ➖ (great view + new build vibe, but tenancy + tower/area trade-offs)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong product (new build tower, view, loggia, heating/cooling comfort potential) but you’re paying a premium per sqm and you inherit a tenant.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €395k–€420k (best-effort guess; I don’t have confirmed close-by sale comps here, so treat this as a negotiating anchor, not a valuation). Push hard on: (1) the fixed move-in constraint (limited lease), (2) any rent level vs market evidence, (3) tower-specific monthly running costs and any reserve fund status, and (4) façade/window performance (wind/noise) at height.

Bottom line

You’re buying “view + new-build tower” more than “classic 3rd district charm.” Micro-area looks convenience-heavy (transit/food/groceries all strong), but tourism/through-traffic can make it feel less neighborhood-cozy. If the rent/lease terms are merely average, you should not pay top-of-market retail for the unit today.

Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €540,000
  • Size: 47 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: underfloor heating
  • District: Vienna 3

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — “premium building” + underfloor heating suggests turn-key, but listing extract is missing key proof points (energy cert, floor level, condition details).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (47 sqm / 2 rooms is tight) Investor: ➖ (building amenities help, but 3rd district pricing + small unit means yield depends heavily on exact micro-location, HOA and rentability) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (balcony + pool + strong transit/walkable POIs reads like an easy daily-life setup)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — attractive “amenities + U3 + balcony” package, but the €/sqm is punchy and you’re paying for the building story; you need to verify the unit specifics (floor, light, noise, fees, garage terms).

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €495k–€525k (best-effort; comps not provided, so treat this as a negotiation anchor, not a valuation). Push hardest on (a) total monthly running costs beyond the stated €211.2 (reserve fund, lift/pool maintenance), (b) whether the Tiefgaragen Square is included vs optional/extra, (c) any short-term resale risk from high building operating costs.

Bottom line

You’re buying “premium building lifestyle” more than raw square meters. If the balcony is genuinely usable and the unit is quiet/bright, it can be a great single/couple home. If HOA/reserves are heavy (pool + garage often are), this can become an expensive-to-hold 47 sqm box.

#466
Attraktive Altbauwohnung in Toplage (Attraktive Altbauwohnung in Toplage)
€147,000 · 33.1 m2 · 1 rooms · Vienna, 15. District, Rudolfsheim-Fuenfhaus
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Address/map mismatch flagged (likely approximate pin).
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €147,000
  • Size: 33.1 m2
  • Rooms: 1
  • District: Vienna 15

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — “period building” can be gorgeous, but without condition/heating/energy info you’re buying the building’s quirks as much as the flat.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (33.1 sqm / 1 room) Investor: ✔ (small-ticket unit, strong rental demand likely if layout is efficient) Lifestyle buyer: ➖ (great if you want a compact city base; can feel tight fast)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the micro-location looks very convenient, but the listing data is missing the exact floor/condition/heating/energy story, so price risk is real.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €130,000–€142,000 (best-effort guess; true comps unknown). Anchor on missing disclosure (floor, state of renovation, heating type, energy class), and treat any upcoming building works (period building stairwell/roof/façade/risers) as a direct discount request. Ask for: recent operating-cost breakdown, reserve fund status, planned works, and whether monthly costs include heating/hot water.

Bottom line

2-minute living beats 2 extra square meters: the POI/transit density screams “easy daily life.” But at 33 sqm, layout quality is everything — one bad corridor/kitchen and it stops being rentable/pleasant. Buy only if the building is financially healthy and the unit has light + quiet (or you get paid for the risk).

#467
Renovierungsbeduerftige Erdgeschoss-apartment in Hofruhelage nahe U4 und U6! Grenze zum 6. und 12. district! (Renovierungsbedürftige Erdgeschoss-Wohnung in Hofruhelage nahe U4 und U6! Grenze zum 6. und 12. Bezirk!)
€165,000 · 50.99 m2 · 2 rooms · Sechshauser Strasse 34, Vienna, 15. District, Rudolfsheim-Fuenfhaus
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €165,000
  • Size: 50.99 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 15

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Needs work — the listing itself signals renovation, so assume you’re buying a project, not a turnkey home.

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms + ground-floor trade-offs can work, but only if courtyard-quiet is real and layout is efficient) Investor: ➖ (could pencil if renovation cost is tightly controlled; exit liquidity in 1150 is fine but ground floor can cap upside) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (if you want a central-ish, transit-rich base and don’t mind a reno)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong price-per-sqm on paper, but “renovation needed” + ground-floor risk means you should only buy with a margin of safety.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €135k–€155k (best-effort guess; comps not provided, so this is mainly a risk/reno/ground-floor discount). Primary levers: (1) scope uncertainty: ask for any photos/quotes or bring a contractor visit; price the unknowns, (2) building financials: push for reserve fund + planned works, (3) heating setup (individual heating) and running-cost uncertainty. One concrete lever tied to a verifiable uncertainty: request the building’s reserve status + any upcoming façade/roof/shaft work; if thin reserves or major works pending, that’s immediate, document-backed discount fuel.

Bottom line

If “courtyard quiet” is legit and the courtyard doesn’t act like an echo chamber, this can be a very livable, well-connected 2-room buy. But ground floor + renovation is where deals quietly go bad: moisture/noise/security and renovation overruns. Buy it only if you can lock renovation cost and you’re comfortable with a slightly harder resale than a mid-floor unit.

Move-in readiness

Move-in ready — reads like a fresh renovation/new build “first occupancy” unit with underfloor heating.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (1 room / ~33 sqm) Investor: ✔ (ticket size + strong rental demand logic if the building/legal setup is clean) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (compact city base, high walkability + transit density)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — good “city studio” fundamentals at a manageable price, but 15th district + micro-street noise risk means you want price discipline and full cost/energy docs.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €170k–€180k (best-effort guess; I don’t have comps/house condition details here). Push for a discount on (a) missing monthly running costs, (b) missing energy certificate, and (c) any unknown building condition items; make the offer conditional on document review and a quiet-hours visit.

Bottom line

This is a classic “smart small” buy: transit + daily-life amenities are strong, and underfloor heating/ lowers near-term hassle. The hidden downside is usually not the flat — it’s the building and the street (noise, common-area quality, and ongoing costs). Get those answers before paying a premium for “new.”

#469
Helle apartment mit Innenhofausrichtung im Dachgeschossausbau - Nahe U4 und U6 Laengenfeld Lane! (Helle Wohnung mit Innenhofausrichtung im Dachgeschossausbau - Nahe U4 und U6 Längenfeldgasse!)
€239,000 · 43.92 m2 · 2 rooms · Sechshauser Strasse 34, Vienna, 15. District, Rudolfsheim-Fuenfhaus
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €239,000
  • Size: 43.92 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • Heating: individual heating
  • District: Vienna 15

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — likely fine if the attic conversion is recent and the building systems are solid; biggest unknown is heat/noise/overheating management.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (too small) Investor: ➖ (strong transit + size, but running costs/heat source and tenant comfort need checking) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (bright, courtyard-facing, excellent connectivity)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — good “city-base” 2-room near major lines, but attic conversions can hide expensive surprises; price is plausible, not a steal.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €215k–€230k (best-effort guess; I don’t have recent building-specific comps). Push hardest on: proof of proper attic conversion approvals, roof condition/warranty, summer overheating mitigation, and clarity on what “floor” actually means (stairs/elevator, internal stairs). If any red flags (humidity, poor insulation, noisy courtyards, weak heating), widen ask down to ~€205k.

Bottom line

2 rooms on ~44 m² is the sweet spot for resale/liquidity. Courtyard orientation is the right instinct in this corridor — buy quiet, not street-facing “bright.” Your risk is not the location; it’s the building/attic execution quality and ongoing costs.

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — likely liveable, but “DG + individual heating” often hides comfort/maintenance questions (heat, noise, summer temps).

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms / ~67 sqm can work for a couple + baby, but it’s tight long-term) Investor: ➖ (good transit + amenity density, but heating system + unknown building/energy data can cap rent appeal) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (walkable, lots of food/shops, fast U4/U6 access)

Final verdict

🟡 NEGOTIATE — the location is convenient and the unit size is practical, but the missing hard facts (energy, floor/lift, DG specifics) + individual heating mean you should price in risk.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range (best-effort, comps unknown): €255k–€270k. Anchor on: individual heating operating costs/upgrade risk, missing energy class, DG heat risk, and any building-level CapEx (roof/façade/stairs). Keep the offer clean but conditional on document review.

Bottom line

Transit + everyday life convenience is the headline here (U4/U6 in ~5 minutes, lots of amenities). The “Schlafzimmer in Hofruhelage” pitch is plausible and valuable — verify with an evening visit. This is a solid buyer-home if the building is healthy; a mediocre deal if the house needs major works or the DG is a summer oven.

€273,300 · 39.86 m2 · 2 rooms · Engerthstrasse 111, Vienna, 20. District, Brigittenau
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €273,300
  • Size: 39.86 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • District: Vienna 20

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — likely a newer/renovated “developer-style” unit, but missing basics (floor, heating, energy class, running costs) means you can’t sanity-check comfort and total cost yet.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (39.86 sqm / 2 rooms = tight long-term) Investor: ➖ (good “easy-to-rent” size + riverside angle, but 20th district micro-location and unknown building/running costs can swing yield) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Donauufer + parks-heavy micro-area reads like outdoors + runs/bike + water views)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — the riverside pitch is strong, but the listing is currently light on the exact details that decide whether this is a smart buy or a shiny trap.

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €245k–€265k (best-effort guess; comps not provided, so treat as a starting anchor only). Focus asks: monthly operating costs (incl. reserve), heating type + meter setup, energy certificate, exact floor/orientation, noise exposure (Engerth Street can be traffic-heavy), and whether the “Donaublick” is permanent or depends on a specific angle/floor. If any of these are weak/expensive, push for a bigger discount or seller-paid upgrades (blinds, AC prep, kitchen package).

Bottom line

You’re paying for the Donau vibe: parks, water, and a clean “modern” story. At ~40 sqm, this is a precision purchase: it works if the building costs are low and the view/noise trade-off is acceptable. Don’t sign on aesthetics — sign on total monthly cost + livability (noise, heat, sun, ventilation).

Depends — likely fine if refreshed, but older-building units often hide wiring/plumbing and soundproofing surprises.

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (size is the limiter) Investor: ➖ (works if the building condition + fees check out) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (compact city base with outdoor space)

Final verdict

Bottom line

You’re paying for the balcony + “older-building charm” in a small footprint. If the building is well-maintained and fees are reasonable, it’s a strong live-in starter. If there’s deferred maintenance or high monthly costs, this quickly becomes overpriced for 44 m².

Buyer fit

Family: ➖ (2 rooms / ~63 sqm is workable for a couple + baby, tight for long-term) Investor: ✔ (U4 proximity + dense amenities = strong tenant demand, if building costs/reserves are sane) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Augarten + lots of food/drink + transit-heavy micro-area)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — location fundamentals look strong for the price bracket, but you’re currently buying blind on operating costs, heating, and energy performance.

Negotiation strategy

Bottom line

This is the kind of Vienna “good-enough period building” deal that wins on walkability and metro access. Two ways it goes wrong: (1) noisy orientation / street-facing windows make it unlivable day-to-day, (2) building financials (low reserves / upcoming facade-roof-stairwell works) turn a “fair price” into an expensive mistake. If the unit is quiet, costs are transparent, and the building is well-managed, it’s a solid buy in a very livable pocket.

Agent assessment — property (ad-anchored)

This note sticks to what the seller actually states in the ad text and what is present as structured fields. If the ad is thin, treat specifics as unknown until you verify them.

What the seller emphasizes:

  • No strong seller emphasis could be reliably extracted from the text.

What is explicitly stated:

  • Key building/usage details are not clearly specified in the ad text.

What is not stated (so assume unknown):

  • Exact floor level / storey
  • Elevator / lift
  • Outdoor space (balcony/loggia/terrace/garden)
  • Parking / garage availability and terms
  • Energy certificate figures (HWB / fGEE)
  • Tenancy status (vacant vs rented, end date)
  • Furnishing included/excluded

Caveats / how to read this ad:

  • If the pin is not confirmed, treat nearby-amenity context as approximate and verify the exact micro-location.

Fit (who this suits)

  • Best fit: buyers who are comfortable doing verification at the viewing (building condition, fees, exact floor/lift, what is included).
  • Less ideal if: you need full upfront disclosure in the listing text before investing time.

Micro-area (local guide)

As stated in the ad, no specific nearby landmarks or transit lines are named. For this apartment, that matters because you should rely on on-the-ground checks (evening noise, walk times) and the report’s generic OSM proxy signals rather than assumed venue proximity.

Macro-area (local guide)

General background (not source-verified): Vienna 20 can vary block-by-block. The best version of central Vienna living is quiet-at-home but walkable-to-everything; the trade-offs are tourism gradient, event-driven crowding, and late-night noise. Treat courtyard vs street exposure and window quality as the real differentiators.

Orientation

Centrality proxy is not available for this listing. Key orientation/access details are not specified (floor, lift).

#474
PROVISIONSFREI - Modernes wohnen am Donauufer mit Donaublick! (PROVISIONSFREI - Modernes wohnen am Donauufer mit Donaublick!)
€408,500 · 45.12 m2 · 2 rooms · Engerthstrasse 111, Vienna, 20. District, Brigittenau
Terrace/OutdoorGarage/ParkingQuiet evenings (proxy)
Ad (EN) | Ad · Maps · Street View
  • Price: €408,500
  • Size: 45.12 m2
  • Rooms: 2
  • District: Vienna 20

Expert panel (interpretive; not a fact record)

Move-in readiness

Depends — “modern living” suggests turnkey, but you’re missing hard facts (building year/condition, energy/heating, monthly costs, floor/lift, orientation, noise).

Buyer fit

Family: ❌ (45 m² / 2 rooms is tight long-term) Investor: ➖ (waterfront appeal helps, but price-per-m² and unknown running costs can squeeze yield) Lifestyle buyer: ✔ (Danube frontage + parks + low tourist pressure is a real quality-of-life combo)

Final verdict

Action: 🟡 NEGOTIATE — strong location signal (river/parks) but the pricing and missing operating/technical details mean you should treat this as “pay for the view only if the numbers check out.”

Negotiation strategy

Target price range: €375k–€399k (best-effort guess; comps not provided, so this is a negotiation anchor, not a valuation). Push hardest on: (1) noise exposure (Engerth Street/arterial + river-side traffic), (2) floor/orientation and any rail/road proximity, (3) total monthly carrying costs + reserve fund, (4) energy/heating details, (5) rental restrictions and short-term letting rules, (6) any “provisionsfrei” developer pricing that leaves less room to bargain.

Bottom line

If the view is real and the apartment is quiet enough inside, this is a high-livability 2-room. But at ~€408.5k for 45.12 m² you’re paying a premium — make them prove it with low running costs, good energy performance, and a genuinely calm interior. Treat it as a lifestyle purchase first, investment second.
